summary: Intense parallel codebase audit of 10 dimensions of how to improve the Alex agent, each finding adversarially verified against live code, then a completeness critique
logs: Auditing 10 dimensions of Alex; each high/medium finding will be adversarially verified.
Audit + verify complete: 53 surviving findings, 1 dropped (refuted/already-done). Running completeness critic.

dimensions.title: Conversation quality & prompt engineering
dimensions.laneSummary: Alex's prompt content (Voice, claim-boundaries, objection-handling, qualification-framework) is genuinely strong and the seeded skill pack is live, but the parameter-assembly layer that feeds operator config into the prompt is brittle in ways the eval harness structurally cannot catch. The single biggest opportunity is the persona-criteria shape mismatch: the live resolvePersona() drops object-shaped qualification/disqualification/escalation criteria to undefined, which then either crashes prompt interpolation or degrades it — and the 72-scenario baseline + golden suite both bypass resolvePersona, so they stay green while prod is affected. Two adjacent, high-leverage gaps: the model is told to compute booking dates from 'today' with no current-date anchor injected (fabricated slots window -> false 'availability trouble' escalation at the book step), and the four conversation-pattern playbooks that would provide archetype few-shot grounding are dead TODO placeholders never loaded.
dimensions.notesOnShippedQuality: Two concrete defects inside shipped code in this lane. (1) The shipped seed-marketplace Alex deployment stores qualificationCriteria/disqualificationCriteria/escalationRules as objects (packages/db/prisma/seed-marketplace.ts:644-656), but the shipped resolvePersona contract requires string arrays and omits non-arrays (packages/schemas/src/agent-persona-config.ts:47-55, test :81-90); the shipped alex builder then sets undefined-valued keys on PERSONA_CONFIG (builders/alex.ts:131-138) and the shipped template engine throws on a present-object/missing-field (template-engine.ts:47-49). This is a live-path break that the shipped eval (run-conversation.ts:174 passes persona straight to the builder, skipping resolvePersona) cannot detect — a test-coverage blind spot in already-shipped infra. (2) The shipped conversation-pattern and market reference files (skills/alex/references/conversation-patterns/*.md, references/markets/*.md) are committed as 'Phase 1a placeholder' stubs with no seeder or loader wiring — dead content that implies grounding which does not exist. Separately confirmed clean: PR #794 (commit 80adc413) correctly removes BOTH false-availability claims ('hold a slot' and 'keep a consultation slot tentatively') from objection-handling.md, so that compliance gap is properly handled in-flight and was not re-proposed.

findings.title: Live persona qualification/disqualification/escalation criteria are silently dropped (or crash interpolation) — eval can't see it because it bypasses resolvePersona
findings.currentState: SKILL.md interpolates the operator persona's structured criteria directly: {{PERSONA_CONFIG.qualificationCriteria}} (skills/alex/SKILL.md:174), {{PERSONA_CONFIG.disqualificationCriteria}} (:178), {{PERSONA_CONFIG.escalationRules}} (:248). The live path resolves persona via resolvePersona() (packages/core/src/platform/prisma-deployment-resolver.ts:133), which only keeps these three fields when they are JS arrays and OMITS them (-> undefined) otherwise — proven by packages/schemas/src/agent-persona-config.ts:47-55 and its test 'omits non-array criteria fields' (packages/schemas/src/__tests__/agent-persona-config.test.ts:81-90). But the actual seeded Alex deployment supplies them as OBJECTS: qualificationCriteria:{ageRequirement:'21+',noContraindications:true}, disqualificationCriteria:{underage:true,...}, escalationRules:{medicalQuestions:true,...} (packages/db/prisma/seed-marketplace.ts:644-656). So at runtime ctx.persona.qualificationCriteria === undefined; the alex builder still sets the key on PERSONA_CONFIG (packages/core/src/skill-runtime/builders/alex.ts:131-138), so the template engine hits its 'present object, missing field' branch and THROWS SkillParameterError('Missing field "qualificationCriteria"') (packages/core/src/skill-runtime/template-engine.ts:47-49; asserted by template-engine.test.ts:54-57). interpolate() is called outside any try/catch (packages/core/src/skill-runtime/skill-executor.ts:171), so the throw bubbles to SkillMode and becomes an EXECUTION_ERROR failed result (skill-mode.ts:107-111) — Alex never produces a turn. Crucially, BOTH the alex builder unit test (builders/alex.test.ts:13-15) and the alex-conversation eval (evals/alex-conversation/run-conversation.ts:108-118) hand-build object criteria and pass the persona DIRECTLY to alexBuilder (run-conversation.ts:174-176), bypassing resolvePersona entirely — so the array-only coercion that nulls the fields in prod is invisible to the 72-scenario baseline and the golden regression suite.
findings.opportunity: The operator's configured qualification gates, disqualifiers, and escalation rules are the deployment-level controls that personalize Alex per clinic. Today they either crash every live turn (if interpolation throws on the seeded object shape) or — if a clinic happens to store arrays — render as a flat, semantically-degraded YAML list. The eval harness's divergence from the production resolver at exactly this seam means the regression suite gives false confidence: it will stay green while prod is broken.
findings.proposedChange: Three coordinated fixes (do the first two at minimum): (1) Make the template engine treat a key that is present-but-undefined the same as an absent optional param — return '' instead of throwing in template-engine.ts:47-49 when decl is optional — so a dropped persona field degrades to a blank section instead of failing the whole conversation. (2) Make resolvePersona tolerate object/record criteria (render to newline 'key: value' lines, or keep the raw object) instead of array-only, so operator-configured objects survive to the prompt; align packages/db/prisma/seed-marketplace.ts to the surviving shape. (3) Route the eval persona through resolvePersona() in evals/alex-conversation/run-conversation.ts so the harness exercises the SAME parameter-resolution path as prod and would catch this class of bug. Since QUALIFICATION_CONTEXT/CLAIM_BOUNDARIES already carry the rich framework, consider whether {{PERSONA_CONFIG.qualificationCriteria}} is even needed in SKILL.md or should be demoted to a thin 'operator overrides' block.
findings.impactRationale: Directly governs whether Alex runs at all on the seeded medspa deployment and whether operator-set qualification/escalation rules reach the model — booking conversion AND safety/compliance (escalationRules includes medicalQuestions). A green eval that diverges from prod at this seam is a reliability landmine.
findings.risk: Loosening the template-engine throw could mask a genuinely-missing REQUIRED token elsewhere — scope the change to optional decls only (required tokens already pre-validated at template-engine.ts:73-77). Changing resolvePersona affects sales-pipeline/website-profiler personas too; keep array handling and ADD object handling rather than replacing.

findings.title: Alex is told to compute booking dates from 'today' but the current date is never injected — the model fabricates the slots-query window
findings.currentState: Phase 4 instructs the model to call calendar-book.slots.query with dateFrom='today's date (ISO 8601)' and dateTo='3 business days from today' (skills/alex/SKILL.md:201-202). The slots.query tool REQUIRES dateFrom/dateTo from the LLM and does not default them server-side (packages/core/src/skill-runtime/tools/calendar-book.ts:132), passing them straight to provider.listAvailableSlots(query) (calendar-book.ts:134-139). No current date/time is injected anywhere into Alex's system prompt: the alex builder sets no date token (packages/core/src/skill-runtime/builders/alex.ts), the executor only appends governance constraints (skill-executor.ts:173), and renderBusinessFacts emits timezone but not 'today' (context-resolver.ts:16-74). A grep for date injection into the prompt turns up nothing. The LLM therefore has no reliable anchor for the current date and will guess (commonly anchored near its training cutoff).
findings.opportunity: A fabricated dateFrom/dateTo means slots.query runs against the wrong window (or a past window). If the provider only returns future availability, it returns empty -> Alex hits the 'I'm having trouble checking availability right now, let me have someone reach out' fallback (SKILL.md:228-230) at the exact moment of highest buying intent, converting a ready-to-book lead into a manual-handoff stall. If it returns slots for a wrong/far window, Alex offers dates that don't make sense. Either way the booking funnel leaks precisely at Phase 4.
findings.proposedChange: Inject the current date/time in the business timezone into the prompt and reference it explicitly. Cheapest: have the alex builder add a CURRENT_DATETIME parameter (e.g. now formatted in the business timezone) and a {{CURRENT_DATETIME}} line near Phase 4; declare it in SKILL.md frontmatter. Defense-in-depth: default dateFrom (today) / dateTo (today+3 business days) server-side in calendar-book.slots.query when the LLM omits or supplies a clearly-stale value, so a hallucinated date can't silently break availability. Make the same date available to booking.create confirmation copy.
findings.impactRationale: Booking conversion + live reliability: this is the literal book step. A wrong date range produces empty availability and a false 'system trouble' escalation, killing conversions that were one tap from booked.
findings.risk: Timezone correctness matters (SG vs MY) — derive from business facts timezone, not server local. Server-side date defaulting must not override a legitimately lead-requested future window; only fill when absent or implausible.

findings.title: The four conversation-pattern playbooks (problem-led, price-shop, branded-request, aftercare) and both market voice files are dead TODO placeholders never loaded into the prompt
findings.currentState: skills/alex/references/conversation-patterns/{problem-led,price-shop,branded-request,aftercare}.md are all stub files whose body is literally '> **TODO — Phase 1a placeholder.**' with a 4-bullet skeleton (e.g. price-shop.md:13, problem-led.md:13, branded-request.md:13, aftercare.md:13). The market voice files skills/alex/references/markets/{sg-medspa,my-medspa}.md are likewise '> **TODO — Phase 1a placeholder.**' (sg-medspa.md:14, my-medspa.md:14). None of these are seeded or loaded: the live skill-pack seeder only ingests objection-handling/qualification-framework/claim-boundaries (packages/db/src/seed/seed-alex-skill-pack.ts:31-50), no seeder or runtime references the conversation-patterns directory (grep for 'conversation-patterns'/'problem-led'/'price-shop' in packages/db returns nothing), and 'markets/' appears only inside a doc-comment example string (packages/core/src/skill-runtime/types.ts:37), never as a load path.
findings.opportunity: These four archetypes (branded request after Lemon8/RedNote research, raw price-shop, problem-led 'fix my acne scars', aftercare) ARE the dominant inbound shapes for SG/MY medspa, and they are exactly where few-shot grounding would lift conversion: how to quote a range tied to consultation, how to anchor a branded-treatment ask to a consult, how to acknowledge a concern without diagnosing. Today the prompt gives only generic phase guidance with zero archetype-specific exemplars or opening-message few-shots — leaving conversion on the table on the most common turns. The empty files also mislead future maintainers into thinking grounding exists.
findings.proposedChange: Author the four conversation-pattern files into real, compact playbooks (orientation + 1-2 worked WhatsApp exemplars each that obey claim-boundaries), then either (a) seed them as additional KnowledgeEntry scopes (e.g. kind=playbook scope=conversation-patterns) wired into SKILL.md as a CONVERSATION_PATTERNS context slot, or (b) fold the highest-value exemplars directly into SKILL.md's Phase 1/Phase 3 as few-shot grounding. Pick ONE injection mechanism and delete or clearly mark the unused files. Same for the two market voice files (or remove them if zh/ms is genuinely deferred per backlog).
findings.impactRationale: Booking conversion: archetype-specific few-shot grounding on the most frequent inbound shapes is the highest-leverage prompt lever available, and it currently does not exist despite the directory implying it does.
findings.risk: New seeded content must pass claim-boundaries review (these are riskLevel medium/high files); exemplars must not introduce guarantee/availability claims. Adding a context scope requires a seeder + SKILL.md token + the provisioning preflight to know about it.

findings.title: WhatsApp intent tag can be silently dropped because the sidecar parser slices everything after the first sidecar tag, and SKILL.md gives both blocks conflicting 'at the very end' instructions
findings.currentState: SKILL.md tells the model two things must come last on WhatsApp: 'end every response with exactly one intent tag on its own line' (skills/alex/SKILL.md:100) and 'At the very end of every response, after a blank line, emit exactly one trailing block' for <qualification_signals> (SKILL.md:310). At runtime the executor parses the sidecar FIRST and sets visibleResponse = everything before the first <qualification_signals> tag (qualification-sidecar-parser.ts:54; called at skill-executor.ts:268-269), THEN runs parseIntentTag on that already-sliced visibleResponse (skill-executor.ts:296). So if the model places the <intent> tag AFTER the sidecar block (a natural reading of 'at the very end'), the intent tag lives in the discarded tail and is never seen by parseIntentTag -> intentClass=null.
findings.opportunity: A dropped intentClass means the WhatsApp template registry can't route the message to the right approved template (whatsapp-registry maps intentClass -> template) and re-engagement attribution (which keys on details.intentClass='re-engagement-offer') misses. The ordering is left to model whim by two mutually-contradictory 'very end' instructions; failures are silent (no error, just a null class).
findings.proposedChange: Make it deterministic at both ends. In the prompt, state an explicit single ordering: the <qualification_signals> block is the ABSOLUTE last thing in the response, and the <intent> tag must appear immediately BEFORE it (or, simpler, fold the intent into the sidecar contract). In code, run parseIntentTag on rawResponseText BEFORE the sidecar slice (skill-executor.ts), or have the sidecar parser strip the sidecar from the FULL string (it already strips in the multi-block path) and return the remainder so a trailing intent tag survives — then position no longer matters.
findings.impactRationale: Live reliability + conversion: silently losing the WhatsApp intent class breaks compliant-template routing and re-engagement attribution on an unknown fraction of turns, with no error surfaced.
findings.risk: Reordering the parse must preserve the existing 'strip-all-on-ambiguity' safety so protocol text never leaks to the contact; cover the intent-after-sidecar case with a test.

verdicts.notes:
Every cited file:line checks out.

1. SKILL.md:174,178,248 — {{PERSONA_CONFIG.qualificationCriteria}}, {{PERSONA_CONFIG.disqualificationCriteria}}, {{PERSONA_CONFIG.escalationRules}} are all present as dot-access template tokens.

2. agent-persona-config.ts:47-55 — resolvePersona() uses Array.isArray() guard; if the inputConfig field is a plain object (not array), the result field is undefined. Exactly as claimed.

3. agent-persona-config.test.ts:81-90 — test "omits non-array criteria fields" exists and passes a string, expects undefined. Seed uses an object — same fate: resolvePersona returns undefined for all three fields.

4. seed-marketplace.ts:644-656 — qualificationCriteria, disqualificationCriteria, escalationRules are ALL plain JS objects (not arrays) in the seeded Alex deployment.

5. prisma-deployment-resolver.ts:133 — toResult() calls resolvePersona(inputConfig), so live prod path nulls all three criteria fields.

6. alex.ts:131-138 — alexBuilder writes PERSONA_CONFIG.qualificationCriteria = ctx.persona.qualificationCriteria. With resolvePersona having returned undefined, the value on the object is undefined.

7. template-engine.ts:47-49 — resolveValue() for a dot-access (field present): it fetches nested = (value as Record)[field]; if nested === undefined → throws SkillParameterError("Missing field ..."). No optional-field escape hatch here.

8. skill-executor.ts:171 — interpolate() is called with no surrounding try/catch at that line; the throw propagates out.

9. skill-mode.ts:107-111 — outer catch converts any thrown error to failedResult with code EXECUTION_ERROR. Alex never produces a response.

10. builders/alex.test.ts:13-15 — test passes qualificationCriteria as an object {budget: "above 200 SGD"} directly on ctx.persona, so alexBuilder puts a non-undefined object on PERSONA_CONFIG.qualificationCriteria. The template engine then renders it as YAML (template-engine.ts:51-52) — no throw. Test does NOT catch the prod undefined path.

11. evals/alex-conversation/run-conversation.ts:108-118, 174 — buildPersona() returns object-typed criteria; ctx is built as {persona} and passed directly to alexBuilder, bypassing resolvePersona. The eval's 72-scenario baseline and golden suite never exercise resolvePersona's array-only coercion and thus cannot catch this failure mode.

SKILL.md:31-39 declares PERSONA_CONFIG with qualificationCriteria as "type: object, required: true" in its sub-schema — but the template engine's resolveValue() does not inspect decl.schema at all for nested field optionality, so that required:true annotation in the sub-schema is inert; the throw at line 49 is unconditional when nested===undefined.

Impact high is credible: with the seeded deployment's object-shaped criteria, every live Alex turn hits EXECUTION_ERROR before the LLM is called. Effort M is credible for the three-part fix (template-engine optional-field grace, resolvePersona object-toleration, eval persona routing through resolvePersona). No evidence this is already shipped or in-flight per the known context.

All cited evidence holds exactly as claimed.

skills/alex/SKILL.md:201-202 — Phase 4 instructs LLM to call `calendar-book.slots.query` with `dateFrom: today's date (ISO 8601)` and `dateTo: 3 business days from today` — pure natural language, no interpolated value.

calendar-book.ts:132 — `dateFrom` and `dateTo` are both in the `required` array; no server-side defaults exist anywhere in the tool.

calendar-book.ts:134-139 — `execute` casts `params as SlotQuery` and passes it directly to `provider.listAvailableSlots(query)` — zero sanitization or defaulting of date fields.

builders/alex.ts:125-141 — The `parameters` object contains exactly: BUSINESS_NAME, OPPORTUNITY_ID, LEAD_PROFILE, BUSINESS_FACTS, OUTCOME_PATTERNS, PERSONA_CONFIG, and (non-prompt) contactId. No CURRENT_DATE or CURRENT_DATETIME parameter exists.

context-resolver.ts:16-74 — `renderBusinessFacts` emits `Timezone: ${facts.timezone}` (line 19) but nothing resembling a current date or time.

skill-executor.ts:173 — System prompt assembled as `${interpolated}\n\n${getGovernanceConstraints()}` — no date suffix added.

Exhaustive grep across all of packages/core/src/skill-runtime/ and skills/alex/ for CURRENT_DATE, CURRENT_DATETIME, inject.*date, today.*prompt, etc. returned zero hits. The finding is real and novel — not present in the shipped or in-flight list. The model genuinely has no anchor for the current date and must hallucinate it when constructing slot queries. Impact is high (every booking attempt in Phase 4 is affected); effort is S (add a CURRENT_DATETIME parameter in alex builder and a {{CURRENT_DATETIME}} token in SKILL.md Phase 4).

All four conversation-pattern files and both market-voice files are confirmed TODO stubs. price-shop.md:13, problem-led.md:13, branded-request.md:13, aftercare.md:13 all contain "> **TODO — Phase 1a placeholder.**"; sg-medspa.md:13 and my-medspa.md:13 are identical placeholders (the finding cites line 14 but the TODO lands at line 13 in both — minor off-by-one, not material).

The "never loaded into the prompt" claim needs precision: skill-loader.ts:133-188 DOES walk the entire references/ tree (including conversation-patterns/ and markets/) and populates SkillDefinition.references. But skill-executor.ts only accesses params.skill.body, .tools, .parameters, .context, and .minimumModelTier — never .references. The field is optional on SkillDefinition (types.ts:57) and confirmed dead-end in execution. Result: files are parsed into a struct that is never read by the executor.

The seeder claim holds exactly: packages/db/src/seed/seed-alex-skill-pack.ts:31-50 lists only three ALEX_SKILL_PACK_SCOPES (objection-handling playbook, qualification-framework playbook, claim-boundaries policy). No conversation-patterns or markets scope is seeded. SKILL.md:55-80 shows only five context slots wired (objection-handling, messaging-rules, business-facts, qualification-framework, claim-boundaries) — no slot for conversation-patterns or markets.

types.ts:37 is a doc-comment string example in an interface declaration, not a load path — that part of the evidence is accurate.

This is novel: not in SHIPPED list, not in IN-FLIGHT PRs (#794/#791/#782). Impact medium (Alex has no per-pattern playbook guidance), effort M (authoring + one injection mechanism) is credible.

All four cited evidence points verified exactly as claimed.

SKILL.md:100 — "end every response with exactly one intent tag on its own line" (WhatsApp section, §98–114).

SKILL.md:310 — "At the very end of every response, after a blank line, emit exactly one trailing block" for <qualification_signals> (§308–324). Both sections independently claim their block is "at the very end" — a genuine conflict.

qualification-sidecar-parser.ts:54 — `const visibleResponse = raw.slice(0, openIdx).trim();` — visibleResponse is everything BEFORE the first <qualification_signals> open tag. Any content after that position (including a trailing <intent> tag) is discarded.

skill-executor.ts:268-269 — sidecar parsed first: `const sidecar = parseQualificationSidecar(rawResponseText); const visibleResponse = sidecar.visibleResponse;`

skill-executor.ts:296 — `const { text: responseText, intentClass } = parseIntentTag(visibleResponse);` — runs on already-sliced text, not rawResponseText.

The silent-drop failure mode is real: if the model follows §310 literally and places <qualification_signals> before the <intent> tag (because §310 says the sidecar is "at the very end"), the intent tag is in the discarded tail → intentClass=null. Conversely, if the model follows §100 and places <intent> last, the sidecar must come before it — but §310 says the sidecar is "at the very end." The prompt gives the model two contradictory "last thing" instructions with no explicit ordering between them. Not in any shipped/in-flight item listed in context. Impact/effort assessment (medium/S) is credible — the fix is prompt clarification plus a one-line change to run parseIntentTag on rawResponseText before the slice.

dimensions.title: Booking & conversion mechanics (the money path)
dimensions.laneSummary: The money path has the right shape (provider abstraction, transactional outbox, failure handler, dedupe guard) but several concrete leaks between a successful booking and the system recording it. The single biggest opportunity is a silent slot-availability collapse: slots.query never applies the bufferMinutes default, so (duration + undefined)=NaN starves the slot grid to roughly one option per day on every booking attempt, on both providers. Two further gaps undercut conversion accounting: a successful booking.create never advances the opportunity to \"booked\" (it relies on an LLM stage.update the prompt never asks for), and the deterministic \"booked\" lifecycle producer is a no-op. The production Google path also lacks the double-booking guard that only the Local path has.
dimensions.notesOnShippedQuality: Concrete defects found inside already-shipped lane code: (1) packages/core/src/skill-runtime/tools/calendar-book.ts:135 casts slots.query input as SlotQuery without SlotQuerySchema.parse(), so the schema's bufferMinutes default(15) never applies and packages/core/src/calendar/slot-generator.ts:55 computes a NaN slot increment that collapses availability to ~1 slot/day. (2) The booking idempotency unique constraint (schema.prisma:1924, migration 20260418200000) is a plain unique index that includes failed rows, so a failed-then-retried same-slot booking is permanently blocked by P2002. (3) GoogleCalendarAdapter.createBooking (google-calendar-adapter.ts:74-84) inserts with no freebusy/conflict re-check and PrismaBookingStore.create (prisma-booking-store.ts:23-42) has no overlap guard — the SLOT_CONFLICT protection in calendar-provider-factory.ts:176-188 only covers the Local provider. (4) booking.create's success path never advances opportunity stage, and both registerBookingCreateHook callers (app.ts:705, inngest.ts:655) are no-ops, so neither the opportunity stage nor the lifecycle snapshot reaches \"booked\" deterministically. Note also that the entire conversation-lifecycle runs with readMode default 'off' (app.ts:710) and is only enabled per-org via the lifecycleTagging.mechanical flag — so findings #1 and #5 manifest as funnel/attribution undercount today and as re-engagement-of-booked-leads once that flag is flipped.

findings.title: Successful booking.create does not advance the opportunity to "booked" — funnel/attribution depend on the LLM remembering an instruction the prompt never gives
findings.currentState: booking.create persists a Booking row, calls the calendar provider, emits a "booked" outbox event, and returns — but it never touches the opportunity pipeline stage (packages/core/src/skill-runtime/tools/calendar-book.ts:142-326 contains zero updateStage call). Stage advancement is entirely LLM-driven via crm-write.stage.update (packages/core/src/skill-runtime/tools/crm-write.ts:38-72). SKILL.md Phase 4 (Book), skills/alex/SKILL.md:197-237, instructs the model to query slots, call booking.create, and send a natural confirmation, but NEVER instructs it to call crm-write.stage.update to "booked". The only stage.update instruction in the whole prompt is for "qualified" (skills/alex/SKILL.md:182). The deterministic lifecycle equivalent (onBookingCreated, packages/core/src/conversation-lifecycle/event-hooks/booking-created-hook.ts:25 toState:"booked") has no producer (see separate finding). By contrast, the "qualified" transition IS produced deterministically from the qualification sidecar (QualificationEvaluationHook wired into the executor at apps/api/src/bootstrap/skill-mode.ts:554).
findings.opportunity: Every successful Alex booking that the model forgets to follow with a stage.update leaves the opportunity stuck at "qualified" (or "interested"). The pipeline, the dashboard funnel, attribution reconciliation (packages/core/src/attribution/reconciliation-runner.ts:46 counts countByStage(orgId,"booked")), and re-engagement targeting all read opportunity stage — so booked-but-not-staged leads are invisible as conversions and can be re-targeted as if still open. A core money-path state transition should not hinge on the model spontaneously issuing a second tool call the prompt never asks for.
findings.proposedChange: Make booking success deterministically advance the stage. Cheapest correct fix: inside booking.create's success branch (calendar-book.ts, right after the confirm+outbox transaction at ~line 299), call opportunityStore.updateStage(orgId, opportunityId, "booked") in the same flow (opportunityId is already resolved at lines 183-194). This is server-side and LLM-independent. Keep it idempotent (updateStage already filters by org and is a no-op-safe updateMany). Secondarily, add an explicit Phase 4 step to SKILL.md telling Alex to call crm-write.stage.update to "booked" after confirming, as defense-in-depth — but the code path is the load-bearing fix.
findings.impactRationale: Directly protects booking-conversion accounting and re-engagement safety: it stops real bookings from being under-counted in the funnel and stops already-booked leads from being chased as open opportunities. Touches the single most important state transition in the money path.
findings.risk: updateStage throws StaleVersionError when count===0 (e.g. opportunity deleted mid-flow); wrap in a try/catch that logs but does not fail the booking (the booking already succeeded — a stage-write failure must not surface as a booking failure to the customer).

findings.title: slots.query passes bufferMinutes=undefined into slot math → (duration + undefined)=NaN collapses availability to ~one slot per day
findings.currentState: SlotQuerySchema defines bufferMinutes with .default(15) (packages/schemas/src/calendar.ts:9), but the calendar-book tool casts raw LLM input with `const query = params as SlotQuery` and never .parse()s it (packages/core/src/skill-runtime/tools/calendar-book.ts:135). Zod defaults only apply on parse. The tool's hand-written inputSchema for slots.query (calendar-book.ts:123-133) does NOT list bufferMinutes, so the model is never told to send it, and the runtime validator (packages/core/src/skill-runtime/input-schema-validator.ts:83-113) only checks types/required — it does not inject defaults. So bufferMinutes arrives undefined. Both providers forward it straight into generateAvailableSlots (local-calendar-provider.ts:80, google-calendar-adapter.ts:68). In the slot loop, packages/core/src/calendar/slot-generator.ts:55 computes `slotCursor.setTime(slotCursor.getTime() + (durationMinutes + bufferMinutes) * 60_000)` = (30 + undefined) = NaN → setTime(NaN) makes the cursor an Invalid Date → the while-condition at slot-generator.ts:40-42 becomes false → the inner loop exits after pushing only the first slot of each day.
findings.opportunity: Alex offers at most ~1 slot per day (always the opening time), over a 3-business-day window that's ~3 slots total, all at clinic-open. SKILL.md Phase 4 asks the model to present 3-5 options (skills/alex/SKILL.md:207-212); it physically cannot when the grid is starved. A lead who can't do 10am Monday but could do 2:30pm is pushed to escalation or drop-off. This silently throttles the booking funnel on every single booking attempt, on both Local and Google providers.
findings.proposedChange: Parse the input with SlotQuerySchema at the tool boundary so the default applies: replace `const query = params as SlotQuery` (calendar-book.ts:135) with `const query = SlotQuerySchema.parse(params)` (or apply `?? 15` when forwarding bufferMinutes). Defensively, also guard slot-generator.ts:55 against a non-finite increment (e.g. `const step = durationMinutes + (Number.isFinite(bufferMinutes) ? bufferMinutes : 0)` with step>0 assertion) so a missing buffer can never again zero out availability. Add a test asserting a full business day yields the expected multi-slot count.
findings.impactRationale: Restores the entire offered-slot inventory on every booking. This is the highest-throughput conversion lever in the lane: more real choices = more bookings, fewer escalations, and it fixes a defect that currently degrades every booking conversation.
findings.risk: Very low — parsing adds the documented default; the only behavioral change is more slots offered. Confirm no caller relied on the (broken) one-slot-per-day output.

findings.title: A failed booking leaves a row that trips the unique constraint, so the lead can never re-book the same slot they actually want
findings.currentState: booking.create persists the Booking row BEFORE calling the provider (packages/core/src/skill-runtime/tools/calendar-book.ts:198-208). On provider error or confirmation-transaction error, BookingFailureHandler sets status:"failed" but keeps the row (packages/core/src/skill-runtime/tools/booking-failure-handler.ts:84-87). The dedupe guard is a PLAIN unique constraint `@@unique([organizationId, contactId, service, startsAt])` (packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma:1924; migration 20260418200000_add_booking_idempotency_constraint/migration.sql) with no partial WHERE excluding failed rows. So if the same contact picks the same slot again after a transient failure, bookingStore.create throws P2002 and the tool returns DUPLICATE_BOOKING (calendar-book.ts:209-228), even though no live booking exists. SKILL.md then tells Alex 'I wasn't able to lock in that slot' (skills/alex/SKILL.md:233-235).
findings.opportunity: The most natural recovery after a transient booking glitch is the lead saying 'try that 2:30 again' — and Alex deterministically refuses, because the failed attempt poisoned that (contact, service, slot) tuple forever. A ready-to-book, fully-qualified lead hits a permanent dead-end at the final step. This converts recoverable infrastructure blips into lost bookings.
findings.proposedChange: Exclude failed (and cancelled) bookings from the idempotency guard. Replace the plain unique constraint with a partial unique index, e.g. `CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ... ON "Booking"(organizationId, contactId, service, startsAt) WHERE status NOT IN ('failed','cancelled')` (Postgres supports partial unique indexes; hand-write the migration). Then a retry of a previously-failed slot inserts cleanly. Alternatively, in the P2002 branch, look up the colliding row and if its status is 'failed'/'cancelled', delete/reactivate it and retry once. Add a regression test: fail a booking, retry same slot, expect success.
findings.impactRationale: Removes a permanent conversion dead-end at the final, highest-intent step of the funnel and improves reliability of recovery from provider/transaction blips. Cheap, high-leverage.
findings.risk: Partial-index name must match Prisma's expectation or drift checks fail (known gotcha). The current full constraint must be dropped and replaced in the same migration; verify no other code relies on failed rows blocking re-insert.

findings.title: Google Calendar booking path has no conflict check — two parallel leads can both confirm the same physical slot
findings.currentState: For the Local provider, the overbooking guard lives in the booking transaction: buildLocalStore.createInTransaction re-queries overlapping non-cancelled/failed bookings and throws SLOT_CONFLICT (apps/api/src/bootstrap/calendar-provider-factory.ts:176-188). But the production Google path does NOT use that store. In booking.create, the durable row is written via PrismaBookingStore.create (packages/db/src/stores/prisma-booking-store.ts:23-42), which performs NO overlap check, and the calendar side is GoogleCalendarAdapter.createBooking (packages/core/src/calendar/google-calendar-adapter.ts:74-84) which calls events.insert blindly with no freebusy re-check. The only dedupe is the per-contact unique constraint (schema.prisma:1924), which does not constrain two DIFFERENT contacts on the same time.
findings.opportunity: slots.query is a read with no hold; between two concurrent conversations both leads can be shown the same 2:30pm slot and both call booking.create. On the Google path nothing stops both from getting a confirmed Booking row + a real Google event for the same chair/room/practitioner. The clinic gets a double-booked slot — a trust/operational failure that surfaces only when both customers show up. This is the production path whenever GOOGLE_CALENDAR_CREDENTIALS is configured (calendar-provider-factory.ts:76-94).
findings.proposedChange: Add an overlap check on the durable-write side that applies regardless of provider. Simplest: give PrismaBookingStore.create the same overlapping-booking guard used by the Local store (query non-cancelled/failed bookings where startsAt<endsAt && endsAt>startsAt inside the create transaction; throw a typed SLOT_CONFLICT). Map SLOT_CONFLICT in booking.create to a model-facing 'that time was just taken — let me offer the next options' result so Alex re-queries rather than dead-ending. Optionally also re-check Google freebusy before events.insert as defense-in-depth. Cover with a concurrent-two-contact test.
findings.impactRationale: Prevents real-world double-bookings (safety/operational trust) on the live Google path and converts a silent overbook into a graceful re-offer that keeps the lead in the funnel. Closes a guard that exists for Local but is absent for the production provider.
findings.risk: Overlap query must use the same buffered window as slot generation to avoid rejecting legitimately adjacent slots; ensure the conflict is mapped to a retryable model remediation, not a hard escalation, so a genuinely-free alternative still books.

findings.title: When an org enables lifecycle tagging, conversations that booked never reach "booked" state — stalled-sweep can re-engage already-booked leads
findings.currentState: The booking-created lifecycle hook is wired but has NO producer: both runtime bootstraps register it as a no-op — apps/api/src/app.ts:705 and apps/api/src/bootstrap/inngest.ts:655 pass `registerBookingCreateHook: () => {}`. The DEFERRED comment (apps/api/src/bootstrap/lifecycle.ts:122-130) states the only bookingStore.create seat (calendar-book.ts) lacks conversationThreadId, so onBookingCreated (packages/core/src/conversation-lifecycle/event-hooks/booking-created-hook.ts) is never fired. Lifecycle is gated per-org by the lifecycleTagging.mechanical governance flag (apps/api/src/bootstrap/inngest.ts:629-642); when an org flips it to 'on', the qualified transition fires (sidecar hook) and the stalled-sweep cron runs — but the booked transition still cannot fire, so a snapshot that actually booked stays at 'qualified'.
findings.opportunity: The moment any pilot org enables lifecycle tagging, the stalled-sweep will see qualified-but-'stuck' conversations that in fact converted, and may fire a re-engagement nudge at a lead who already booked ('still thinking about it?') — a credibility-damaging, conversion-irrelevant message. The booked lifecycle state (distinct from opportunity stage) is structurally unreachable. This is the lifecycle-snapshot twin of the opportunity-stage finding and arms a footgun behind a flag operators can flip.
findings.proposedChange: Wire a real booking-created producer. The cleanest seam: have booking.create emit enough context (it already has contactId, orgId, opportunityId/serviceId and writes a 'booked' outbox event at calendar-book.ts:277-298) and resolve conversationThreadId by a (contactId, organizationId) thread lookup at the producer, then call onBookingCreated. If threading the id down is too invasive now, at minimum gate it: refuse to enable lifecycleTagging.mechanical for an org until the booked producer exists, so qualified-tracking can't run without booked-tracking. Add a test that a booking advances the snapshot to 'booked' when mode='on'.
findings.impactRationale: Prevents re-engaging already-booked leads (conversion + brand-trust harm) and makes the funnel's booked state real before the feature is switched on. Sharpens a DEFERRED note into a concrete, flag-activated defect.
findings.risk: Crosses package layers (tool boundary lacks conversationThreadId by design); the thread lookup must be tenant-scoped and tolerate no-thread (web/test) cases by no-op'ing rather than throwing inside the booking success path.

verdicts.notes:
All evidence holds and is stronger than claimed:

1. calendar-book.ts:31-38 — OpportunityStoreSubset interface has only findActiveByContact + create. updateStage is not even in the interface; the tool is structurally incapable of calling it. The success branch (lines 266-314) does: booking.update(status→confirmed) + outboxEvent.create(type:"booked") + return. Zero stage advancement.

2. skills/alex/SKILL.md:182 — the only crm-write.stage.update instruction is for "qualified". Phase 4 (lines 196-237) lists only calendar-book.slots.query, calendar-book.booking.create, and crm-write.activity.log (failure paths). No "booked" stage instruction exists anywhere in the file.

3. booking-created-hook.ts:13-34 — the lifecycle hook exists with toState:"booked", but lifecycle.ts:122-128 contains an explicit code comment: "DEFERRED wiring. The only bookingStore.create seat lives at calendar-book.ts:187 which does NOT have conversationThreadId in scope." apps/api/src/app.ts:705 and bootstrap/inngest.ts:655 both pass registerBookingCreateHook: () => {} (no-op). The handler is registered (lifecycle.ts:129) but no caller ever fires it.

4. apps/api/src/bootstrap/skill-mode.ts:554 — qualificationEvaluationHook IS wired as claimed, confirming the asymmetry: qualified = deterministic, booked = LLM-dependent with a missing prompt instruction.

The proposed fix (add updateStage call inside the success transaction at ~line 299, after the confirm+outbox writes, and extend OpportunityStoreSubset to include updateStage) is structurally correct. The opportunityId is already resolved at lines 183-194, making this genuinely S-effort. Impact is high because funnel/attribution and the "booked" stage in the CRM are load-bearing for the money path.

All cited evidence verified against live code:

1. packages/schemas/src/calendar.ts:9 — `bufferMinutes: z.number().int().nonnegative().default(15)` exists exactly as claimed. Zod default only fires on `.parse()`.

2. packages/core/src/skill-runtime/tools/calendar-book.ts:134-135 — `execute: async (params: unknown) => { const query = params as SlotQuery;` — confirmed, raw type cast, no `.parse()` call.

3. calendar-book.ts:123-133 — `inputSchema.properties` lists only dateFrom/dateTo/durationMinutes/service/timezone; `bufferMinutes` is absent. `required` array is those same 5 fields. The LLM is never told to send bufferMinutes, so it will not be in params.

4. packages/core/src/skill-runtime/input-schema-validator.ts:83-113 — `validateToolInput` only checks required fields and declared property types; no default injection. Confirmed.

5. packages/core/src/calendar/local-calendar-provider.ts:80 — `bufferMinutes: query.bufferMinutes` passed directly to generateAvailableSlots. Confirmed.

6. packages/core/src/calendar/google-calendar-adapter.ts:67 — same: `bufferMinutes: query.bufferMinutes`. Confirmed.

7. packages/core/src/calendar/slot-generator.ts:55 — `slotCursor.setTime(slotCursor.getTime() + (durationMinutes + bufferMinutes) * 60_000)` — confirmed. With bufferMinutes=undefined: (30 + undefined)*60000 = NaN; setTime(NaN) makes slotCursor an Invalid Date. Next while-condition at lines 40-42: `NaN + durationMinutes*60_000 <= dayEnd.getTime()` is false → loop exits. First slot IS pushed before line 55 executes, so exactly 1 slot per day is the outcome.

Bug is real and novel — not in SHIPPED list, not in any cited IN-FLIGHT PR (#791 = medical escalation, #794 = conversion-polish objection-closing, #782 = WorkTrace invariant). Impact is high (availability collapses to 1 slot/day, making booking nearly non-functional). Effort is S (one-line fix at calendar-book.ts:135 plus optional defensive guard in slot-generator.ts:55).

All four cited evidence points verified exactly as claimed.

1. calendar-book.ts:198-208 — `bookingStore.create()` is called BEFORE the provider (line 199), confirming the row is persisted as "pending_confirmation" before any provider call.

2. calendar-book.ts:209-228 — On P2002 (unique constraint violation), the code calls `findBySlot()` (line 211) to retrieve the colliding row but does NOT inspect its `status`. It unconditionally returns `fail("DUPLICATE_BOOKING", ...)` at line 217-227 regardless of whether the colliding row's status is "failed" or "cancelled".

3. booking-failure-handler.ts:84-87 — On provider or confirmation error, the transaction sets `status: "failed"` on the existing row (`tx.booking.update({ data: { status: "failed" } })`). The row is kept, not deleted.

4. schema.prisma:1924 — `@@unique([organizationId, contactId, service, startsAt])` is a plain (non-partial) unique constraint with no WHERE clause excluding failed/cancelled rows.

5. migration 20260418200000_add_booking_idempotency_constraint/migration.sql:2 — `ALTER TABLE "Booking" ADD CONSTRAINT ... UNIQUE ("organizationId", "contactId", "service", "startsAt")` — plain constraint, confirmed.

6. prisma-booking-store.ts:63-66 — `findBySlot` uses `findFirst({ where: { organizationId, contactId, service, startsAt } })` with no status filter, so the returned `existingBooking` in the P2002 branch could be a "failed" row.

7. SKILL.md:233-235 — confirmed: on booking.create failure Alex says "I wasn't able to lock in that slot just now."

8. The git commit af78bfe3 ("fix(agent-home): exclude failed bookings") only changed dashboard metrics/display files — it did NOT touch calendar-book.ts, booking-failure-handler.ts, or schema.prisma. Not already shipped.

9. None of the in-flight PRs (#794 conversion-polish, #791 medical escalation) are visible on the current branch and their described scope does not cover this idempotency path.

The bug is real and novel: a transient provider failure leaves a "failed" Booking row that blocks all future retries of the same slot via P2002, with no status-aware escape path in the P2002 catch block.

All four cited locations verified exactly as claimed.

1. packages/db/src/stores/prisma-booking-store.ts:23-42 — `PrismaBookingStore.create` calls `this.prisma.booking.create` directly with zero overlap check. Confirmed.

2. packages/core/src/calendar/google-calendar-adapter.ts:74-84 — `createBooking` calls `this.client.events.insert` blindly; no freebusy re-check before insert. Confirmed.

3. apps/api/src/bootstrap/calendar-provider-factory.ts:175-188 — Local path's `buildLocalStore.createInTransaction` runs a conflict query (`startsAt:{lt:endsAt}, endsAt:{gt:startsAt}, status:{notIn:["cancelled","failed"]}`) inside a Prisma `$transaction` before writing. Confirmed.

4. packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma:1924 — `@@unique([organizationId, contactId, service, startsAt])` — deduplicates only same-contact+service+slot, does NOT prevent two different contacts from booking the same physical slot. Confirmed.

Critical flow context (packages/core/src/skill-runtime/tools/calendar-book.ts:196-264): In `booking.create`, step 1 calls `deps.bookingStore.create(...)` (which is `PrismaBookingStore`, wired at apps/api/src/bootstrap/skill-mode.ts:129,279), then step 2 calls `provider.createBooking(...)`. For both Google and Local providers, the initial durable write goes through the NO-overlap-check `PrismaBookingStore.create`. The Local path's conflict guard in `buildLocalStore.createInTransaction` is used by `LocalCalendarProvider` internally for its own calendar-event step, but the race window exists before that at the shared `bookingStore.create` call.

The double-booking race is real and novel (not in SHIPPED or IN-FLIGHT list). Impact=high is credible for a booking product (money path). Effort=M is credible (add overlap query inside a transaction to PrismaBookingStore.create + SLOT_CONFLICT mapping in calendar-book.ts).

All five cited locations verified against live code on main.

apps/api/src/app.ts:705 — `registerBookingCreateHook: () => {}` confirmed no-op.
apps/api/src/bootstrap/inngest.ts:655 — `registerBookingCreateHook: () => {}` confirmed no-op.
apps/api/src/bootstrap/lifecycle.ts:12-28 — File header explicitly documents all five hooks as "DEFERRED (no caller passes a real registrar yet)"; line 129 does call `deps.registerBookingCreateHook(handler)` but since both callers supply `() => {}`, the handler is registered with an inert callback that never fires.
apps/api/src/bootstrap/lifecycle.ts:122-130 — DEFERRED comment at lines 122-128 states calendar-book.ts:187 is the only bookingStore.create seat and lacks conversationThreadId.
packages/core/src/conversation-lifecycle/event-hooks/booking-created-hook.ts:18 — `if (!event.conversationThreadId) return;` is an additional silent guard even if the hook were somehow triggered with null.
packages/core/src/skill-runtime/tools/calendar-book.ts — grep for `conversationThreadId` returns zero hits; the `ctx` object exposes `orgId` and `contactId` only. The outbox event written at lines 277-298 carries contactId/organizationId/bookingId/opportunityId/service but no conversationThreadId.

Not in SHIPPED or IN-FLIGHT context. Most recent git commits touching these files predate all listed shipped items. The bug is real: when `lifecycleTagging.mechanical` is `"on"` for an org, the stalled-sweep cron runs (inngest.ts:620-666 wires only the cron, not the booking hook), so a conversation snapshot that reached "qualified" and then booked stays at "qualified" indefinitely — making it eligible for re-engagement by the stalled-sweep.

Impact medium / effort M credible: the fix requires a new (contactId, organizationId) → conversationThreadId DB lookup at the producer boundary crossing package layers, plus tests.

dimensions.title: Model routing, inference economics & the turn loop
dimensions.laneSummary: The router's tiering logic itself is the next lever, and it is mis-calibrated: resolveTier keys cheap-vs-strong model on the intra-call LLM-loop counter rather than real conversation depth, so flipping it ON would route nearly every actual sales reply (qualification, objections, booking) to Haiku while the eval baseline - which runs router=undefined - is structurally blind to that downgrade. The single biggest opportunity is to re-key tiering on conversation depth AND add a router-ON eval variant before the flag flip, so the cost play is not bought with silent conversion loss. The turn loop is also resilience-thin: the per-call timeout is decorative, in-flight calls are never aborted (leaking requests + paid-for retries under a 10-min SDK default), and live Alex currently sends no temperature (sampling at ~1.0) with zero persisted cost/latency/cache telemetry to judge any of it.
dimensions.notesOnShippedQuality: Concrete defects inside shipped code in this lane: (1) anthropic-tool-adapter.ts:114/141 declares a profile.timeoutMs and ModelConfig carries timeoutMs (model-router.ts:90), but messages.create is invoked with no timeout/signal - the per-call timeout is dead code and the executor's Promise.race (skill-executor.ts:223) never cancels the underlying request, leaking it. (2) The Anthropic client is constructed bare (skill-mode.ts:341) with no explicit timeout/maxRetries, so the SDK's 10-min/2-retry defaults silently apply beneath the 30s wall. (3) skill-mode.ts:344 has a stale comment referencing TracePersistenceHook as if registered, but the hook is never instantiated/added (hooks array at line 540-546) - so ExecutionTrace.tokenUsage/durationMs stay empty for live Alex even though the executor computes them. (4) The shipped tier logic conflates the LLM-loop counter with conversation messageIndex (skill-executor.ts:149 -> skill-tier-context-builder.ts:26), making greeting->Haiku fire on the first LLM call of every turn regardless of depth, and making escalation->critical (model-router.ts:101, fed by this-invocation-only governance logs) effectively unreachable on single-LLM-call turns. (5) max_tokens is handled identically to end_turn (skill-executor.ts:260), returning truncated replies as complete.

findings.title: resolveTier keys on the intra-invocation LLM-loop counter, not conversation depth - routes EVERY inbound message's first reply to Haiku regardless of how deep the sale is
findings.currentState: skill-executor.ts:146-155 builds the tier context with turnCount: turnCount - 1, where turnCount is the executor's LLM-loop iteration counter (skill-executor.ts:191-197, incremented at the top of the while-loop). skill-tier-context-builder.ts:26 maps that straight to messageIndex. model-router.ts:97-98 Rule 1 returns default (Haiku) whenever messageIndex === 0. Because every Alex invocation starts the loop at turnCount=1, messageIndex is ALWAYS 0 on the first (and, for any reply needing no tool, the ONLY) LLM call - so the greetings-are-cheap rule fires for a lead on message #1 and a lead deep in a price negotiation on message #15 identically. The full conversation history IS available (params.messages, skill-executor.ts:183) but resolveProfile never consults it. Rule 3 escalation->critical (model-router.ts:101) reads governanceHook.getGovernanceLogs() which is empty on the first LLM call and only reflects THIS invocation's tool-governance, so critical is unreachable on a single-LLM-call turn.
findings.opportunity: The router's value prop is cheap model for trivial turns, strong model for hard turns. As wired, trivial means first LLM iteration of the call, true for nearly every conversational reply (Alex usually answers in one LLM call with no tool). Flipping the router ON would silently downgrade the bulk of real medspa sales replies - objection handling, price negotiation, qualification - to Haiku, while the eval baseline (#672) is measured Sonnet-everywhere and is structurally blind to tier choice (router=undefined in the eval, evals/alex-conversation/run-conversation.ts:261). The conversion regression would ship invisibly.
findings.proposedChange: Feed real conversation depth into the tier context: pass messageIndex from params.messages (count of user/total messages) into buildTierContext instead of turnCount - 1, and rename the loop counter so the concepts are not conflated. Keep a separate signal for is-this-a-follow-up-LLM-iteration. Re-tune for a sales chat: a genuine first inbound greeting can be Haiku, but turns 2+ (active qualification/objection/booking) should default to premium (Sonnet). Make previousTurnEscalated also consult inbound pre-input-gate / prior-message escalation state. Before flipping the flag, add a router-ON variant to the eval harness so tier decisions are exercised against the 72-scenario baseline.
findings.impactRationale: Directly governs booking conversion: it picks which model answers the hardest, highest-intent turns. Mis-keyed tiering means the cost-optimization is bought with conversion quality on exactly the turns that close or lose the appointment, and the eval cannot catch it.
findings.risk: Re-tuning toward premium on deeper turns reduces the cost savings the router promised; needs a cost/quality trade-off decision and an eval run that actually exercises tiers. Changing messageIndex semantics touches existing tier tests.

findings.title: Live Alex (router OFF) sends NO temperature to Anthropic - runs the frontline compliance-sensitive sales agent at the provider default (~1.0)
findings.currentState: The only temperature in the SkillMode path comes from modelConfig.temperature via resolveProfile (skill-executor.ts:160-161), which requires a router. In production the executor is constructed with router=undefined (apps/api/src/bootstrap/skill-mode.ts:547-555), so profile is undefined every turn. The adapter only sets temperature when params.profile?.temperature !== undefined (anthropic-tool-adapter.ts:149-151); otherwise it omits the field and the API defaults to 1.0. The router's intended values (Haiku 0.7 / Sonnet 0.5 / Opus 0.3, model-router.ts:44/50/56) never reach the wire today. (temp 0.1 in apps/chat/interpreter/*.ts and 0.6 in conversation-deps.ts are different, non-SkillMode paths.)
findings.opportunity: A frontline agent that must stay inside claim-boundaries and a qualification script is sampling at maximum variance. Higher temperature increases off-script phrasing, fabricated specifics, and unsubstantiated medical-benefit claims - which the claim-classifier must then catch (more classifier load, more handoffs, more false-positive deferrals) - and increases run-to-run inconsistency that makes the eval baseline noisier and harder to lock (#672).
findings.proposedChange: Set a deterministic default temperature in the live path independent of the router: have AnthropicToolAdapter default to a low temperature (e.g. 0.4-0.5) when no profile is supplied, or have the production bootstrap pass a fixed conservative temperature. One-line, flag-independent safety/quality win that also de-noises the eval baseline. When the router is later enabled, its per-tier temperatures take over.
findings.impactRationale: Safety/compliance and conversion: lower sampling variance reduces unsubstantiated-claim risk (PDPA / medical-advertising exposure for medspa) and produces steadier, more on-script qualification - and stabilizes the eval baseline that gates the router flip.
findings.risk: Slightly less linguistic variety; pick a value (0.4-0.5) that preserves warmth. No conflict with the eval's forced temp:0 (eval uses its own temp0-adapter).

findings.title: Per-call timeout is dead and in-flight calls are never aborted: the only guard is a 30s race that leaks the request, atop the SDK's silent 10-min/2-retry default
findings.currentState: ModelConfig carries timeoutMs (default 8000, model-router.ts:30/90) and the adapter's profile type declares timeoutMs (anthropic-tool-adapter.ts:114), but messages.create (anthropic-tool-adapter.ts:141-152) is called with NO timeout, NO signal, no AbortController - the field is decorative. The Anthropic client is constructed bare (new Anthropic({ apiKey }), skill-mode.ts:341), so SDK defaults apply: 600000ms (10 min) request timeout and maxRetries=2. The executor's only wall is a Promise.race against remainingMs = maxRuntimeMs(30s) - elapsed (skill-executor.ts:216-243), but the race only stops awaiting - it does not cancel the underlying HTTP request, which keeps running and keeps being SDK-retried up to 2x at 10-min each. SkillMode.execute calls the executor once with no retry/fallback and turns any throw into a failed ExecutionResult (skill-mode.ts:73-111), so a single slow/aborted turn yields no reply to the lead.
findings.opportunity: On a WhatsApp sales chat, a lead who waits >30s gets dropped (failed result, no reply) while the abandoned request and its retries keep consuming an Anthropic connection and tokens you pay for but never use. A transient 5xx/429 is invisibly retried twice at 10-min each before the 30s race fires, so the failure mode under provider hiccups is silent drop not fast graceful fallback. There is no fast per-call timeout to trip a degrade-to-cheaper-tier or a quick second attempt.
findings.proposedChange: (1) Construct the Anthropic client with explicit timeout and maxRetries (e.g. timeout: 15000, maxRetries: 1) at skill-mode.ts:341 so calls fail fast and predictably. (2) Thread the executor's AbortController signal into chatWithTools and forward it to messages.create({ ..., signal }) so an aborted call is actually cancelled (stops the leak and the wasted retries). (3) Honor profile.timeoutMs by passing it as the per-call { timeout } request option. This makes the documented 8s/per-tier timeout real and the 30s wall enforce-with-cancel instead of fire-and-leak.
findings.impactRationale: Live reliability and cost: prevents silent lead drops under latency, stops paying for abandoned/retried requests, and gives a real fast-timeout hook to degrade gracefully instead of failing the conversation.
findings.risk: Tighter timeouts/retries could surface as more visible fast-failures under genuine provider slowness; needs a sensible fallback (one retry on a cheaper tier or a holding message). Threading the signal touches the adapter signature and tests.

findings.title: TracePersistenceHook is referenced by a stale comment but never wired into live Alex - per-conversation token/latency telemetry has no producer
findings.currentState: The production hooks array (skill-mode.ts:540-546) is exactly [GovernanceHook, safetyGateHook, claimClassifierHook, pdpaConsentGateHook, whatsAppWindowGateHook]. TracePersistenceHook is never instantiated - the only mention is a stale comment claiming the safety gate is registered BEFORE TracePersistenceHook (skill-mode.ts:344). The executor DOES compute per-conversation token usage and duration (skill-executor.ts:245-246, 300-306) and the hook + DB column exist to persist them (trace-persistence-hook.ts:43, prisma-execution-trace-store.ts:17/46, ExecutionTrace.tokenUsage), but with the hook absent the column stays empty for Alex. SkillMode.execute also drops tokenUsage when mapping SkillExecutionResult -> ExecutionResult (skill-mode.ts:93-106). Net: live Alex cost and latency are unobservable. Separately, the adapter reads only input_tokens/output_tokens (anthropic-tool-adapter.ts:181-185) and LLMUsage has no cache fields (llm-types.ts:39-42), so prompt-cache hit-rate is unmeasurable and the 64k token budget counts cache-read tokens at full weight.
findings.opportunity: You cannot manage what you cannot see. The decision to flip the router (a cost play) and to lock the eval baseline both need a before/after on tokens-per-conversation, p50/p95 latency, and cache hit-rate per org/skill. Today none of that is persisted for Alex, so the router's projected savings and the caching depth cannot be validated against real traffic, and a cost regression (e.g. cache misses from a prompt change) would be invisible.
findings.proposedChange: Wire TracePersistenceHook into the live Alex hooks array (trigger:chat_message + input-parameters hash), and confirm the executor invokes runAfterSkillHooks/runOnErrorHooks so it fires (today the executor loop does not call those runners - either call them or emit usage inline). Extend LLMUsage and the adapter to capture cache_read_input_tokens / cache_creation_input_tokens, and subtract cached-read tokens from budget accounting. Surface tokens/latency/cache-hit per org so the router flip and eval lock can be judged on data.
findings.impactRationale: Cost/latency observability is the prerequisite for safely flipping the router and locking the eval baseline; without it the two biggest pending levers in this lane are flown blind, and cache regressions go undetected.
findings.risk: Adding a per-turn persistence hook adds a small write per conversation; it is fire-and-forget (errors swallowed) so it should not block replies, but verify it does not double-count against any existing WorkTrace persistence.

findings.title: A max_tokens-truncated reply is returned to the lead as if complete - mid-sentence cutoffs on a 1024-token cap with no continuation
findings.currentState: skill-executor.ts:260 treats stopReason === max_tokens identically to end_turn: it extracts the (truncated) text and returns it as the final response with status success. No continuation request, no retry at higher max_tokens, no flag/log that the reply was cut. The cap is tight: DEFAULT_MAX_TOKENS=1024 in the adapter (anthropic-tool-adapter.ts:16/143) governs the router-OFF path, and the default tier (where nearly every turn lands per finding #1) is also maxTokens=1024 (model-router.ts:44). A reply that runs long - a detailed objection rebuttal, a multi-option booking proposal - gets silently chopped mid-sentence and sent to the customer over WhatsApp.
findings.opportunity: A truncated, mid-sentence sales message reads as broken and erodes trust at exactly the high-effort moments (long rebuttals, multi-slot offers) that move a booking. Because the qualification sidecar and intent tag are parsed from the same truncated text (skill-executor.ts:266-296), a cutoff can also drop the trailing sidecar/intent, corrupting qualification capture.
findings.proposedChange: Detect stopReason === max_tokens explicitly: either (a) issue one continuation turn (append the partial assistant text, re-call, concatenate), or (b) at minimum log/flag the truncation and avoid sending an obviously mid-sentence message (trim to the last complete sentence + brief continuation). Raise the conversational max_tokens floor for Alex (e.g. 1536-2048) since the reply is the product. Ensure sidecar/intent parsing is robust to (or re-requested on) truncation.
findings.impactRationale: Booking conversion and data integrity: prevents broken-looking replies at decisive moments and protects the qualification-signal capture that downstream lifecycle/routing depends on.
findings.risk: A continuation turn adds latency/cost and consumes one of the 6 turn slots; raising max_tokens raises per-reply cost. Bounding to one continuation keeps it contained.

verdicts.notes:
All cited lines check out exactly as described.

skill-executor.ts:191-195: `turnCount` initializes to 0, increments to 1 at the top of the while-loop, then calls `resolveProfile(params, turnCount, ...)` with `turnCount=1` on every invocation's first LLM call.

skill-executor.ts:146-147: `buildTierContext({ turnCount: turnCount - 1, ... })` — so on the first LLM call, `turnCount - 1 = 0`.

skill-tier-context-builder.ts:26: `messageIndex: input.turnCount` — maps the intra-invocation counter directly to `messageIndex`.

model-router.ts:97-98: Rule 1 fires when `context.messageIndex === 0` → returns `"default"` (Haiku). This is unconditional on every invocation's first (and, for tool-free turns, only) LLM call.

params.messages (skill-executor.ts:183) is never passed to resolveProfile or buildTierContext, so real conversation depth (e.g., 15 prior messages in a price negotiation) is invisible to the router.

model-router.ts:101: Rule 3 (`previousTurnEscalated → "critical"`) reads governance logs that are empty at the start of each invocation, confirming it is unreachable on a single-LLM-call turn.

The finding is not already-shipped or in-flight: the router flag is default OFF (confirmed in context), and no in-flight PR addresses this specific logic flaw. Impact is genuinely high for when the flag is flipped, but since the router is currently dormant in production the immediate blast radius is zero — impact is high-when-enabled, not high-today. Effort M is appropriate.

All cited evidence verified exactly as claimed. (1) anthropic-tool-adapter.ts:149-151: temperature is only spread into the API request when `params.profile?.temperature !== undefined` — the spread is guarded by that conditional, so no profile = no temperature field on the wire. (2) skill-executor.ts:137-143: `resolveProfile()` immediately returns `undefined` when `!this.router`. (3) skill-executor.ts:161: the only path that sets temperature on the returned profile flows through `modelConfig.temperature` from the router. (4) apps/api/src/bootstrap/skill-mode.ts:547-550: production bootstrap constructs `new SkillExecutorImpl(adapter, toolsMap, undefined, hooks, ...)` — the third constructor argument is the `router?` parameter (confirmed by skill-executor.ts:100 constructor signature), and it is explicitly `undefined`. (5) model-router.ts:44/50/56: router temperatures are Haiku=0.7, Sonnet=0.5, Opus=0.3 — never reached in production today. (6) conversation-deps.ts:63 (temp=0.6) is a completely separate non-SkillMode fetch path, as stated. No in-flight PR (#794, #782) touches temperature. Finding is novel, not in shipped context, not in any open PR. Impact assessment of "high" is credible: a compliance-sensitive sales agent running at provider default (~1.0) is a real production quality/safety gap that is fixed by a one-line default in the adapter or at bootstrap construction time.

All four cited code locations verified:

1. packages/core/src/skill-runtime/adapters/anthropic-tool-adapter.ts:114 — profile param type includes `timeoutMs: number`, confirmed.

2. anthropic-tool-adapter.ts:141-152 — `messages.create()` passes model, max_tokens, system, messages, tools, and optionally temperature from profile, but zero timeout or signal. profile.timeoutMs is accessible but never forwarded. Confirmed.

3. apps/api/src/bootstrap/skill-mode.ts:341 — `new Anthropic({ apiKey: process.env["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"] })` with no timeout or maxRetries. SDK defaults (600s, maxRetries=2) apply. Confirmed.

4. packages/core/src/skill-runtime/skill-executor.ts:216-243 — Promise.race with remainingMs timeout exists. No AbortController or signal created. The race rejects the await chain but does not cancel the underlying HTTP request; the Anthropic SDK connection continues running.

Additional: packages/core/src/model-router.ts:30 — DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_MS=8000; resolveProfile (skill-executor.ts:157) calls router.resolve(slot) with no timeoutMs override option, so timeoutMs=8000 lands in the profile (model-router.ts:90) — but never reaches messages.create. When ALEX_MODEL_ROUTER_ENABLED is OFF, resolveProfile returns undefined (skill-executor.ts:143), so profile is undefined and the adapter falls back to DEFAULT_MODEL/DEFAULT_MAX_TOKENS with no timeout at all.

This is not in the SHIPPED list (prompt caching, memory eviction, ModelRouter structure) nor in any cited in-flight PR (#794 conversion-polish, #791 medical red-flag, #782 WorkTrace invariant). The 30s Promise.race wall clock error surfaces to the user but the underlying HTTP call continues up to 20 min (10 min × 2 SDK retries), leaking the connection.

correctedImpact: medium (not high) — the 30s race does protect the user-facing turn from hanging past 30s; the leak is a resource/cost concern, not a correctness or data-safety bug. The proposed fix is straightforward and clearly bounded.
correctedEffort: S (not M) — all three changes are local: pass timeout+maxRetries to the Anthropic constructor, thread AbortController signal through chatWithTools into messages.create, and pass profile.timeoutMs as the per-call option. No schema changes or cross-package refactors needed.

All eight evidence points verified against live code:

1. apps/api/src/bootstrap/skill-mode.ts:540-546 — hooks array is exactly [GovernanceHook, safetyGateHook, claimClassifierHook, pdpaConsentGateHook, whatsAppWindowGateHook]. TracePersistenceHook never instantiated or imported (grep found only the stale comment at line 344).

2. apps/api/src/bootstrap/skill-mode.ts:344 — stale comment reads "DeterministicSafetyGateHook registered BEFORE TracePersistenceHook (and any future hooks..." — TracePersistenceHook is referenced here as though it will exist, but never wired.

3. packages/core/src/skill-runtime/hooks/trace-persistence-hook.ts:43 — hook correctly stores tokenUsage: result.tokenUsage in afterSkill, and onError handles errors too. Hook is correct; it is simply not wired.

4. packages/db/src/stores/prisma-execution-trace-store.ts:17 — tokenUsage: { input: number; output: number } field exists; the DB column exists and the store is ready to accept the data.

5. packages/core/src/skill-runtime/skill-executor.ts imports only runBeforeLlmCallHooks, runAfterLlmCallHooks, runBeforeToolCallHooks, runAfterToolCallHooks from hook-runner.ts (lines 34-39). runAfterSkillHooks and runOnErrorHooks exist in hook-runner.ts:77+89 but are only called from batch-skill-handler.ts — never from skill-executor.ts. So even if TracePersistenceHook were added to the hooks array, afterSkill would never fire in the chat path.

6. packages/core/src/platform/modes/skill-mode.ts:93-106 — return object maps SkillExecutionResult to ExecutionResult with fields workUnitId, outcome, summary, outputs, mode, durationMs, traceId, error, injectedPatternIds. tokenUsage is not passed through; the field is silently dropped.

7. packages/core/src/skill-runtime/adapters/anthropic-tool-adapter.ts:181-185 — adapter maps only response.usage.input_tokens and response.usage.output_tokens. No cache_read_input_tokens or cache_creation_input_tokens captured.

8. packages/core/src/skill-runtime/llm-types.ts:39-42 — LLMUsage interface has only inputTokens and outputTokens. No cache fields.

Not in the shipped/in-flight context. Impact is high (not medium): prompt caching IS shipped and active, but cache hit-rate is completely unmeasurable since cache_read tokens are silently dropped; the 64k budget counter overcounts cached tokens at full weight; and live Alex cost/latency have zero observability post-call. Two distinct fixes required: (a) wire the hook AND call runAfterSkillHooks/runOnErrorHooks from skill-executor.ts, (b) extend LLMUsage + adapter for cache fields. Effort M is accurate.

All four cited locations confirmed in live code. skill-executor.ts:260 — a single `if (response.stopReason === "end_turn" || response.stopReason === "max_tokens")` branch handles both cases identically: it extracts text, parses the qualification sidecar, and returns `status: "success"` with no truncation log, no retry, and no continuation. anthropic-tool-adapter.ts:16 — `DEFAULT_MAX_TOKENS = 1024`. anthropic-tool-adapter.ts:143 — the API call resolves `max_tokens` as `params.profile?.maxTokens ?? params.maxTokens ?? DEFAULT_MAX_TOKENS`; when the router flag is OFF, no profile is injected, so this falls through to 1024. model-router.ts:44 — the `default` slot specifies `maxTokens: 1024` (premium=2048, critical=4096). This finding is real, novel (not in SHIPPED or IN-FLIGHT context), and the impact/effort rating is credible.

dimensions.title: Safety, claim classifier & escalation precision
dimensions.laneSummary: The deterministic safety machinery (inbound pre-input escalation gate, banned-phrase output gate, Layer-2/3 claim classifier) is well-architected and fail-closed, but its PRECISION is unbalanced: it over-escalates routine medspa conversation while leaving named-contraindication and self-disclosed-minor safety gaps open. The single biggest opportunity is bidirectional — the claim-classifier hook ignores the model's confidence entirely (it is recorded for audit but never gates a decision), which is the latent root cause of over-flag #673 and a direct landmine on the planned off→observe→enforce flip. The escalation-trigger taxonomy needs both narrowing (bare 'anxious', un-negated condition keywords) and widening (medications, first-person minor age).
dimensions.notesOnShippedQuality: Two concrete defects inside shipped code. (1) Escalate tool drops the LLM-provided summary and customerSentiment: createEscalateToolFactory collects reason/summary/customerSentiment (packages/core/src/skill-runtime/tools/escalate.ts:14-18,45-54) but calls assembler.assemble with messages:[] and never forwards summary/customerSentiment (escalate.ts:65-72), so HandoffPackageAssembler builds the human-facing conversationSummary from an empty array — turnCount:0, keyTopics:[], a generic suggestedOpening (packages/core/src/handoff/package-assembler.ts:43-58). The operator who picks up the escalation gets none of the context Alex computed. (2) Substantiation matching is verbatim-substring only: matchClaim requires sentenceLower.includes(claim.claimText.toLowerCase()) (packages/core/src/governance/classifier/substantiation-resolver.ts:62) and matchRegulatory likewise (:89). Because the LLM paraphrases, an approved-but-paraphrased efficacy/safety claim will almost never substring-match a curated ApprovedComplianceClaim, so substantiation resolves 'missing' and the sentence is rewritten/escalated — this is a structural amplifier of the over-flag problem the moment the classifier is enabled in enforce. Also note: the dialogue/emotional-classifier.ts module is exported from the core barrel (index.ts:134) but has zero importers in skill-runtime, apps/api, or apps/chat, so it is dead in the live Alex path — there is no deterministic frustration/anger escalation backstop and the inbound taxonomy has no negative-sentiment category; frustration escalation is entirely LLM-discretionary.

findings.title: Claim-classifier hook applies NO confidence threshold — every flag/allow is taken at any confidence, a landmine on the planned off→enforce flip
findings.currentState: In ClaimClassifierHook.decideAction the model's confidence is read only to stamp audit details (packages/core/src/skill-runtime/hooks/claim-classifier.ts:192) and is never used to gate a decision. claimType==="none" → allow regardless of confidence (claim-classifier.ts:196); any flagged type (efficacy/safety-claim/superiority/urgency/testimonial/medical-advice/diagnosis/credentials) → rewrite/escalate regardless of confidence (claim-classifier.ts:198-268). ClaimClassifierConfigSchema (packages/schemas/src/governance-config.ts:37-43) has only {mode, latencyBudgetMs, model} — no confidenceThreshold field. The offline eval DOES enforce a per-entry expectedConfidenceFloor (packages/core/src/governance/classifier/eval/run-eval.ts:111), so the floor exists in measurement but not in production.
findings.opportunity: This is the root mechanism behind over-flag #673 in BOTH directions. False-positive: a low-confidence (e.g. 0.35) medical-advice/testimonial/diagnosis classification ESCALATES the whole turn — freezes the conversation and hands the lead to a human (claim-classifier.ts:198, ESCALATE_ONLY at :57). False-negative: a low-confidence "none" on a sentence that is actually an unsubstantiated efficacy/safety claim is silently allowed (:196). Because the classifier ships mode=off today and the known backlog is to flip it off→observe→enforce, enabling enforce without a floor will rewrite/escalate a large fraction of normal turns on day one of activation.
findings.proposedChange: Add confidenceThreshold (default ~0.7, per-jurisdiction overridable) to ClaimClassifierConfigSchema. In decideAction, when result.confidence < threshold treat a flagged claimType as kind:"allow" (with an observe-level verdict recorded for telemetry) rather than escalate/rewrite, and treat a low-confidence "none" as a soft-escalate or re-check candidate. Gate the off→observe→enforce flip on a tuned threshold measured against the existing golden-set confidence distribution.
findings.impactRationale: Directly governs conversion vs safety on the planned activation path: without a floor, flipping to enforce converts routine, compliant sentences into human handoffs (lost bookings) while still letting genuinely-uncertain unsafe sentences slip as "none". A single tunable closes both leaks and de-risks the flip ops has already scheduled.
findings.risk: Threshold too high re-opens false-negatives; mitigate by deriving the default from the golden-set floors (0.75–0.95 there) and keeping enforce gated on an observe-mode bake that reports flip-rate by confidence bucket.

findings.title: Pre-input gate escalates on the bare word 'anxious'/'anxiety' — collides head-on with Alex's designed 'aesthetic anxiety' objection and blocks Alex before he can respond
findings.currentState: COMMON_ESCALATION_TRIGGERS sensitive_keyword_mental_health matches /\b(depress(ed|ion)|anxiety|anxious|suicidal|self.harm|eating disorder|anorexia|bulimia)\b/i with NO clinical-context qualifier and NO negation (packages/core/src/governance/escalation-triggers/common.ts:64-70). In enforce mode the pre-input gate blocks platformIngress.submit and sends a handoff on ANY match (packages/core/src/channel-gateway/pre-input-gate.ts:99-109,186-187). Meanwhile Alex's playbook explicitly treats procedure/outcome anxiety as a core objection: 'The lead has aesthetic anxiety… fear-based hesitation, not a price question. Don't dismiss it; take it seriously.' (skills/alex/references/medspa/objection-handling.md:61-62), and FEAR_PATTERNS in the (unused) emotional model lists exactly these words as a normal sales concern (packages/core/src/dialogue/emotional-classifier.ts:19).
findings.opportunity: A lead writing 'I'm a bit anxious about whether it'll hurt' or 'nervous/anxious about my first time' — the single most common medspa lead emotion — hits sensitive_keyword_mental_health and, under enforce, is handed to a human before Alex ever replies.
