This is a pure false-positive that kills the exact conversations Alex was built to win, and it routes high-intent leads into a 4h human SLA queue instead of an instant reassuring reply.
findings.proposedChange: Split the mental-health trigger: keep a high-precision clinical sub-pattern that still escalates (suicidal|self-harm|eating disorder|anorexia|bulimia, and qualified forms like 'anxiety disorder|diagnosed with (anxiety|depression)|panic attacks|(on|taking) (anti-?depressants|anxiety medication)'), but REMOVE the bare 'anxious|anxiety|depress(ed)' tokens from the auto-escalate set so ordinary procedure nerves stay with Alex. Add negations for the clinical sub-pattern as well.
findings.impactRationale: High conversion leverage: nervous-but-interested leads are the bread-and-butter of medspa booking. Auto-escalating them to a slow human queue both loses the booking and burns operator time on a non-issue, while the genuine clinical risk (suicidal ideation, eating disorders) is still caught by the tightened pattern.
findings.risk: Tightening could miss a coded cry-for-help; mitigate by retaining the explicit suicidal/self-harm/eating-disorder tokens unconditionally and only narrowing the generic-anxiety tokens, which are not themselves safety red flags in an aesthetics context.

findings.title: Inbound escalation taxonomy has no medication/contraindication category — Roaccutane, anticoagulants, keloid history pass straight to Alex
findings.currentState: The full inbound trigger set (COMMON_ESCALATION_TRIGGERS) covers pregnancy/breastfeeding, prior adverse reaction, prior complaint, competitor-negative, multi-treatment combo, minors, a fixed list of chronic conditions (diabetes/hypertension/cancer/chemo/pacemaker/epilepsy/seizures), and mental health (packages/core/src/governance/escalation-triggers/common.ts:3-70). A grep across packages/core and skills/alex finds zero references to isotretinoin/Roaccutane/Accutane, warfarin/anticoagulant/blood-thinner, retinoid, immunosuppressant, or keloid. These are first-order contraindications for the core medspa procedures (lasers, peels, microneedling, injectables) — e.g. active isotretinoin is a hard contraindication for ablative/energy treatments.
findings.opportunity: A lead writing 'I'm on Roaccutane, can I still do laser?' or 'I'm on blood thinners — is filler okay?' is a genuine safety red flag, yet the deterministic inbound gate does not fire, so safety rests entirely on the LLM choosing to redirect. This is the inbound (lead-message) mirror of the in-flight output-side red-flag work (#791), which scans Alex's RESPONSE; a contraindication disclosed by the lead should be caught before Alex ever forms a reply.
findings.proposedChange: Add a new sensitive_keyword (or dedicated 'contraindication') entry with patterns for isotretinoin/Roaccutane/Accutane, anticoagulant/warfarin/blood thinner/(on )aspirin therapy, oral steroids/immunosuppressant, and keloid/keloid-prone, mapped to medical_safety_trigger in REASON_CODE_BY_TRIGGER. Keep it conservative and negation-guarded ('no longer on', 'stopped').
findings.impactRationale: Safety/compliance: these are the disclosures most likely to cause real harm if a procedure proceeds, and SG/MY regulators expect doctor-gated suitability assessment. A deterministic inbound catch converts a latent liability into a governed handoff, independent of LLM judgment.
findings.risk: Over-escalation on 'I stopped Roaccutane last year'; mitigate with negations identical to the prior-adverse-reaction entry pattern (common.ts:23).

findings.title: Minor-protection trigger only catches third-party phrasing ('my daughter/teenage/under 18') — a lead stating their own age ('I'm 16') is not flagged
findings.currentState: sensitive_keyword_minor matches /\b(my (daughter|son)|teenage|under ?\s?(16|18))\b/i (packages/core/src/governance/escalation-triggers/common.ts:50-53). There is no pattern for first-person age self-disclosure such as 'I'm 16', 'I am 15', 'I'm 17 years old', or 'turning 16'. A grep for self-disclosed minor-age patterns in the governance tree returns nothing.
findings.opportunity: Aesthetic procedures on minors require parental consent / are restricted in SG and MY. A 16-year-old inbound lead writing 'hi I'm 16, can I get fillers?' bypasses the deterministic gate entirely — the worst-case compliance scenario — and safety again falls to LLM discretion. The current pattern protects only when an adult mentions a minor, not when the minor self-identifies.
findings.proposedChange: Extend the minor trigger with first-person age patterns, e.g. /\bi'?m\s?(1[0-7])\b/i, /\bi am\s?(1[0-7])\b/i, /\b(1[0-7])\s?(years old|yo|y\/o)\b/i, /\bturning\s?(1[0-7])\b/i, mapped (as today) to sensitive_keyword → sensitive_inbound. Consider raising to medical_safety_trigger given consent stakes.
findings.impactRationale: Compliance/safety: serving a minor an aesthetic-procedure conversation without a human gate is a bright-line regulatory and reputational risk in both target jurisdictions; a 4-character age pattern closes it deterministically.
findings.risk: False positives on '16 years of experience' or '16 units of botox'; mitigate by anchoring on first-person subject ('I'm/I am/turning') and excluding 'units'/'years of experience' via negation or tighter anchoring.

findings.title: sensitive_keyword and multi_treatment_combo triggers have no negation guard — 'I'm NOT diabetic', 'my mum had cancer', 'I don't want to combine treatments' all over-escalate
findings.currentState: In COMMON_ESCALATION_TRIGGERS only pregnancy, breastfeeding, prior_adverse_reaction and prior_complaint carry negations array entries (common.ts:8,14,23,32). sensitive_keyword_medical_condition (common.ts:55-60), sensitive_keyword_minor (:50-53), sensitive_keyword_mental_health (:64-70) and multi_treatment_combo (:42-48) have NO negations. The scanner suppresses an entry only if a negation matches in the same sentence (packages/core/src/governance/scanner/escalation-trigger-scanner.ts:62), so without negations these fire on any keyword mention regardless of polarity or third-party reference.
findings.opportunity: Benign mentions become hard blocks in enforce mode: 'I'm not diabetic so this is fine right?', 'my mum has hypertension but I'm healthy', 'I don't want to combine botox and filler' all hand the lead to a human before Alex can answer. This compounds the over-flag conversion loss, and unlike the classifier (mode=off today) the deterministic gate is the first thing operators turn on.
findings.proposedChange: Add negations to the medical-condition, minor, mental-health and multi-treatment entries mirroring the existing prior_adverse_reaction pattern: suppress when 'no/never/not/don't/no history of/my (mum|mother|father|friend)' co-occurs in the same sentence as the keyword. Keep blocking the genuine first-person affirmative disclosures.
findings.impactRationale: Conversion: every spurious block is a lost or delayed booking and an operator-queue item; negation guards are the cheapest precision lever and bring these categories up to the bar the pregnancy/adverse-reaction entries already meet.
findings.risk: A too-broad family negation ('my mum had cancer, and I have it too') could suppress a real disclosure; scope negations to subject/polarity tokens and keep them sentence-local as the scanner already requires.

verdicts.notes: All cited evidence confirmed as stated. claim-classifier.ts:192 — confidence is read into baseDetails only (audit stamp). claim-classifier.ts:196 — claimType==="none" returns allow unconditionally. claim-classifier.ts:198-268 — all flagged paths (ESCALATE_ONLY, REWRITEABLE, credentials, defensive fallback) branch on claimType only; there is no comparison against any confidence value anywhere in decideAction. governance-config.ts:37-43 — ClaimClassifierConfigSchema is exactly {mode, latencyBudgetMs, model}; no confidenceThreshold field exists. Exhaustive grep for "confidenceThreshold" across packages/core/src/skill-runtime/ and packages/schemas/ returned zero hits. run-eval.ts:111 — the offline eval DOES check `result.confidence >= golden.expectedConfidenceFloor`, confirming the floor exists in measurement only, not production. Not in SHIPPED or IN-FLIGHT context. The gap is real and sharpens the known risk on the off→enforce flip.

All four evidence citations check out against live code.

packages/core/src/governance/escalation-triggers/common.ts:64-70: Entry `sensitive_keyword_mental_health` is exactly as claimed — pattern `/\b(depress(ed|ion)|anxiety|anxious|suicidal|self.harm|eating disorder|anorexia|bulimia)\b/i`, and crucially has NO `negations` field (unlike pregnancy:8, breastfeeding:14, prior_adverse_reaction:23 which all have negations arrays). The `negations` field is optional per types.ts:16.

packages/core/src/channel-gateway/pre-input-gate.ts: The cited line numbers are slightly off (the scanner runs at line 99; the enforce block that calls replySink.send is at line 186 and returns true at line 187), but the behavior description is accurate. In enforce mode (mode !== "off" and mode !== "observe"), any match short-circuits submit and sends handoff text.

skills/alex/references/medspa/objection-handling.md:61-62: Confirmed word-for-word: "The lead has aesthetic anxiety about outcome, often based on media images of over-treated results. This is fear-based hesitation, not a price question."

packages/core/src/dialogue/emotional-classifier.ts:19: FEAR_PATTERNS confirmed at line 19, includes `anxious` alongside `nervous|worried|fear|risk` — framing these as normal sales concern signals.

Collision is real and novel: bare `anxious` in a message like "I'm anxious about looking unnatural" would fire the pre-input gate before Alex can respond, triggering a human handoff, directly contradicting the objection-handling playbook that designates aesthetic anxiety as a core objection Alex should handle.

Not already-shipped or in-flight: PR #791 (merged, medical red-flag escalation Slice 1) only adds `medical_safety` HandoffReason to SKILL.md and escalate tool — it does not touch common.ts escalation trigger patterns. No open PR in the list touches the mental-health trigger.

Impact corrected to medium (not high): The gate fires only in enforce mode; if most deployments run in observe mode, the live collision rate may be low. The impact is real but depends on operational configuration that is not confirmed from code alone. Effort S is accurate — the fix is a regex split in one file (common.ts).

Evidence holds. The gap in COMMON_ESCALATION_TRIGGERS is confirmed: packages/core/src/governance/escalation-triggers/common.ts:55-59 has the sensitive_keyword_medical_condition entry covering diabetes/hypertension/cancer/chemo/pacemaker/epilepsy/seizures, but zero patterns for isotretinoin/Roaccutane/Accutane, anticoagulants, retinoids, immunosuppressants, or keloid. A grep across all trigger files (common.ts, sg.ts, my.ts) and skills/alex/SKILL.md confirms none of these terms appear anywhere in the deterministic enforcement layer. Demo fixture packages/db/prisma/fixtures/demo-knowledge.ts:329-330 mentions Accutane and keloid as soft disqualifiers in instructional text but this is not a trigger rule. However, this is NOT novel: PR #791 (merged to main as commit 38f0129d) explicitly documents this as "Slice 2 (ContraindicationGateHook) — designed and deferred." The #791 spec commit (16a1dbe4) and the corresponding plan file explicitly call out the contraindication hook as a follow-on slice. Slice 1 only added the medical_safety schema reason and updated SKILL.md prompt-level language. The gap is real and the fix is correctly scoped as S effort, but it is already identified, designed, and in the tracked backlog as a named next slice — making this already-in-flight rather than novel. Impact is corrected to medium rather than high: Slice 1's SKILL.md changes give Alex some LLM-level awareness of medical red flags; the missing piece is deterministic pre-input gating (the ContraindicationGateHook), which is a defense-in-depth layer, not the only layer.

common.ts:50-53 confirms the only minor-protection pattern is /\b(my (daughter|son)|teenage|under ?\s?(16|18))\b/i — exclusively third-party phrasing. Broad grep across all of packages/core/src/ for first-person age phrases (i'm 16, i am 17, turning 16, years old, y/o) returned zero results. The gap is real. The sensitive_keyword category maps to sensitive_inbound (types.ts:25), not medical_safety_trigger — the proposed elevation is a valid secondary observation. Not in SHIPPED/IN-FLIGHT/KNOWN BACKLOG. Impact high is credible: minors self-disclosing age in a medspa WhatsApp context carry parental consent obligations in both SG and MY. Effort S is accurate — adding 3-4 regex alternations to a single entry in common.ts plus one test case.

All cited line numbers verified exactly. common.ts: pregnancy (line 8), breastfeeding (line 14), prior_adverse_reaction (line 23), prior_complaint (line 32) each have a negations array. multi_treatment_combo (lines 43-48), sensitive_keyword_minor (lines 50-53), sensitive_keyword_medical_condition (lines 55-60), sensitive_keyword_mental_health (lines 64-70) have NO negations field. Scanner at escalation-trigger-scanner.ts:62 does `if (anyMatches(sentence.text, entry.negations)) { continue; }` and anyMatches returns false when entry.negations is undefined (line 49), so these four entries fire on any keyword match regardless of polarity or subject. Not in SHIPPED or IN-FLIGHT context. Last commit to common.ts was d817a62f (PR #429). One nuance: the mental_health entry has an explicit code comment (lines 61-63) stating "AI should not engage with these in an aesthetic-clinic context" suggesting its lack of negation guard may be intentional — the real value of this fix is for sensitive_keyword_medical_condition (e.g. "I'm NOT diabetic", "my mum had cancer") and multi_treatment_combo (e.g. "I don't want to combine treatments"). Impact medium and effort S are both credible.

dimensions.title: Learning loop, memory & compounding (does Alex get better over time?)
dimensions.laneSummary: The extract->store side of the loop is genuinely wired and live (gateway-bridge -> processConversationEnd -> DeploymentMemory + evidence rows), but the loop does NOT close into improved conversion at pilot scale and proves no lift. The single biggest opportunity: the injection bar is effectively unreachable at pilot volume (default sourceCount>=3) and the pilot-relaxation escape hatch (pilotMode) is wired but set by zero deployments, so learned patterns almost never reach Alex's prompt during a pilot. Compounding this, decay penalizes already-graduated in-use patterns (lastSeenAt tracks writes not injections), nothing reads the persisted WorkTrace.injectedPatternIds to measure lift, extraction fires only on a fragile 30-min in-memory timer that bookings never trigger, and there is no background consolidation despite a comment claiming one.</notesOnShippedQuality>none — but two concrete defects/correctness gaps inside shipped code: (1) inngest.ts:4 documents a 'canonical-merge cron (PR-3.2d)' that was never wired — only dailyPatternDecayCron is registered (inngest.ts:797); the comment is false and would mislead anyone debugging why patterns never consolidate. (2) ConversationLifecycleTracker.dispose() (conversation-lifecycle.ts:107-112) clears timers WITHOUT firing pending conversation-ends, so graceful shutdown silently drops every in-flight session (including booked ones) instead of flushing them to extraction. Neither is a crash, but both cause silent loss of learning signal in shipped behavior.
dimensions.notesOnShippedQuality: Two concrete defects inside shipped lane code: (1) apps/api/src/bootstrap/inngest.ts:4 — header comment claims a 'canonical-merge cron (PR-3.2d)' was wired, but the registered function list (inngest.ts:797) contains only dailyPatternDecayCron; no canonical-merge function exists in production (grep finds none). The comment is false and misleads debugging of why same-key patterns never consolidate. (2) packages/core/src/channel-gateway/conversation-lifecycle.ts:107-112 — dispose() clears all session timers but does NOT fire their conversation-end handlers, so a graceful chat-process shutdown silently drops every in-flight session (including booked conversations awaiting the 30-min timer) rather than flushing them to extraction. Both cause silent loss of learning signal in already-shipped behavior.

findings.title: OUTCOME_PATTERNS effectively never inject at pilot scale: default surfacing bar is sourceCount>=3 and the pilot-relaxation escape hatch is wired but unconfigured on every deployment
findings.currentState: Default (non-pilot) surfacing requires a pattern to reach sourceCount>=3 AND confidence>=0.66 before it is injected: context-builder.ts:135-138 queries listHighConfidence with SURFACING_THRESHOLD.minSourceCount and re-filters via filterSurfaceablePatterns (outcome-pattern-extractor.ts:20-26). SURFACING_THRESHOLD={minSourceCount:3,minConfidence:0.66} (deployment-memory.ts:76). computeConfidenceScore(3,false)=0.5+0.15*ln(3)=0.6648, so sourceCount=3 is the exact minimum that clears both gates. Reaching sourceCount=3 requires the SAME observable pattern to be extracted from THREE separate booking-attributed conversations, each cosine-similar >=0.84 in the same canonicalKey bucket (compounding-service.ts:460-485). The pilot-relaxed bar (sourceCount>=2 & conf>=0.6, OR >=2 distinct booking-ids at sourceCount=1) exists (outcome-pattern-extractor.ts:32-72) but only activates when input.pilotMode is true, resolved from AgentDeployment.inputConfig.outcomePatterns.pilotMode (skill-mode.ts:567, alex.ts:119). Grep confirms NO deployment seed/config/UI sets pilotMode=true anywhere in the repo; the only writer is a raw inputConfig replace in marketplace.ts:312 with no merge and no dashboard surface.
findings.opportunity: The whole compounding loop's INJECTION side is gated so high it almost never fires at the low traffic that defines a pilot. The mechanism designed to fix exactly this (pilotMode) ships dead because no deployment flips it and there is no operator path that round-trips the outcomePatterns namespace. Net effect: Alex's extraction side runs and writes patterns, but those patterns are extremely unlikely to ever reach Alex's prompt during a pilot, so the loop does not visibly improve conversion.
findings.proposedChange: Make pilot-relaxed surfacing the default for pilot deployments without requiring per-deployment config: either (a) default pilotMode to TRUE for the medspa pilot vertical (resolveOutcomePatternsConfig default flip behind the same medspa enum that already special-cases the vertical), or (b) lower the steady-state SURFACING_THRESHOLD.minSourceCount from 3 to 2 (confidence 0.6) until volume justifies tightening — the cross-key-collision and rejection counters already exist to detect mis-calibration. Add a dashboard/marketplace control that merges outcomePatterns.pilotMode into inputConfig rather than replacing the blob, so operators can opt in/out per deployment.
findings.impactRationale: This is the difference between a learning loop that is structurally present and one that actually injects learned closing behavior into live conversations. At pilot volume the current bar is effectively unreachable, so booking-conversion lift from compounding is ~0 until traffic is large — the opposite of the pilot's purpose.
findings.risk: Lowering the bar surfaces less-corroborated patterns (more noise into Alex's prompt). Mitigated because patterns are advisory-only, escaped against injection (outcome-pattern-extractor.ts:80-114), and the cross-key-collision counter flags enum mis-calibration. Flipping a vertical default is one constant; the merge-not-replace UI is additive.

findings.title: Decay keys off last WRITE, not last injection — high-confidence patterns that are actively driving live conversations still decay, and the 'used' signal that would fix it is already persisted and thrown away
findings.currentState: lastSeenAt is written ONLY on create and incrementConfidence (prisma-deployment-memory-store.ts:27,38) — both WRITE paths. The daily decay cron decrements confidence by 0.1 (floor 0.3) for any pattern whose lastSeenAt < now-180d (inngest-functions.ts:38-61; prisma-deployment-memory-store.ts:116-135; windowDays=PATTERN_DECAY_WINDOW_DAYS=180, decayAmount 0.1, floor 0.3 at inngest.ts:297-299). Surfacing/injection (listHighConfidence -> context-builder.ts:146 -> renderOutcomePatternsForContext) does NOT touch lastSeenAt. So 'last seen' means 'last corroborated by a new booking-attributed extraction', not 'last actually used in a conversation'. Meanwhile the genuine usage signal IS captured: every Alex turn persists BuiltContext.injectedPatternIds onto WorkTrace.injectedPatternIds (alex.ts:122 -> skill-mode.ts:105 -> work-trace-recorder.ts:111 -> prisma-work-trace-store.ts:269/536) and is then never read back (grep: no SELECT/where/count over injectedPatternIds anywhere).
findings.opportunity: A pattern that compounding worked hard to graduate (sourceCount>=3, high confidence) and is injected into Alex daily will decay below the surfacing bar after 180 idle-from-WRITES days even while it is in active use — because once a pattern is well-learned it stops generating NEW writes (it just gets surfaced). Decay therefore punishes precisely the patterns the loop already validated. The fix data already exists (injectedPatternIds on WorkTrace); it is computed, persisted, and discarded.
findings.proposedChange: Treat injection as a 'seen' event for decay: when a WorkTrace is finalized with non-empty injectedPatternIds, bump lastSeenAt for those memory rows (a cheap updateMany on id-set), OR have the decay query exclude rows referenced by a WorkTrace.injectedPatternIds in the window. This both stops decaying in-use patterns AND closes the dead-write of injectedPatternIds. It is also the minimal first step toward per-pattern lift (the B1 reader) since it forces a query over injectedPatternIds.
findings.impactRationale: Protects the highest-value, already-graduated closing patterns from silently aging out of Alex's prompt, directly preserving booking-conversion lift the loop already earned. Also converts a currently-dead persisted field into a live signal.
findings.risk: A read-side lastSeenAt bump adds write volume on hot conversation finalize; batch by id-set to keep it one updateMany. Bumping lastSeenAt must NOT increment sourceCount/confidence (usage != corroboration) — keep them distinct so confidence still reflects independent booking evidence only.

findings.title: The proof leg is fully open: WorkTrace.injectedPatternIds is written at finalize but nothing reads it, and the eval that could measure lift runs with patterns empty + ungoverned
findings.currentState: injectedPatternIds flows builder -> ExecutionResult -> WorkTrace and is persisted (alex.ts:145, skill-mode.ts:105, work-trace-recorder.ts:111, prisma-work-trace-store.ts:269). It is deliberately EXCLUDED from the work-trace content hash (work-trace-hash.ts:22), so it is metadata only. Grep for any read of injectedPatternIds with select/where/findMany/count/group/aggregate returns ONLY a test (skill-mode.test.ts:215) — no production reader, no join against Booking, no per-pattern conversion comparison. The alex-conversation eval cannot fill the gap: run-conversation.ts:129 documents 'OUTCOME_PATTERNS (empty — no ContextBuilder wired)' and run-conversation.ts:261 builds the executor with [] hooks, so the eval is structurally blind to whether injected patterns change Alex's behavior.
findings.opportunity: There is no closed-loop feedback that any injected pattern actually raises booking rate; the flywheel 'spins' (extract->store->inject) with no measurement that it converts. Without a reader, operators cannot tell a useful pattern from noise, cannot retire bad patterns, and cannot justify lowering the surfacing bar with data.
findings.proposedChange: Add a per-pattern lift reader: for each pattern id, count finalized WorkTraces whose injectedPatternIds contains it that are booking-attributed (join via the existing DeploymentMemoryEvidence anchor on bookingId, prisma-deployment-memory-evidence-store.ts:40) vs not, and expose injected-conversation conversion rate. This is the B1 measurement the backlog already names, but sharpened by a concrete defect: the input data is ALREADY persisted on every trace and only needs a query — the work is a reader, not new instrumentation.
findings.impactRationale: Turns the learning loop from assert-only to proven, enabling data-driven retirement of noisy patterns and confident relaxation of the surfacing bar — both of which protect booking conversion and prompt-token cost.
findings.risk: Attribution join is contact/window-fuzzy (see fallback tier), so early lift numbers are noisy at pilot volume — present as directional, not authoritative. This sharpens the known volume-gated B1 item rather than re-deriving it.

findings.title: Pattern extraction fires ONLY on a 30-minute in-memory inactivity timer in the chat process — bookings never trigger it, and a chat restart drops every in-flight booked conversation before it is learned
findings.currentState: ConversationCompoundingService.processConversationEnd is invoked exclusively via ConversationLifecycleTracker.onConversationEnd (gateway-bridge.ts:117). The only paths to fireEnd are the 30-min inactivity setTimeout (conversation-lifecycle.ts:53,114-121) and closeConversation() (conversation-lifecycle.ts:96-101). Grep shows closeConversation has ZERO production callers — no booking/won/lost path calls it. Sessions live in a plain in-memory Map (conversation-lifecycle.ts:57) inside the chat process, with no persistence. So a booked conversation is only extracted ~30 min after the customer's LAST message, and if the chat process restarts/deploys in that window, all pending sessions (including the booked ones) are lost and never extracted.
findings.opportunity: The loop preferentially LOSES its most valuable training signal. Booked conversations are exactly the ones pattern extraction depends on, yet they are the most likely to be dropped: a successful booking often ends the chat (customer stops replying), so the 30-min timer is the slowest path, and any deploy during that window discards them. There is no durable queue and no booking-triggered extraction.
findings.proposedChange: Trigger extraction deterministically on the booking signal, not just inactivity: when the crm-write/booking tool succeeds (or on opportunity stage->booked/won), call closeConversation(sessionKey,'won') so the booked transcript is extracted immediately while in memory. Additionally, make session-end durable — emit a persisted 'conversation.ended' event (Inngest) consumed by the compounding worker — so a chat-process restart cannot silently drop pending sessions. At minimum, flush active sessions on graceful shutdown (dispose() currently just clears timers without firing ends, conversation-lifecycle.ts:107-112).
findings.impactRationale: Directly increases the volume and freshness of booked-conversation training signal — the scarce input the entire compounding loop is built on. Fewer dropped booked transcripts means patterns graduate faster and Alex compounds sooner, improving booking conversion over time. Also a reliability fix (data loss on deploy).
findings.risk: Booking-triggered close must dedupe against the inactivity timer (guard on session existence already handles double-close, conversation-lifecycle.ts:97-98). A durable event path is more work; the shutdown-flush is a small safe first step.

findings.title: No consolidation/canonical-merge cron exists despite a code comment claiming one — patterns only merge at write-time, so same-key duplicates each strand below the surfacing bar permanently
findings.currentState: inngest.ts:4 header comment states 'pattern-decay + canonical-merge crons were wired (PR-3.2b/PR-3.2d)', but the registered cron list (inngest.ts:797) contains only dailyPatternDecayCron — there is no canonical-merge function anywhere (grep for canonicalMerge/consolidat/mergeMemor in production code finds nothing in memory). Merging happens ONLY inline in trackPattern at write time: a new pattern merges into an existing same-canonicalKey row only if cosine>=0.84 at the moment of that write (compounding-service.ts:460-485). Two patterns in the same canonicalKey bucket that were each created when no >=0.84 sibling yet existed (or created while embeddings were disabled, since DisabledEmbeddingAdapter.embed THROWS and aborts the whole extraction — disabled-embedding-adapter.ts:14) remain separate rows forever, each accumulating sourceCount independently and each likely stalling below the surfacing bar of 3.
findings.opportunity: Write-time-only merge fragments corroboration: three bookings that all express the same objection-close can land as 2-3 separate rows (sourceCount 1-2 each) and never surface, even though their COMBINED evidence clearly clears the bar. This compounds finding #1 — the surfacing bar is hard to reach partly because corroboration is scattered across un-merged duplicates. There is also a stale, misleading comment asserting a cron that does not exist.
findings.proposedChange: Add the missing background canonical-merge pass: a daily cron that, per (deployment,canonicalKey) bucket, re-embeds and merges rows above OUTCOME_PATTERN_MERGE_THRESHOLD, summing sourceCount and keeping the highest-confidence content — recovering corroboration that write-time merge missed (including rows created during embedding outages). At minimum, fix the inngest.ts:4 comment to stop claiming a cron that was never wired.
findings.impactRationale: Consolidating fragmented corroboration lets validated closing patterns actually reach the surfacing bar and reach Alex, improving booking conversion; it also removes a documentation falsehood that would mislead anyone debugging why patterns never surface.
findings.risk: A merge pass that sums sourceCount can over-credit if the same conversation produced near-duplicate patterns; dedupe by source conversation/evidence bookingId, not just text similarity. Re-embedding all pattern rows daily has cost — scope to buckets with >1 row.

findings.title: Pattern extraction is double-gated on two independent LLM calls agreeing on 'booked', and fallback attribution credits ANY same-contact booking — silently dropping real patterns and admitting noise
findings.currentState: summarize() and extractFacts() run in parallel as two SEPARATE LLM calls (compounding-service.ts:212-215). The fact-extraction prompt asks the model to self-judge the outcome and 'Populate patterns ONLY when the conversation outcome is a booking' (extraction-prompts.ts:55) WITHOUT being told the summarizer's outcome. Pattern processing then requires BOTH that summarization.outcome is booking-shaped AND extraction.patterns is non-empty (compounding-service.ts:244-248). When the two calls disagree (summarizer says 'booked' but the fact-extractor judged the same transcript differently and returned patterns:[]), patterns are silently lost. Separately, when booking attribution can't match a workTraceId, the fallback tier credits ANY booking by the same org+contact within a 24h post-conversation window (booking-attribution.ts:65-76), and Booking/Contact carry no deploymentId (booking-attribution.ts:62-64) — so a website/walk-in booking, or another agent's booking, is attributed to Alex's pattern. Under pilot relaxation, 2 distinct fallback bookings surface a pattern at sourceCount=1 (PILOT_MULTI_BOOKING_MIN_DISTINCT=2, outcome-pattern-extractor.ts:34,64-66).
findings.opportunity: The loop both under-captures (loses patterns when two LLM calls disagree on outcome) and over-credits (admits patterns whose 'booking' Alex may not have caused). Both degrade the signal-to-noise of injected OUTCOME_PATTERNS, the exact question of whether the injected context is useful or noise.
findings.proposedChange: (1) Collapse the outcome decision to one authority: pass summarization.outcome (or the booking-attribution tier) INTO the fact-extraction step, or extract patterns in a single call that is told the outcome, so the 'booked' judgment isn't litigated twice. (2) Tighten fallback attribution: require the fallback booking to share the conversation's contact AND not be already attributed to another deployment's WorkTrace; prefer strong (workTraceId) tier and treat fallback as evidence-tier-only (don't let fallback-only patterns surface at sourceCount=1 even under pilot). (3) When summarizer says booked but extractor returns no patterns, log/metric the disagreement so the prompt bug is visible.
findings.impactRationale: Higher-precision patterns mean the injected OUTCOME_PATTERNS block is trustworthy closing advice rather than noise, protecting booking conversion (good patterns surface) and Alex's credibility/safety (it isn't coached by conversations it didn't win).
findings.risk: Single-call extraction-with-outcome slightly increases prompt size per extraction; cost is offline (post-conversation), not on the live reply path. Tightening fallback reduces pattern volume at pilot scale — acceptable given the precision gain and finding #1's separate volume fix.

verdicts.notes:
All cited evidence verified against live main-branch code:

1. packages/schemas/src/deployment-memory.ts:76 — SURFACING_THRESHOLD = { minSourceCount: 3, minConfidence: 0.66 } confirmed exactly as stated. computeConfidenceScore(3,false) = min(0.95, 0.5 + 0.15*ln(3)) ≈ 0.6648 per line 72 formula — sourceCount=3 is the exact minimum that barely clears.

2. packages/core/src/memory/context-builder.ts:135-138 — Default non-pilot path uses SURFACING_THRESHOLD.minSourceCount (3) and SURFACING_THRESHOLD.minConfidence (0.66) for listHighConfidence query; pilot path uses PILOT_SURFACING_MIN_CONFIDENCE and minSourceCount=1 — confirmed.

3. packages/core/src/memory/context-builder.ts:214-216 — filterSurfaceablePatterns (high bar) vs filterPilotModeSurfaceable (relaxed bar) branching on input.pilotMode — confirmed.

4. packages/core/src/memory/outcome-pattern-extractor.ts:20-26 — filterSurfaceablePatterns enforces sourceCount>=3 AND confidence>=0.66 — confirmed.

5. packages/core/src/memory/outcome-pattern-extractor.ts:40-72 — pilot escape hatch (sourceCount>=2 & conf>=0.6, or >=2 distinct booking-ids) exists and is functional — confirmed.

6. packages/schemas/src/outcome-patterns-config.ts:12-14 — pilotMode defaults to false in OutcomePatternsConfigSchema — confirmed.

7. apps/api/src/bootstrap/skill-mode.ts:567 — resolveOutcomePatternsConfig(ctx.deployment.inputConfig ?? null) with no setter anywhere that provides pilotMode=true — confirmed.

8. No seed sets pilotMode=true: seed-marketplace.ts Alex deployment (lines 639-691) has inputConfig with businessName/tone/bookingLink/qualificationCriteria etc — NO outcomePatterns key. Full grep of non-worktree, non-test TS files finds no pilotMode=true assignment outside tests.

9. apps/api/src/routes/marketplace.ts:312 — PATCH /deployments/:id does store.update(orgId, id, { inputConfig }) — raw full-object replace confirmed. No merge logic present.

10. packages/core/src/memory/compounding-service.ts:460-485 — same-canonicalKey-bucket + cosine >=OUTCOME_PATTERN_MERGE_THRESHOLD (0.84 from schemas:94) required to increment sourceCount — confirmed.

Conclusion: the finding is accurate. pilotMode is permanently false on every deployment in the codebase. The pilot-relaxed surfacing path (PR-3.2e) is fully implemented but unconfigured anywhere. Impact corrected to medium: this is a configuration gap on a feature that only matters after bookings accumulate; the learning loop is delayed but not permanently broken — once any deployment manually patches outcomePatterns.pilotMode=true via the API it works. Effort S confirmed: flipping the default or adding pilotMode:true to the medspa seed is 1–5 lines.

All cited evidence verified against live code on main:

1. `lastSeenAt` written ONLY on `create` (prisma-deployment-memory-store.ts:27) and `incrementConfidence` (prisma-deployment-memory-store.ts:38). Both are corroboration/write paths only.

2. `decayStale` at prisma-deployment-memory-store.ts:116-135 gates on `lastSeenAt: { lt: input.cutoffDate }` with `cutoffDate = now - 180 days` (PATTERN_DECAY_WINDOW_DAYS=180 at packages/schemas/src/deployment-memory.ts:83), decrement 0.1, floor 0.3 (apps/api/src/bootstrap/inngest.ts:298-299). Confirmed exactly as claimed.

3. Context injection path at context-builder.ts:146 calls `listHighConfidence`, then `renderOutcomePatternsForContext` (line 217-218) returns `injectedPatternIds` — no `lastSeenAt` update occurs here. Confirmed.

4. `injectedPatternIds` flows: alex.ts:122 captures it → skill-mode.ts:105 attaches to ExecutionResult → work-trace-recorder.ts:111 stores it in WorkTrace → prisma-work-trace-store.ts:269/536 persists to DB.

5. A comprehensive grep for any code that reads `injectedPatternIds` back from WorkTrace to update DeploymentMemory `lastSeenAt` returns zero results (outside test/persistence code). No `bumpLastSeen`, `touchLastSeen`, `markUsed`, or similar function exists anywhere in packages/.

6. The `injectedPatternIds` field is explicitly marked "analytics-only" in the hash exclusion test (work-trace-hash.test.ts:71-73) — no feedback loop to memory is wired.

The bug is real and novel: high-confidence patterns injected every conversation will decay after 180 days of no new bookings attributed to them, even if they were used in hundreds of conversations. The injectedPatternIds dead-write is confirmed. No in-flight PR or shipped work addresses this (not in the SHIPPED/IN-FLIGHT context).

All cited evidence checks out exactly:

1. packages/core/src/platform/work-trace-recorder.ts:111 — `injectedPatternIds: executionResult?.injectedPatternIds ?? []` — confirmed write path.

2. packages/core/src/platform/work-trace-hash.ts:22 — `"injectedPatternIds"` is in EXCLUDED_BASE with comment "analytics-only metadata … persisted … for per-pattern conversion-lift queries, but not part of trace integrity." Confirmed excluded from hash.

3. packages/db/src/stores/prisma-work-trace-store.ts:269 — `injectedPatternIds: trace.injectedPatternIds ?? []` — confirmed persistence on every finalize.

4. evals/alex-conversation/run-conversation.ts:129 — comment reads exactly "OUTCOME_PATTERNS (empty — no ContextBuilder wired)" — confirmed eval runs without pattern injection.

5. evals/alex-conversation/run-conversation.ts:261 — `new SkillExecutorImpl(adapter, tools, undefined, [])` — comment "No router (undefined) and NO hooks ([])"; executor is structurally blind to patterns. Confirmed.

6. Exhaustive grep for any production reader using injectedPatternIds with select/where/findMany/count/groupBy/aggregate returns zero results. The only non-test appearances are write paths (recorder, store persist, platform-ingress, platform-lifecycle, skill-mode) and type declarations.

7. prisma-deployment-memory-evidence-store.ts:40 — `countDistinctBookingIds` is the only query; it does not join to WorkTrace.injectedPatternIds.

The work-trace-hash.ts comment at line 18 explicitly anticipates a future "per-pattern conversion-lift queries" reader — the field was designed for it — but no such reader exists anywhere in production or any open worktree. The finding is real, novel (not shipped, not in the in-flight PRs listed in context), and the evidence is precise.

All cited evidence verified against main-branch source:

1. conversation-lifecycle.ts:53 — DEFAULT_INACTIVITY_TIMEOUT_MS = 30 * 60 * 1000 (30 min). Exact match.
2. conversation-lifecycle.ts:57 — `private sessions = new Map<string, ActiveSession>()`. Confirmed plain in-memory Map.
3. conversation-lifecycle.ts:96-101 — closeConversation() exists and works correctly but has zero production callers. Grep across entire repo: all callers are in test files (packages/core/src/__tests__/conversation-lifecycle.test.ts). The lifecycleTracker is a closure-local variable inside createGatewayBridge() (gateway-bridge.ts:116), so no outside code can reach it.
4. conversation-lifecycle.ts:107-112 — dispose() clears timers via clearTimeout and calls sessions.clear() with no fireEnd() call. Confirmed: booked in-flight sessions are silently dropped on shutdown.
5. gateway-bridge.ts:117 — onConversationEnd wired to compoundingService.processConversationEnd. Confirmed.
6. calendar-book.ts:199 — bookingStore.create() is called but has no conversationThreadId in scope and does not call closeConversation. The booking success writes an outbox event of type "booked" but does not trigger extraction.
7. apps/api/src/bootstrap/lifecycle.ts:122-128 — code comment explicitly says "DEFERRED wiring. The only bookingStore.create seat lives at packages/core/src/skill-runtime/tools/calendar-book.ts:187 which does NOT have conversationThreadId in scope." The onBookingCreated hook is registered but fires only a LifecycleWriter transition (to state "booked") — it does not call closeConversation or trigger pattern extraction.
8. apps/chat/src/main.ts shutdown handler (lines 421-425) — only calls stopHealthChecker() and app.close(). No dispose(), no flush of active sessions, no Inngest event emitted.
9. No conversation.ended or persisted session-end event type exists anywhere in the codebase.

All three parts of the claim hold: (a) extraction fires only on 30-min inactivity, (b) closeConversation has zero production callers so bookings never trigger it, (c) process restart silently drops all pending sessions. Finding is not in the shipped or in-flight lists.

All four cited locations verified:

1. apps/api/src/bootstrap/inngest.ts:4 — header comment reads "pattern-decay + canonical-merge crons were wired (PR-3.2b/PR-3.2d)". Comment is factually wrong: PR-3.2b is the write-time two-stage merge inside trackPattern, and PR-3.2d is the decay cron — neither is a background canonical-merge cron.

2. apps/api/src/bootstrap/inngest.ts:797 — registered Inngest functions list contains only `dailyPatternDecayCron` for memory; no canonical-merge function is registered. Full grep of packages/core/src/memory/, apps/api/src/bootstrap/, and the broader codebase finds zero implementations of canonicalMerge, consolidat, or mergeMemor in production code (outside worktrees).

3. packages/core/src/memory/compounding-service.ts:460-485 — trackPattern does the cosine-gated merge at write-time only: line 456 embeds the new pattern, lines 460-486 query the same-canonicalKey bucket and merge only if cosine >= OUTCOME_PATTERN_MERGE_THRESHOLD (0.84, per packages/schemas/src/deployment-memory.ts:94). If no same-bucket entry meets the threshold, a new row is created (line 508).

4. packages/core/src/llm/disabled-embedding-adapter.ts:14 — embed() throws EmbeddingsUnavailableError unconditionally. However, the claim that "rows created during embedding outages remain separate rows forever" is slightly wrong: the throw propagates through trackPattern (line 456 is before any write), and the caller catches it at compounding-service.ts:306-308 with console.error and continues — so when embeddings are disabled, patterns are NOT created at all, not created as separate rows. This sub-claim is inaccurate but does not undermine the main structural gap.

The core finding is correct: two patterns assigned the same canonicalKey at different write times, where cosine was < 0.84 at each respective write (possible if patterns are semantically adjacent but not highly similar), accumulate as separate rows each with sourceCount=1, never merging, both stalling permanently below the surfacing bar of minSourceCount=3 (packages/schemas/src/deployment-memory.ts:76). No background pass exists to correct this. The PR-3.2 plan (docs/superpowers/plans/archive/2026-05-14-agent-infra-pr3.2.md) never included a background merge cron — this gap was never designed or shipped.

Impact/effort assessment: medium impact and M effort are credible. The problem is structural (write-time-only merge) but affects only patterns that happen to land below the 0.84 cosine floor at separate write times — not every pattern. A daily merge cron is bounded in scope.

All five cited evidence points hold exactly as described.

1. Double parallel LLM calls: compounding-service.ts:212-215 — `Promise.all([this.summarize(...), this.extractFacts(...)])` confirmed. Two independent LLM calls run concurrently; neither receives the other's output.

2. Extractor self-judges outcome: extraction-prompts.ts:55 — "Populate 'patterns' ONLY when the conversation outcome is a booking" is in the extractor prompt verbatim. The `buildFactExtractionPrompt` function receives only the raw transcript (line 21-63); `summarization.outcome` is never passed in.

3. Silent pattern loss on disagreement: compounding-service.ts:244-248 — gate is `shouldExtractOutcomePatterns(summarization.outcome) && extraction.patterns?.length`. When summarizer says "booked" but the extractor independently judged otherwise and returned `patterns:[]`, patterns are lost with no log/metric. No observability for this disagreement exists in the code.

4. Fallback attribution lacks deploymentId scoping: booking-attribution.ts:60-64 — comment at line 62-64 explicitly reads "Per-deployment scoping is not enforced: neither Booking nor Contact carries a deploymentId column today." The `findInWindow` call at line 67-72 queries only by `organizationId` + `contactId` + time window, no deployment filter.

5. Pilot evidence path surfaces at sourceCount=1: outcome-pattern-extractor.ts:34 (`PILOT_MULTI_BOOKING_MIN_DISTINCT = 2`) and lines 64-66 — `countDistinctBookingIds` has no tier filter; two fallback-tier bookings from a walk-in or another agent satisfy the `>= 2` check and surface a pattern even at sourceCount=1. context-builder.ts:138 confirms `memoryMinSourceCount = 1` in pilot mode, widening the candidate set to include sourceCount=1 entries.

Not in shipped or in-flight context. The proposed fixes (pass summarizer outcome into extractor, tighten fallback scoping via schema migration, add disagreement logging) are all novel work. Impact medium / effort M are credible — the double-gating is a correctness gap but only fires on LLM disagreement; the fallback noise requires a schema migration to fully fix, making it genuinely M effort.

dimensions.title: Tool surface, reliability & missing capabilities
dimensions.laneSummary: Alex's tool surface is small and trust-hardened (factory-with-context closes the AI-1 vector; booking has a DB-level duplicate guard; reinjection truncation/omission and sentinel-wrapping are solid), but it is missing the single most book-moving capability — reschedule/cancel — even though the CalendarProvider implements both methods end-to-end. The biggest opportunity is closing that gap: the provider, the Booking model, and the outbox pattern are all ready; only a new tool operation (plus one small BookingStore.findUpcomingByContact method) stands between Alex and self-serve appointment changes, which directly protect show-rate and cut human-escalation load.
dimensions.notesOnShippedQuality: Three concrete defects inside already-shipped code: (1) escalate.ts:65-72 calls the HandoffPackageAssembler with messages:[] and hardcoded empty snapshots, so the entire topic/objection/sentiment/suggested-opening engine (package-assembler.ts:43-58) is inert and every human handoff is context-free — sentiment is even hardcoded to 'neutral' on 'negative_sentiment' escalations. (2) crm-write.stage.update (crm-write.ts:67) returns the full mapped Opportunity row — contactId, estimatedValue, revenueTotal, assignedStaff, notes (prisma-opportunity-store.ts:348-370) — into model reinjection, defeating the pii.ts allow-list that hides contactId/phone/email everywhere else. (3) booking.create can orphan a real calendar event when the post-provider confirmation transaction fails: failureHandler marks the booking 'failed' and escalates but never cancels the already-created event (calendar-book.ts:300-314), despite provider.cancelBooking being available. Separately, the `idempotent: true` flag on tool operations (types.ts:203) is declared but never read by the executor — booking is protected only by its DB unique constraint, not by any runtime idempotency layer; this is acceptable today but the flag is decorative. Note: web-scanner.ts is NOT in Alex's live tool set (it belongs to the website-profiler skill, skills/website-profiler.md:29), so its operations were excluded from this lane.

findings.title: No reschedule/cancel tool — every appointment change is force-escalated despite a fully-ready provider
findings.currentState: Alex's calendar-book tool exposes only two operations: slots.query and booking.create (packages/core/src/skill-runtime/tools/calendar-book.ts:118-142). There is NO booking.reschedule or booking.cancel operation anywhere in the skill-runtime (grep confirms zero hits). When a lead says 'can I move my appt to next week' or 'I need to cancel', Alex has no tool — it must escalate to a human (SKILL.md Bucket C) or it can only describe the reschedulePolicy text that gets injected into BUSINESS_FACTS (context-resolver.ts:56).
evidence: skills/alex/SKILL.md:48-54 (Alex tools list has no reschedule/cancel)
packages/schemas/src/calendar.ts:95-96 (CalendarProvider.cancelBooking + rescheduleBooking already in the interface)
findings.opportunity: Reschedules are show-rate gold: a lead who wants to move a time is still a booking — but with no tool, Alex hands it to a human, and an unanswered reschedule request becomes a no-show or a churned lead. Cancels handled by Alex free the slot (re-bookable) and trigger the failure/outbox path cleanly. The provider, the Booking model (rescheduleCount/rescheduledAt fields already exist, google-calendar-adapter.ts:100-101), the outbox pattern, and the duplicate guard are ALL already built — this is the single highest-leverage capability the runtime is most ready for.
findings.proposedChange: Add booking.reschedule (effectCategory external_mutation) and booking.cancel (external_mutation) operations to the calendar-book tool, wired to provider.rescheduleBooking/cancelBooking. Resolve the target booking from the trusted ctx.contactId (NOT LLM input) via a new BookingStore.findUpcomingByContact(orgId, contactId) method (today the store only has findById + findBySlot — prisma-booking-store.ts:53-67, so one small store method is needed). Emit a 'booking.rescheduled'/'booking.cancelled' outbox event in the same transaction as the status flip, mirroring booking.create. Reuse failureHandler for provider errors. Then add a Phase-5 'Reschedule / Cancel' section to SKILL.md.
findings.impactRationale: Directly protects booking conversion and show-rate (a saved reschedule is a kept appointment) and cuts human-escalation load on a routine, fully-automatable action. The most book-moving capability where the runtime is already 100% ready end-to-end.
findings.risk: Reschedule/cancel are external_mutation — must route through the same governance/approval posture as booking.create and never act on an LLM-supplied bookingId (resolve from trusted contact). Need to handle 'which booking' ambiguity when a contact has multiple upcoming bookings (clarify, don't guess).

findings.title: Every human handoff is blind — the rich handoff-summary engine is fed an empty messages array
findings.currentState: The escalate tool calls assembler.assemble({ leadSnapshot: { channel: "whatsapp" }, qualificationSnapshot: { signalsCaptured: {}, qualificationStage: "unknown" }, messages: [] }) (packages/core/src/skill-runtime/tools/escalate.ts:65-72). HandoffPackageAssembler.buildSummary() derives keyTopics, objectionHistory, sentiment, and a tailored suggestedOpening FROM the messages (package-assembler.ts:43-58) — but with messages:[] every handoff gets turnCount:0, keyTopics:[], objectionHistory:[], sentiment hardcoded to 'neutral', and the generic fallback opener 'Hi! Thanks for your interest...'. leadSnapshot has no lead name; qualificationSnapshot is empty.
evidence: packages/core/src/handoff/package-assembler.ts:104-115 (suggestOpening returns generic fallback with no topics)
packages/core/src/skill-runtime/skill-executor.ts:268-296 (the executor HAS both the conversation messages and the parsed qualification sidecar — neither is threaded to the escalate tool)
findings.opportunity: Escalations are the hardest, highest-value leads — frustrated customers (reason negative_sentiment), complex objections, out-of-scope pricing. The human picks them up with ZERO context: a 'negative_sentiment' handoff literally arrives tagged sentiment:'neutral' with a cheery generic opener. The entire purpose of the assembler (a warm, informed human start) is defeated, which lengthens handle time and loses conversions on exactly the leads where a good handoff matters most.
findings.proposedChange: Thread the conversation context into the escalate tool so the assembler can do its job. The executor already holds params.messages and the validated qualification sidecar payload; pass them through SkillRequestContext (or a message-provider closure on the escalate factory) so escalate.handoff.create calls assemble({ messages: <real transcript>, qualificationSnapshot: <from sidecar signals>, leadSnapshot: { channel, name: ctx contact name } }). Even passing the last N user/assistant turns lights up keyTopics/objections/sentiment/suggestedOpening.
findings.impactRationale: Improves conversion recovery on escalated (highest-intent, highest-risk) leads and shortens human response time — it activates already-built infrastructure that is currently inert.
findings.risk: Handoff messages may carry PII; the handoff record is operator-facing (not model-facing) so the channel PII-minimization rules differ — confirm the handoff store/notifier path is allowed to hold raw transcript, and redact phone/email in the assembled summary if it surfaces in operator notifications.

findings.title: crm-write.stage.update leaks the full opportunity row (contactId, financials, notes) back into the model context
findings.currentState: stage.update returns ok(result) where result is the full mapped Opportunity row (crm-write.ts:67-71). PrismaOpportunityStore.updateStage returns mapRowToOpportunity(row) which includes contactId, estimatedValue, revenueTotal, assignedStaff, lostReason, and free-text notes (prisma-opportunity-store.ts:348-370). That whole object is serialized and re-injected to the model by filterForReinjection (skill-executor.ts:435-449). This directly defeats the PII allow-list: crm-query.contact.get deliberately strips contactId/phone/email via sanitizeContactForPrompt (pii.ts:5-9, crm-query.ts:36), yet a stage.update hands the model contactId plus internal financials and operator notes.
evidence: packages/db/src/stores/prisma-opportunity-store.ts:348-370 (mapRowToOpportunity exposes contactId, estimatedValue, revenueTotal, assignedStaff, lostReason, notes)
packages/core/src/skill-runtime/pii.ts:1-9 (the allow-list that this bypasses — contactId/phone/email intentionally hidden)
packages/core/src/skill-runtime/skill-executor.ts:435-449 (tool result is re-injected to the model)
findings.opportunity: The model echoes what it can see. After a stage.update the model holds the contact's internal notes ('budget tight, ghosted twice'), estimatedValue/revenueTotal, and assigned-staff name — any of which it can leak to the customer mid-conversation, and contactId re-entering the context erodes the trust-bound-ID isolation the rest of the runtime carefully maintains. It is a quiet compliance/PII regression riding on a write the agent makes on nearly every qualified lead.
findings.proposedChange: Make stage.update return only the entityState it already sets — ok(undefined, { entityState: { opportunityId, stage } }) — or a tight projection { id, stage }. The stage transition is the only fact the model needs; the full row should never be reinjected. Mirrors how activity.log already returns ok(undefined, ...).
findings.impactRationale: Closes a PII/financial-leak path that undermines safety/compliance and the channel PII-minimization invariant, on a high-frequency write — low effort, removes model-visible internal data.
findings.risk: Confirm no downstream consumer of the tool result depends on the full row (only the model reinjection consumes it today; the DB write is unaffected).

findings.title: booking.create inserts an LLM-supplied slot with no server-side availability re-check (over-booking risk)
findings.currentState: booking.create takes slotStart/slotEnd/calendarId as free LLM input (calendar-book.ts:147-163) and passes them to provider.createBooking with available:true hardcoded (calendar-book.ts:239-243). GoogleCalendarAdapter.createBooking calls events.insert blindly at those times with no freebusy re-check (google-calendar-adapter.ts:74-84). The only guard is the DB unique constraint on (org, contact, service, startsAt) (schema.prisma @@unique) which catches the SAME contact double-booking the SAME slot — it does NOT prevent booking a stale/already-taken slot for a different contact, or a time the model fabricated that was never in the offered set.
evidence: skills/alex/SKILL.md:214-218 (slot-selection rules are PROSE-only — the model is trusted to pick a valid offered slot)
findings.opportunity: Between slots.query and the customer choosing, another lead can take the slot, or the model can mis-map an ambiguous reply ('the later one') to a wrong/occupied time. Google events.insert does not refuse overlaps, so this silently double-books the clinic — a real-world ops failure (two leads, one chair) and a trust hit when staff discover the clash. The slot-selection safety today is entirely prose in the prompt with no deterministic backstop.
findings.proposedChange: Before insert, have booking.create re-query the provider's available slots for that day (or call a provider hasConflict(start,end) check) and reject with a structured SLOT_NO_LONGER_AVAILABLE result + modelRemediation ('that time was just taken, here are fresh options') when the requested window is not currently free. Cheapest version: re-run listAvailableSlots for the chosen day and confirm the exact start is present before insert.
findings.impactRationale: Prevents double-bookings (live reliability + clinic trust) and converts a silent failure into a graceful re-offer that keeps the booking flow alive instead of producing a conflicting invite.
findings.risk: Adds one extra provider read on the booking hot path (latency); a TOCTOU window still exists but is dramatically narrowed. Must not block on transient provider errors — degrade to current behavior rather than failing a ready-to-book lead.

findings.title: Self-serve bookingLink is resolved into PERSONA_CONFIG but never referenced in the Alex prompt (dead fallback)
findings.currentState: alexBuilder resolves ctx.persona.bookingLink into parameters.PERSONA_CONFIG.bookingLink (builders/alex.ts:136), but skills/alex/SKILL.md never interpolates {{PERSONA_CONFIG.bookingLink}} anywhere (grep confirms the only SKILL references are qualificationCriteria/disqualificationCriteria/escalationRules/tone/customInstructions). So the booking link is threaded all the way to the template and then dropped. The legacy sales-pipeline/role-prompts.ts (line 49) and agent-runtime/system-prompt-assembler.ts (line 86) DO use it, but those are different runtimes — the live SkillMode/Alex path does not.
evidence: skills/alex/SKILL.md (no {{PERSONA_CONFIG.bookingLink}} reference anywhere in the body)
packages/core/src/sales-pipeline/role-prompts.ts:49 (proof the field is meant to be a first-class prompt token in the legacy runtime)
findings.opportunity: When the calendar tool is not configured (isCalendarProviderConfigured false → CALENDAR_NOT_CONFIGURED, calendar-book.ts:96-104) or slots.query fails, Alex's only move today is 'I'm having trouble checking availability, let me have someone reach out' (SKILL.md:228-230) — a dead end that loses a ready-to-book lead until a human appears. A configured self-serve booking link is a zero-cost conversion safety net for exactly that failure, and it is already plumbed to the prompt.
findings.proposedChange: Reference {{PERSONA_CONFIG.bookingLink}} in the SKILL.md Book phase as the explicit fallback when the calendar tool returns CALENDAR_NOT_CONFIGURED or slots.query fails: 'If a booking link is configured, share it so they can self-book; otherwise note the failed attempt and escalate.' Keep it conditional so clinics without a link are unaffected.
findings.impactRationale: Recovers otherwise-lost bookings when the calendar integration is absent or erroring — a live-reliability conversion backstop using a field already resolved at zero added cost.
findings.risk: Must gate on a non-empty link (default is empty string) so Alex never emits a blank/garbage URL; ensure the link isn't offered when the calendar tool succeeds (avoid undercutting the in-chat booking flow).

findings.title: booking.create can leave an orphaned calendar invite when the confirm-transaction fails after the provider succeeded
findings.currentState: booking.create does: (1) persist pending booking, (2) provider.createBooking — a REAL Google event is inserted (calendar-book.ts:235-249), (3) confirm booking + write outbox in one tx (calendar-book.ts:267-299). If step 3's transaction throws, the catch calls failureHandler.handle({ failureType: 'confirmation_failed' }) which marks the booking 'failed' and opens an escalation (booking-failure-handler.ts:83-132) — but it NEVER cancels the calendar event created in step 2. The known calendarResult.calendarEventId (calendar-book.ts:233) is discarded.
evidence: packages/core/src/skill-runtime/tools/calendar-book.ts:300-314 (on confirm-tx failure → failureHandler, no event cleanup)
packages/core/src/calendar/google-calendar-adapter.ts:110-112 (cancelBooking exists and would clean it up)
findings.opportunity: On this failure the customer receives a real Google Calendar invite (the event exists), but Alex tells them 'I couldn't complete the booking, a human will follow up' (booking-failure-handler.ts SAFE_MESSAGE) and the DB booking is 'failed'. The result is a ghost appointment on the clinic calendar that no one owns — a confusing double-state that can cause a staffed slot for a lead the system believes did not book. It is an edge case but a genuine consistency defect in shipped booking code.
findings.proposedChange: In the confirmation-tx catch branch, when calendarResult.calendarEventId is present, best-effort call provider.cancelBooking(calendarEventId, 'confirmation_failed') before/within failureHandler so the orphaned event is removed (or flag it on the escalation so the human knows an invite exists). Log-and-swallow the cancel error so cleanup failure does not mask the original failure.
findings.impactRationale: Removes a live data-consistency hazard (ghost calendar events vs 'failed' bookings) that erodes clinic trust in the agent and can cause mis-staffed slots — reliability fix using an already-implemented provider method.
findings.risk: Compensating cancel is itself a network call that can fail; treat as best-effort and never let it throw out of the failure path. Confirm cancelBooking takes the calendar event id (it does — events.delete by eventId).

verdicts.notes: All cited evidence verified against live code. calendar-book.ts:118-142 has exactly two operations ("slots.query" and "booking.create") — no booking.reschedule or booking.cancel anywhere in the tools directory. SKILL.md:48-53 lists five tools (crm-query, crm-write, calendar-book, escalate, delegate) with no reschedule/cancel entry. CalendarProvider interface at calendar.ts:95-96 declares both cancelBooking and rescheduleBooking. GoogleCalendarAdapter implements cancelBooking (events.delete, line 110) and rescheduleBooking (events.patch, line 114). LocalCalendarProvider implements both at lines 150-154. PrismaBookingStore at prisma-booking-store.ts:53-67 has findById and findBySlot but no findUpcomingByContact — confirming the proposed new store method is genuinely absent. context-resolver.ts:55-56 injects cancellationPolicy and reschedulePolicy as plain text strings into BUSINESS_FACTS, so Alex can only describe the policy, not act on it. No in-flight branches or recent commits touch reschedule/cancel tooling. Not in the known-shipped or in-flight context. Finding is novel and the gap is real.

All cited code matches exactly. escalate.ts:65-72 passes messages:[], leadSnapshot:{channel:"whatsapp"} (no name), qualificationSnapshot:{signalsCaptured:{},qualificationStage:"unknown"} to assembler.assemble(). package-assembler.ts:43-58 derives turnCount/keyTopics/objectionHistory/sentiment/suggestedOpening entirely from messages — empty array yields all-empty/neutral/generic fallback (line 97: texts.length===0 returns "neutral"; line 114: generic opener). skill-executor.ts:183-186 has params.messages available as a real LLMMessage[] array but it is never threaded into the escalate factory. SkillRequestContext (types.ts:372-387) has no messages field. The gap is real and unaddressed — git log shows escalate.ts last touched in the initial wiring commit (96c9395b), not in any SHIPPED or IN-FLIGHT work. Not covered by conversion-polish #794, medical red-flag #791, or any other in-flight item. Impact high is correct — every escalation loses all conversation context that the assembler is designed to use. Effort M is credible (add messages to SkillRequestContext or pass via factory closure; no migration required).

All four cited evidence points check out exactly.

1. crm-write.ts:67-71: `stage.update` calls `opportunityStore.updateStage(ctx.orgId, opportunityId, stage)` and passes the result directly into `ok(result as Record<string, unknown>, { entityState: { opportunityId, stage } })`. The full store return value lands in `data`.

2. prisma-opportunity-store.ts:117-136 + 348-374: `updateStage` does an `updateMany` then a `findFirstOrThrow` and returns `mapRowToOpportunity(row)`, which includes `contactId` (line 351), `estimatedValue` (364), `revenueTotal` (365), `assignedStaff` (367), `lostReason` (368), `notes` (369).

3. pii.ts:1-9: The allow-list (`sanitizeContactForPrompt`) is only called in crm-query.ts:36 for `contact.get`. There is no equivalent sanitizer applied to the write-path result; the PII protection is purely opt-in at the query call site.

4. reinjection-filter.ts + skill-executor.ts:435-449: `filterForReinjection` receives `op ?? FALLBACK_READ_OP`. The `stage.update` operation definition (crm-write.ts:38-71) sets no `summarizeForModel` or `resultClass`, so the filter classifies the return as "structured" (non-empty object, no multi-element array). The full Opportunity JSON (~400-500 chars) fits well within the 2000-char `DEFAULT_REINJECTION_POLICY.maxToolResultChars`, so it passes verbatim.

5. Contrast with `activity.log` (crm-write.ts:99): returns `ok(undefined, { entityState: ... })` — `data` is undefined — so `Object.keys(data).length === 0` → classified scalar → passes only `{ status, entityState }`, zero row data reinjected. That is the pattern the finding recommends applying to `stage.update`.

No evidence this is shipped or in-flight: the context list includes PII minimization as shipped but the specific `stage.update` data bypass is not mentioned. Impact/effort assessment (medium/S) is credible: the fix is a one-line change (`ok(result...)` → `ok(undefined, ...)`), but the PII bypass is a real semantic gap in the allow-list design.

All four cited evidence points verified against live code on main:

1. calendar-book.ts:147-163 — `slotStart`, `slotEnd`, `calendarId` are free-form string inputs from the LLM with zero server-side range/set validation.

2. calendar-book.ts:239-243 — `available: true` is hardcoded when calling `provider.createBooking`. No re-query of listAvailableSlots occurs before the insert.

3. google-calendar-adapter.ts:74-84 — `createBooking` calls `this.client.events.insert(...)` directly with no freebusy pre-check. The freebusy API (`this.client.freebusy.query`) is only used in `listAvailableSlots` (lines 52-71).

4. SKILL.md:214-218 — slot-selection rules are prose-only ("If reply is a single digit 1-5 matching an offered slot, select that slot"). No runtime enforcement that the selected slot came from the offered set.

5. schema.prisma:1924 — the DB unique constraint is `@@unique([organizationId, contactId, service, startsAt])`, which guards same-contact same-slot duplicates only. A different contact booking the same time window, or the model supplying a fabricated or stale timestamp, bypasses this entirely.

`LocalCalendarProvider.createBooking` (local-calendar-provider.ts:87-103) also has no pre-insert slot check; it relies on `createInTransaction` to enforce uniqueness at the DB level, but GoogleCalendarAdapter has no such guard — `events.insert` into Google Calendar succeeds silently even if the slot is occupied.

The finding is not in the shipped list, not in any of the three in-flight PRs (#794 conversion-polish, #791 medical red-flag, #782 WorkTrace invariant), and not in the known backlog. Impact medium / effort M are credible: the fix (re-query listAvailableSlots for the chosen day and confirm the start is present before insert) is self-contained within calendar-book.ts with no schema changes needed.

All three evidence points check out. (1) alex.ts:136 puts `bookingLink: ctx.persona.bookingLink ?? ""` into PERSONA_CONFIG — confirmed. (2) skills/alex/SKILL.md: the PERSONA_CONFIG schema block (lines 31-39) does not list `bookingLink` as a declared parameter field, and a grep for `PERSONA_CONFIG.bookingLink` in SKILL.md returns nothing — confirmed. (3) The Phase 4: Book failure handlers (SKILL.md:228-237) tell Alex to say "let me have someone reach out" with no mention of a self-serve link. (4) skills/sales-pipeline.md:44 declares `bookingLink: { type: string, required: false }` in its schema AND uses `{{PERSONA_CONFIG.bookingLink}}` at line 123 — demonstrating the live Alex path is missing what the sibling skill already has. (5) role-prompts.ts:49 shows `BOOKING LINK: {bookingLink}` in the legacy runtime — confirmed. (6) system-prompt-assembler.ts:86-92 conditionally builds a `## Booking` section from `persona.bookingLink` in the legacy assembler. Collectively: the field is resolved, placed in the parameter bag, but the Alex SKILL.md schema does not declare it and the prompt body never interpolates it — it is dropped before the LLM sees it. Impact is medium (medspa clinics may have a bookingLink configured; when calendar-book fails, Alex gives a vague escalation instead of a self-serve link). Effort is S (add one conditional sentence to Phase 4 failure text and add bookingLink to the SKILL.md schema block). Novelty is confirmed: not in any SHIPPED or IN-FLIGHT item listed.

All cited evidence verified line-by-line:

1. calendar-book.ts:233 — `calendarResult` declared as `{ calendarEventId?: string | null }`.
2. calendar-book.ts:235-249 — `provider.createBooking(...)` called; real Google Calendar event inserted; result stored in `calendarResult`.
3. calendar-book.ts:300-314 — catch block calls `deps.failureHandler.handle({ ..., failureType: "confirmation_failed" })` only. No call to `provider.cancelBooking`. Both `provider` (line 180, closure-local) and `calendarResult` (line 233, closure-local) ARE in scope here, so the fix is unobstructed.
4. booking-failure-handler.ts:83-132 — `handle()` marks booking `status: "failed"`, creates escalation with metadata `{ bookingId, provider, failureType, retryable, service }` — no `calendarEventId` field on `BookingFailureInput` interface (lines 3-12), no provider compensation call anywhere in the handler.
5. google-calendar-adapter.ts:110-112 — `cancelBooking(bookingId, _reason)` passes its argument directly as `eventId` to `client.events.delete`, so calling `provider.cancelBooking(calendarResult.calendarEventId)` would correctly delete the orphaned event.

The bug is real and novel (not in shipped list or in-flight PRs). Impact is medium — requires the uncommon double-failure (provider succeeds, DB tx fails), but when it occurs the customer receives a calendar invite for an appointment the system has marked failed. Effort is S: both `provider` and `calendarResult` are already in scope, so the fix is a 3-5 line best-effort cancel in the catch block. Impact/effort ratings match the claim.

dimensions.title: Governance, autonomy & trust ramp
dimensions.laneSummary: Alex's governance lane has a working deny-based compliance floor and a clean ingress chokepoint, but the AUTONOMY/TRUST half of it is largely scaffolding that does nothing at runtime. The single biggest issue: the earned-trust ramp is doubly-dead — Alex's listing trustScore is never incremented by live approvals (recordTrustEvent is gated on a `listing:` principal Alex never sets) AND never read for tool admission (GovernanceGate hardcodes 'guided' unless an operator pins trustLevelOverride), so autonomy is a static binary with no path from track-record to reduced friction. Compounding this, the conservative launch posture is silently lost (onboarding writes a key the runtime doesn't read), the per-deployment blast-radius/circuit-breaker safety hooks are built but unwired, and there is zero operational telemetry on governance decisions — so an operator cannot see bookings systematically parking. These are not new features to invent; they are wiring gaps in already-built machinery, each tied directly to booking conversion (booking parks at the default posture) or safety (unbounded write blast radius, dropped supervised floor).
dimensions.notesOnShippedQuality: Concrete defects found inside shipped code in this lane: (1) onboard.ts:105 writes governanceSettings.startingAutonomy while the only runtime reader (resolveTrustLevelOverride, policy-overrides-config.ts:79) reads governanceSettings.trustLevelOverride — the keys never connect, so the intended supervised launch posture is a no-op and onboarded Alex runs 'guided'. (2) SkillRuntimePolicyResolver (skill-runtime-policy-resolver.ts) is dead code — the live executor is constructed with policy=undefined (skill-mode.ts:550) and uses the static DEFAULT_SKILL_RUNTIME_POLICY, so per-deployment circuitBreakerThreshold/maxWritesPerHour/allowedModelTiers resolved by PrismaDeploymentResolver never reach any runtime gate. (3) maxWritesPerExecution and writeApprovalRequired are defined on both ExecutionConstraints and SkillRuntimePolicy but are never read anywhere except their declarations — they are inert fields implying enforcement that does not exist. (4) BlastRadiusHook and CircuitBreakerHook are implemented, tested, and exported but absent from every live hook array in apps/api and apps/chat. (5) The trust-score read path (applyAutonomyToRiskTolerance/adjustIdentity) is wired only into the legacy orchestrator propose-pipeline (propose-helpers.ts:260), not the SkillMode path Alex actually runs, so even the marketplace trustAdapter built in app.ts:353 has no effect on live Alex.

findings.title: The score-based trust ramp is fully inert for Alex — never incremented on live approvals AND never read for tool admission
findings.currentState: GovernanceGate.evaluate() sets the tool-admission trustLevel at packages/core/src/platform/governance/governance-gate.ts:91-93 as `trustLevelOverride ?? DEFAULT_CARTRIDGE_CONSTRAINTS` (trustLevel 'guided'). It NEVER reads workUnit.deployment.trustLevel — the score-derived level computed in prisma-deployment-resolver.ts:131 (trustLevelFromScore). That constraints.trustLevel is what flows to the executor (skill-mode.ts:80 -> skill-executor.ts:356 -> governance-hook.ts:17). On the write side, the only code that increments the score is platform-lifecycle.ts:571-593 recordTrustEvent, which early-returns unless proposal.parameters['_principalId'] resolves via the app.ts:349 resolver `if (!principalId.startsWith('listing:')) return null`. Alex's WorkUnit actor is the LEAD session id ({id: message.sessionId, type:'user'} at channel-gateway.ts:290) and its conversational proposals set no `listing:` _principalId, so recordApproval/recordRejection are no-ops for every Alex booking approval. The READ-back path applyAutonomyToRiskTolerance/adjustIdentity (trust-adapter.ts:54-64) is only called from orchestrator/propose-helpers.ts:260 (legacy propose-pipeline), never the SkillMode path Alex runs.
findings.opportunity: The product story is an earned autonomy ramp ('Alex earns the right to act unsupervised as approvals accrue'). In reality the listing.trustScore for Alex's live conversion work neither fills (no record) nor drains (no rejection record) nor is consulted (gate ignores it). Autonomy is a static binary: 'guided' unless an operator manually pins trustLevelOverride. There is no path by which good track record reduces approval friction, and no path by which a string of rejections tightens Alex — the safety/trust knob the architecture advertises does nothing.
findings.proposedChange: Pick ONE direction and wire it end-to-end rather than leaving a half-built ramp. Minimal: in GovernanceGate.evaluate, when no trustLevelOverride is present, derive constraints.trustLevel from workUnit.deployment.trustLevel (the score-derived level) instead of hardcoding 'guided' — this activates the read side with zero new infra. Then close the write side: when an Alex booking/approval is responded to via platform-lifecycle, record a trust event keyed by the DEPLOYMENT/listing (reuse engine.recordApproval(listingId, taskCategory) with the deployment's listingId), not gated on a `listing:` _principalId that Alex never sets. If the ramp is NOT wanted for launch, delete trustLevelFromScore/the score read entirely and make trustLevelOverride the single documented knob — do not ship a dead ledger that implies safety it doesn't provide.
findings.impactRationale: This is the central deception in this lane: the trust ramp is the mechanism that is supposed to govern how much Alex does unsupervised, and it is computed-but-discarded on both edges. Activating the read side lets earned trust actually reduce booking-approval friction (conversion); closing the write side makes rejections actually tighten Alex (safety). Either way removes a safety illusion identical in spirit to the known spend-threshold stored!=enforced gap, but for the core autonomy axis.
findings.risk: Activating the read side means an Alex deployment whose listing.trustScore >= 55 would auto-approve external_mutation (booking) — must be gated behind explicit per-deployment opt-in or a conservative default score so it cannot silently flip a clinic to autonomous. The recommended safe first step is read-side activation behind the SAME launch-posture gate operators already understand.

findings.title: Onboarding writes governanceSettings.startingAutonomy but the runtime only reads governanceSettings.trustLevelOverride — the launch posture is silently dropped
findings.currentState: onboard.ts:105 creates every Alex deployment with `governanceSettings: { startingAutonomy: 'supervised' }`. The SDK manifest defaults startingAutonomy to 'supervised' (packages/sdk/src/manifest.ts:42). But the ONLY runtime reader of governanceSettings is resolveTrustLevelOverride (policy-overrides-config.ts:75-83), which reads the `trustLevelOverride` key — NOT `startingAutonomy`. A grep confirms startingAutonomy is never read at runtime anywhere (only re-declared in SDK schema types).
