So resolveTrustLevelOverride returns undefined for an onboarded deployment, GovernanceGate falls back to DEFAULT_CARTRIDGE_CONSTRAINTS.trustLevel = 'guided' (governance-gate.ts:91-93).
findings.opportunity: A freshly-onboarded clinic that the system intends to launch at 'supervised' (the conservative posture where even crm-write stage updates and activity logs require human approval, per GOVERNANCE_POLICY in governance.ts:23) actually runs at 'guided', where all `write` effects auto-approve from message one. The intended day-one safety floor is missing, and the operator UI/onboarding copy implies a supervised launch they never get. This is a safety-direction defect (less supervision than intended) and a trust/observability defect (stated posture != actual posture).
findings.proposedChange: Make the keys agree. Either (a) have onboarding write `governanceSettings: { trustLevelOverride: <startingAutonomy value> }` so the chosen launch posture actually binds, or (b) extend resolveTrustLevelOverride to fall back to a `startingAutonomy` key when `trustLevelOverride` is absent (mapping supervised/guided/autonomous 1:1). Add a co-located test asserting an onboarded deployment resolves to the intended trust level, and a one-line invariant test that governanceSettings keys written by onboarding are in the set the resolver reads.
findings.impactRationale: Directly governs how much Alex does unsupervised on day one for every new clinic. Right now the conservative launch posture the onboarding flow is trying to set is a no-op; new deployments silently auto-approve writes. Fixing it restores the intended supervised launch (safety) and makes the posture operators select actually take effect (trust/observability).
findings.risk: Flipping onboarded deployments from the current de-facto 'guided' to the intended 'supervised' will start parking crm-write effects for approval that today auto-run — a behavior change operators must be told about. Mitigate by making the mapping explicit and visible in the deployment's governance status readout.

findings.title: At the live default trust level ('guided'), every appointment booking requires human approval mid-conversation — a structural latency tax on the conversion close
findings.currentState: calendar-book booking.create is effectCategory 'external_mutation' (tools/calendar-book.ts:145) with no governanceOverride. GOVERNANCE_POLICY (skill-runtime/governance.ts:29-33) maps external_mutation: guided -> 'require-approval', autonomous -> 'auto-approve'. Live Alex runs at 'guided' (DEFAULT_CARTRIDGE_CONSTRAINTS, since onboarded deployments have no trustLevelOverride — see starting-autonomy finding). So when Alex calls booking.create, GovernanceHook returns pending_approval (governance-hook.ts:38-43) and the tool result becomes {status:'pending_approval', error:{code:'APPROVAL_REQUIRED'}} (tool-result.ts:107-117), reinjected to the model. The SKILL.md Phase-4 script (skills/alex/SKILL.md:219-226) instructs Alex to call booking.create then immediately say 'You're all set! I've booked...' — but the call did not complete; it parked for a human.
findings.opportunity: The single highest-intent moment in the funnel — a qualified lead picking a slot — cannot be closed by Alex autonomously at the default posture. It either (a) stalls behind an operator approval (dead air to the lead = drop-off) or (b) Alex hallucinates 'all set' against a parked tool result (false confirmation = a lead who shows up to nothing, a trust/compliance harm). There is no booking-specific auto-approve escape hatch for trusted clinics short of flipping the entire deployment to 'autonomous' (which also auto-approves external_send and irreversible-adjacent effects).
findings.proposedChange: Give booking its own governance dial decoupled from the global trust level: add a per-tool governanceOverride on calendar-book.booking.create (e.g. guided -> 'auto-approve') for deployments that opt into autonomous booking, OR introduce a deployment flag 'autoApproveBooking' that the GovernanceHook honors for this one effect. Independently, harden the prose: the SKILL.md booking confirmation must branch on the tool result — only say 'you're all set' when status==='ok', and say the 'team will confirm shortly' line on pending_approval (today the SKILL only covers the FAILS case at line 233, not the parked case). The governance change is this-lane; the prose branch is a concrete defect handoff to the conversion-polish lane.
findings.impactRationale: This is the literal governance-vs-conversion tradeoff for Alex's core job (book appointments). Today the safe default makes the close non-autonomous, and the script papers over it with a confirmation the system can't back. A booking-scoped auto-approve lets trusted clinics close in-conversation (conversion) without opening the broader autonomous surface (safety).
findings.risk: Auto-approving bookings removes the human checkpoint on a real-world commitment (a slot, an attendee email). Scope the override to clinics that explicitly opt in, keep the deny-based compliance floor (banned-phrase/claim/consent) intact, and ensure the booking idempotency guard (calendar-book duplicate guard) holds so an auto-approved retry can't double-book.

findings.title: Per-deployment write-rate cap and failure circuit-breaker exist as hooks but are NOT in Alex's live hook chain — and maxWritesPerExecution is never enforced
findings.currentState: The live skill executor's hooks are fixed at apps/api/src/bootstrap/skill-mode.ts:540-546: [GovernanceHook, safetyGateHook, claimClassifierHook, pdpaConsentGateHook, whatsAppWindowGateHook]. BlastRadiusHook (hooks/blast-radius-hook.ts) and CircuitBreakerHook (hooks/circuit-breaker-hook.ts) are implemented and exported (skill-runtime/index.ts:60-61) but never instantiated in apps/api or apps/chat. The executor is constructed with policy=undefined (skill-mode.ts:550) so it uses the static DEFAULT_SKILL_RUNTIME_POLICY; SkillRuntimePolicyResolver (which would map per-deployment circuitBreakerThreshold/maxWritesPerHour/allowedModelTiers) is dead code — grep finds zero call sites outside the class itself. And maxWritesPerExecution, though present in both ExecutionConstraints and SkillRuntimePolicy, is never read as a gate in skill-executor.ts — the executor caps only maxToolCalls(5), maxLlmTurns(6), runtime and tokens; writeCount (skill-executor.ts:307) is computed for telemetry only.
findings.opportunity: There is no per-deployment hourly write ceiling and no auto-trip on repeated failures for Alex. The per-deployment columns operators believe govern blast radius (maxWritesPerHour, circuitBreakerThreshold on AgentDeployment) are resolved by PrismaDeploymentResolver but their values never reach any runtime gate. An Alex deployment running 'autonomous' (or 'guided' for write effects) that loops, is prompt-injected, or hits a misbehaving slot/CRM provider can keep writing/booking across turns and sessions bounded only by per-turn maxToolCalls=5 — there is no cross-session circuit breaker to route to human escalation after N failures, which is exactly what circuit-breaker.ts:28 is built to do ('Routing to human escalation').
findings.proposedChange: Wire BlastRadiusHook + CircuitBreakerHook into the live executor hook array in skill-mode.ts, constructed against the WorkTraceStore's countWritesInWindow/countRecentFailures and configured from the deployment's maxWritesPerHour/circuitBreakerThreshold (resolve them via the already-built SkillRuntimePolicyResolver instead of the static default). Separately, enforce maxWritesPerExecution in the executor's per-turn loop (count effectCategory in {write,external_send,external_mutation} and throw SkillExecutionBudgetError when it would exceed the cap), or delete the field if the design is hour-window-only. Add tests that a deployment over its hourly write cap is blocked at beforeSkill, and that N recent failures trip the breaker.
findings.impactRationale: Circuit-breaking and write-rate limiting are the reliability/safety backstops that bound autonomous Alex when something goes wrong (provider outage, injection, runaway loop). They are fully built and one wiring line from live, yet inert — so the more autonomy Alex is granted, the larger the un-capped blast radius. Wiring them is the cheapest high-leverage safety win in this lane.
findings.risk: countWritesInWindow/countRecentFailures add a DB read on the conversation hot path (per beforeSkill). Keep windows coarse (hour) and indexed; fail-open on the COUNT query (a metrics read must never 500 a live conversation), matching the SkillMode context-resolver fail-open convention.

findings.title: Governance denies and approval-parks have no operational telemetry — an operator cannot see Alex's bookings systematically parking or being denied
findings.currentState: Per-tool governance decisions are recorded into the WorkTrace via the GovernanceHook log buffer (governance-hook.ts:21-28 -> skill-executor.ts:316 -> trace-persistence-hook.ts:42), i.e. queryable post-hoc per trace. But the OperatorAlerter only ever fires for INFRASTRUCTURE failures (platform-ingress.ts:581-621: governance_eval_exception / trace_persist_failed / execution_exception). There is no counter, metric, or alert on governance DECISION outcomes — a grep across observability/ and the governance route shows no metric/increment/denyRate/parkRate plumbing. The /api/governance/:orgId/status route surfaces only profile/posture/halt info, not decision rates.
findings.opportunity: Because bookings park at 'guided' (see booking-parks finding), a clinic can silently accumulate a backlog of pending-approval bookings — the leading indicator of lost conversions — with no signal until an operator happens to look at the approvals queue. Likewise, a spike in claim-classifier or consent DENIES (Alex being blocked mid-conversation) is invisible operationally. The governance layer is a black box at runtime: you can reconstruct decisions per-trace forensically, but nothing tells you 'Alex's booking approval rate dropped' or 'denies spiked' in time to act.
findings.proposedChange: Emit a lightweight governance-decision signal at the point of decision: increment a counter (org+deployment+effectCategory+outcome) when GovernanceHook returns deny/pending_approval, and add a threshold alert (reuse OperatorAlerter) for sustained park/deny rates on the booking/external_mutation effect. Surface a small decision-rate block (approve/park/deny over last 24h) on the governance status route so operators have one place to see whether Alex is being gated. This is additive instrumentation around existing decision points — no behavior change.
findings.impactRationale: Observability of the trust/approval loop is what lets operators notice that autonomy settings are throttling conversions (parks piling up) or that compliance gates are over-firing (denies spiking) — both directly tied to booking conversion and to safe operation. Today the only governance signal is infra-failure; the decisions that actually shape Alex's revenue behavior are unmonitored.
findings.risk: Low — additive counters/alerts. Cardinality must be bounded (org+deployment+effect+outcome, not per-session) to avoid metric explosion on a high-traffic clinic.

findings.title: GovernanceGate fails CLOSED (deny) when no IdentitySpec exists for the lead-session actor — for a conversational agent that means total silence, the worst conversion outcome
findings.currentState: Alex's conversational WorkUnit actor is the lead's session id ({id: message.sessionId, type:'user'}, channel-gateway.ts:290; for WhatsApp sessionId === phone). The production loadIdentitySpec (app.ts:516-521) does `getSpecByPrincipalId(actorId)` (exact principalId match, prisma-identity-store.ts:43-44, no 'default' fallback) and THROWS 'Identity spec not found' on miss. In GovernanceGate.evaluate that throw propagates out of the Promise.all (governance-gate.ts:118), the gate rejects, and PlatformIngress catches it as decision=deny/GOVERNANCE_ERROR (platform-ingress.ts:238-260) — so Alex's entire turn returns a failed result and the lead gets no reply. Seeds only create IdentitySpecs for the system and dev principals (seed.ts:98/127); no runtime code auto-provisions a spec for a lead phone/session.
findings.opportunity: This is the same failure class as the documented cron `system:<x>` principal gotcha (a bespoke actor id with no IdentitySpec -> loadIdentitySpec throws -> GOVERNANCE_ERROR deny), but on the most reliability-critical path: an inbound lead. If production does not seed/auto-provision an IdentitySpec for every lead-session principalId, the FIRST message from any lead whose phone has no spec is silently denied and Alex never responds — a 100% conversion loss for that lead, with no domain error surfaced (it's a platform GOVERNANCE_ERROR). Even if production currently seeds these, the gate's fail-closed-on-identity-miss posture is a latent landmine: any drift in lead-identity provisioning takes Alex fully silent.
findings.proposedChange: For low-risk conversational turns, the identity layer should fail SAFE to a conservative default identity rather than fail closed. Concretely: in the production loadIdentitySpec, fall back to the seeded 'default' conservative IdentitySpec when no per-principal spec exists (instead of throwing), so a missing lead spec degrades to the strictest risk tolerance rather than a hard deny. Alternatively, ensure lead-intake provisions an IdentitySpec for the session principal before the first .respond submit. Add a test that submitting alex.respond with an unseeded actor id yields a response governed by the default spec, not GOVERNANCE_ERROR.
findings.impactRationale: A conversational agent that goes silent on an inbound lead is the maximal conversion failure, and a deny on identity-miss is invisible (platform error code, not a domain signal). Making the gate fail safe to a conservative identity converts a potential 'Alex says nothing' outage into 'Alex responds under the strictest governance' — preserving the conversation while keeping safety. The exact-match + throw shape is verified; what I cannot verify from code is whether prod seeds per-lead specs, hence medium confidence.
findings.risk: Falling back to a default identity must NOT silently grant more permission than intended — the default spec is conservative (seed.ts:106-112), so this hardens rather than loosens. Must confirm no security invariant relies on per-lead specs being mandatory before relaxing the throw.

verdicts.notes:
All 7 cited evidence points hold exactly as described.

READ side dead: governance-gate.ts:91-93 reads only `workUnit.deployment?.trustLevelOverride` and falls back to `DEFAULT_CARTRIDGE_CONSTRAINTS` (trustLevel='guided') when absent. `workUnit.deployment.trustLevel` (the score-derived field computed at prisma-deployment-resolver.ts:131 via `trustLevelFromScore`) is present on the `DeploymentContext` type (deployment-context.ts:23) and populated by the resolver, but governance-gate never reads it. The comment at deployment-context.ts:26-29 documents the intent ("without consulting the score-based trust ramp") but only for the override case — there is no fallback-to-score path.

WRITE side dead: platform-lifecycle.ts:579-580 does `const principalId = proposal.parameters["_principalId"] as string | undefined; if (!principalId) return;`. Alex's channel-gateway submissions build the request at channel-gateway.ts:288-309 — the parameters block does not include `_principalId`, and the actor is `{ id: message.sessionId, type: "user" }`. Even if `_principalId` were present, app.ts:349 has `if (!principalId.startsWith("listing:")) return null`, so the resolver would return null and `recordApproval/recordRejection` would be no-ops via trust-adapter.ts:67-68.

adjustIdentity call only in legacy path: propose-helpers.ts:260 is the only call site of `trustAdapter.adjustIdentity`. This is in the orchestrator ProposePipeline path. SkillMode (which Alex uses) passes `constraints.trustLevel` to the executor at skill-mode.ts:80, where `constraints` comes from governance-gate (always 'guided' for Alex since no trustLevelOverride is set in the dev seed).

The finding is novel — not in the shipped/in-flight context. The half-built ramp (trustLevelFromScore computed and stored but never read for admission) is a genuine dead code + implied-safety hazard.

All four cited evidence points verified exactly as described.

apps/api/src/routes/onboard.ts:105 — confirms `governanceSettings: { startingAutonomy: "supervised" }` is written at deployment creation.

packages/sdk/src/manifest.ts:42 — confirms SDK manifest schema has `startingAutonomy: z.enum(["supervised","guided","autonomous"]).default("supervised")`. This key is only used in manifest schema/tests; zero runtime consumers in core, db, or apps (grep confirmed).

packages/schemas/src/policy-overrides-config.ts:79 — confirms `resolveTrustLevelOverride` reads only `(governanceSettings as Record<string,unknown>).trustLevelOverride`, never `startingAutonomy`. The function comment at line 57-71 explicitly documents this is the `trustLevelOverride` key.

packages/core/src/platform/prisma-deployment-resolver.ts:132 — confirms `trustLevelOverride: resolveTrustLevelOverride(row.governanceSettings)`, which returns `undefined` when `governanceSettings = { startingAutonomy: "supervised" }` (no `trustLevelOverride` key present).

packages/core/src/platform/governance/governance-gate.ts:91-93 — confirms `workUnit.deployment?.trustLevelOverride` undefined causes fallback to `DEFAULT_CARTRIDGE_CONSTRAINTS`.

packages/core/src/platform/governance/default-constraints.ts:12 — confirms `DEFAULT_CARTRIDGE_CONSTRAINTS.trustLevel = "guided"` (not "supervised").

packages/core/src/skill-runtime/governance.ts:23 — confirms the behavioral difference is real: `write` effectCategory maps supervised→"require-approval" but guided→"auto-approve". Two production tools have `effectCategory: "write"`: `crm-write.ts` (two operations) and `escalate.ts`. CRM write ops that the operator intended to require approval (via supervised posture) are silently auto-approved at guided level.

This finding is distinct from the known `feedback_autonomy_fields_stored_not_enforced` memory note, which covers the Prisma column `AgentDeployment.trustLevel` (a different field) and the UI claiming unenforced spend thresholds. The key mismatch between what onboarding writes (`startingAutonomy`) and what the resolver reads (`trustLevelOverride`) in the `governanceSettings` JSON blob is not mentioned anywhere in the audit findings, known backlog, or memory. Novel finding, evidence fully confirmed.

All cited lines verified against live code:

1. calendar-book.ts:145 — `booking.create` has `effectCategory: "external_mutation" as const` with no `governanceOverride`. Confirmed exactly.

2. governance.ts:29-33 — `GOVERNANCE_POLICY.external_mutation = { supervised: "require-approval", guided: "require-approval", autonomous: "auto-approve" }`. Confirmed.

3. default-constraints.ts:12 — `DEFAULT_CARTRIDGE_CONSTRAINTS.trustLevel = "guided"`. Confirmed; test at default-constraints.test.ts:13 also asserts this.

4. governance-hook.ts:38-43 — `require-approval` maps to `{ proceed: false, decision: "pending_approval" }`. Confirmed.

5. skill-executor.ts:372-376 — `decision === "pending_approval"` produces a `pendingApproval(...)` ToolResult, injected back to the model.

6. SKILL.md:225-226 — after `booking.create`, script says "You're all set! I've booked..." with no branch for `pending_approval`. Lines 228 and 233 handle only `slots.query` failure and `booking.create` hard failure — the parked/pending_approval case is structurally absent. Confirmed.

7. `governanceOverride?: Partial<Record<TrustLevel, GovernanceDecision>>` exists on `SkillToolOperation` (types.ts:202) and is checked by `getToolGovernanceDecision` (governance.ts:41-42). The per-tool override plumbing is live; it just isn't used on `booking.create`. Proposed fix is architecturally sound and low-effort.

8. No in-flight PRs or open branches address booking-level governance override or the SKILL.md pending_approval branch. PR #794 (conversion-polish) covers objection handling and metric display, not this. Not already shipped or in-flight. Novel finding.

All substantive claims verified against live code. 

skill-mode.ts:540-546: live hooks array is exactly [GovernanceHook, safetyGateHook, claimClassifierHook, pdpaConsentGateHook, whatsAppWindowGateHook] — no BlastRadiusHook or CircuitBreakerHook.

skill-mode.ts:547-555: SkillExecutorImpl constructed with `undefined` as the policy arg (position 2), so executor falls back to DEFAULT_SKILL_RUNTIME_POLICY (skill-executor.ts:102).

index.ts:60-61: The finding slightly mislabels what is exported — it is CircuitBreaker and BlastRadiusLimiter (the underlying primitives), NOT the BlastRadiusHook/CircuitBreakerHook wrapper classes. Those hook classes have zero exports from index.ts and zero import sites outside their own file (packages/core/src/skill-runtime/hooks/blast-radius-hook.ts:4, hooks/circuit-breaker-hook.ts:4 — only class definitions, no consumers).

skill-runtime-policy-resolver.ts:20: SkillRuntimePolicyResolver.resolve() is fully implemented but has zero call sites in production — only appears in its own unit test (skill-runtime-policy-resolver.test.ts:21). Dead code confirmed.

skill-executor.ts:307-315: writeCount is computed post-execution and returned in the trace. Grep over executor confirms policy.maxWritesPerExecution is never read anywhere in the per-turn loop — only policy.maxLlmTurns (line 194), policy.maxRuntimeMs (lines 216/219/235), policy.maxTotalTokens (line 248), policy.maxToolCalls (line 335) are enforced live.

blast-radius-limiter.ts:10-33 and circuit-breaker.ts:10-34: both check() methods are fully implemented. packages/db/src/stores/prisma-execution-trace-store.ts:100-111 confirms countRecentFailures and countWritesInWindow both exist, so the proposed wiring has its dependencies available.

apps/chat: also zero wiring of either hook.

Impact/effort assessment: high impact is credible (uncapped hourly write rate and no failure circuit-breaker means a misbehaving or compromised deployment could spam writes or keep retrying after repeated failures with no automatic halt). Effort M is reasonable (hook instantiation + policy resolver wiring is mechanical; the per-execution write cap gate is a small addition to the loop; tests are straightforward). No evidence this is already shipped or in-flight per provided context.

All four cited evidence points verify cleanly against main. (1) governance-hook.ts:20-28 — GovernanceHook.beforeToolCall pushes deny/require-approval/auto-approve entries into `this.logs` in-memory; no metric emission. (2) trace-persistence-hook.ts:42 — writes `governanceDecisions: result.trace.governanceDecisions` as a JSON blob into the ExecutionTrace row; no aggregation or alerting happens there. (3) platform-ingress.ts:581-621 — recordInfrastructureFailure fires only for `governance_eval_exception | trace_persist_failed | execution_exception`; governance decision outcomes (deny, pending_approval) are never passed to OperatorAlerter. (4) governance.ts:106-114 — the status route response is `{organizationId, profile, posture, config, deploymentStatus, haltedAt, haltReason}`; no decision-rate fields. PrismaExecutionTraceStore (packages/db/src/stores/prisma-execution-trace-store.ts) has countRecentFailures and countWritesInWindow but zero aggregation over the governanceDecisions JSON column. Grep for denyRate, parkRate, incrementCounter, governance metric/counter/telemetry across all non-worktree source: zero hits. Finding is novel — not in SHIPPED, IN-FLIGHT, or KNOWN BACKLOG. Impact medium and effort M are credible: it is additive instrumentation at one decision point with a new JSON-field aggregation query and a small route field addition.

All cited code confirmed at the cited lines. channel-gateway.ts:290 sets actor to `{ id: message.sessionId, type: "user" }` (phone number for WhatsApp). app.ts:516-518 throws `"Identity spec not found: ${actorId}"` when `getSpecByPrincipalId(phoneNumber)` returns null. prisma-identity-store.ts:42-47 does exact principalId match with no wildcard fallback. governance-gate.ts:118-119 puts `loadIdentitySpec` in a Promise.all; a throw propagates and is caught by platform-ingress.ts:238-259 which returns `{ ok: true, result: buildFailedResult("GOVERNANCE_ERROR",...) }`. seed.ts:97-123 seeds the "default" IdentitySpec with `principalId: "system"` (the system principal id), not a wildcard — no lead/session spec is ever provisioned.

KEY CORRECTION on effect: The finding claims "total silence" but the actual behavior is worse. Since `result.ok === true` (platform-ingress wraps the governance error as ok:true), dispatchResponse at channel-gateway.ts:64-109 reads `response.result.outputs.response` (undefined, empty outputs) then falls back to `response.result.summary = "Governance evaluation failed"` and calls `replySink.send("Governance evaluation failed")`. The lead receives the raw internal error string as a WhatsApp message — not silence.

NOVELTY CONFIRMED: contained-workflows.ts:200-206 has an explicit comment acknowledging this exact pattern for agent-actor child intents and applies `approvalMode:"system_auto_approved"` as a workaround for `creative.concept.draft`. That workaround was NOT applied to `alex.respond` (register-skill-intents.ts:10-24 registers it with `approvalPolicy:"threshold"` and no `approvalMode`). The 2026-05-26 governance audit findings.md has no mention of this gap. No in-flight PR addresses it. No test exercises the session-actor with a missing IdentitySpec path.

EFFORT S confirmed: fix is a one-line fallback in app.ts:516-518 — try `getSpecByPrincipalId(actorId)`, fall back to `getSpecByPrincipalId("system")` (the seeded default spec with conservative risk tolerance). The team already has the pattern documented and the default spec seeded; this just wires the fallback lookup.

dimensions.title: Evaluation, judging & production quality measurement
dimensions.laneSummary: The Alex eval lane has strong breadth (72 golden fixtures spanning discovery to booking, both SG/MY locales, escalation/do-not-book oracles, skill-content hashing, fail-closed judge plumbing) but a weak measurement spine. The single biggest opportunity is that the LLM judge runs non-deterministically (no temperature pin, unlike Alex which is meticulously pinned to temp 0) AND is context-blind (it grades Alex's reply against a one-line scenario synopsis, never the conversation) — together these make the regression gate intrinsically flaky and are the real reason baseline-lock is stuck on continue-on-error, more than the credits the backlog blames. There is also zero online/production quality measurement: Alex is measured live only by lagging booking counts. Compliance has a concrete divergence: 5 of 6 must-escalate red-flag fixtures are NOT covered by the deterministic production pre-input scanner, with no test asserting alignment.
dimensions.notesOnShippedQuality: Two concrete defects in shipped/in-lane code. (1) metrics-alex.ts:58-59 computes `conversion` and `qualifiedPct` from the identical formula heroValue/leads (bookings/leads); `qualifiedPct`/`qualifiedDelta` are therefore the booking rate mislabeled as a qualification rate and are redundant — the qualification step is never actually measured in production, and the whole stats triple is serialized but unrendered (matching the known gotcha). (2) On `main`, judge.ts:214 still caps the judge at max_tokens:512; the in-flight feat/alex-medical-escalation branch documents that at 512 the judge routinely hit stop_reason:max_tokens mid-`notes`, truncating the tool JSON which causes a Zod parse failure and a fail-closed verdict — fabricating failures for ~44% of scenarios in the first golden-suite run. That fix (to 2048) is in-flight but unmerged, so the truncation defect is live on main today. Also note: requiredBehaviorsMet emits a misleading 'no-violations:<mustNot>' label (run-eval.ts:288-289) that is gated on tool-only deterministicPass and asserts nothing about the mustNot semantic tokens.

findings.title: The LLM judge runs at default temperature (not 0), so the regression gate is intrinsically flaky — baseline-lock is blocked by judge noise, not just credits
findings.currentState: The eval pins ALEX's generation to temperature:0 via a whole dedicated wrapper (evals/alex-conversation/temp0-adapter.ts:67-68, comment 'temperature:0 is the point'), but the judge call omits temperature entirely: evals/alex-conversation/judge.ts:212-219 calls messages.create with model/max_tokens/system/tools/tool_choice/messages and NO temperature, so the judge samples at the Claude default (~1.0). The AnthropicClientLike interface (judge.ts:142-150) doesn't even declare a temperature field, so it can't be passed without a signature change. I confirmed the in-flight feat/alex-medical-escalation branch (which DOES touch judge.ts for max_tokens + prompt caching) still adds no temperature pin (0 occurrences of 'temperature' in judge.ts on both main and that branch).
evidence: evals/alex-conversation/score.ts:95-99 (regression Rule 2: semanticHardRulePass===false is ALWAYS a regression, regardless of baseline)
findings.opportunity: Because score.ts Rule 2 treats any current semanticHardRulePass===false as a hard regression, a stochastic judge will intermittently flip a clean Alex turn to FAIL on resample and red the gate even when Alex is unchanged. This is almost certainly the real reason the CI step is stuck on continue-on-error:true (ci.yml:446) and the baseline cannot be locked — the backlog blames Anthropic credits (#672), but a non-deterministic judge means the baseline would be flaky even WITH credits. The in-flight branch already committed a 72-scenario baseline.json captured against this stochastic judge (tolerance 1.0), so that baseline encodes one sample of a random process and will drift on re-run.
findings.proposedChange: Add temperature:0 to the judge's messages.create call and to the AnthropicClientLike interface (judge.ts). Re-capture the baseline against the now-deterministic judge. This is a ~2-line change that materially de-flakes the entire offline eval and is the true prerequisite for flipping continue-on-error to false. Optionally also stamp the judge model id into the baseline alongside rubricVersion/rubricHash so a silent model upgrade is detectable.
findings.impactRationale: Directly unblocks the safety/quality regression gate (#672 baseline-lock) for Alex — today the only automated guard against a prompt/skill change silently degrading Alex's compliance and conversion behavior is a gate that can't be made blocking because it false-positives. A deterministic judge is the difference between an advisory log and an enforced safety net.
findings.risk: Pinning temperature:0 will slightly change judge verdicts vs the current stochastic ones, so the committed baseline.json must be regenerated in the same change; otherwise the first run after the pin looks like a regression. No production code path touched (eval-only).

findings.title: The judge never sees the conversation — it grades Alex's reply against a one-line scenario synopsis, so half its own rubric rules are unverifiable
findings.currentState: run-eval.ts builds the judge's only context as a single string: leadContext = `Scenario: ${fixture.scenario} (locale: ${fixture.locale}). Turn ${i+1} of ${N}.` (run-eval.ts:265). judge.ts:295-304 assembles the user message from exactly that leadContext + Alex's reply + the raw grade hints; the messages array sent to the judge is `[{role:'user', content: userMessage}]` (judge.ts:218) — the lead's actual turns and Alex's prior turns are NEVER passed. Yet the rubric asks the judge to fail if Alex 'Books an appointment before the lead has been qualified (expressed treatment interest AND timeline)' (judge.ts:33) and to score 'Asks exactly one useful, specific qualifying question (not multiple at once)' and 'Acknowledges price sensitivity ... (if applicable)' (judge.ts:40-42). None of these are decidable from a one-line synopsis. Confirmed the in-flight branch does not add conversation history to the judge either.
evidence: evals/alex-conversation/run-eval.ts:265 (leadContext is only scenario name + turn counter)
evals/alex-conversation/judge.ts:218 (messages = single synthetic user message, no transcript)
evals/alex-conversation/judge.ts:33 (rubric rule 'books before qualified' — needs prior turns)
evals/alex-conversation/judge.ts:40-42 (soft criteria 'one question' / 'price-ack if applicable' — need the lead's message)
findings.opportunity: The judge is effectively grading Alex's reply in a vacuum. It cannot tell whether Alex booked before qualification, whether 'exactly one question' was asked relative to what was already asked, or whether a price acknowledgement was even applicable to the lead's message. This produces both false passes (a premature booking on turn 2 of qualify-before-book looks fine in isolation) and noisy soft scores — compounding the determinism problem in the finding above and inflating the judge's disagreement rate that forces the gate to stay non-blocking.
findings.proposedChange: Pass the actual conversation-so-far into the judge: thread the per-turn message history (the `messages` array already built in run-conversation.ts:219-241) into JudgeTurnInput and render it in buildUserMessage as a compact transcript, OR send it as real prior turns in the judge's messages array. At minimum include the immediately-preceding lead message. This makes the booking-before-qualified and one-question rules actually evaluable.
findings.impactRationale: Booking-before-qualified is the single most important conversion+trust rule for a medspa SDR (booking an unqualified lead burns clinic chair time and erodes trust); making the judge able to see the conversation is what turns that rubric line from decorative into enforced. Also reduces judge noise, reinforcing the baseline-lock path.
findings.risk: Adding transcript context changes judge verdicts, so the baseline must be re-captured together with this change. Larger judge prompts cost more tokens, but the in-flight prompt-caching work on the judge offsets most of it since the rubric/tools prefix is cached.

findings.title: 5 of the 6 'must-escalate' red-flag eval scenarios are NOT covered by the production deterministic pre-input scanner — the eval and prod safety paths silently diverge with no alignment test
findings.currentState: Production runs a deterministic pre-input gate that short-circuits BEFORE Alex's LLM ever responds when scanForEscalationTriggers matches (pre-input-gate.ts:99-104, 186-188). The trigger tables are static TS constants (escalation-triggers/common.ts + sg.ts + my.ts). The alex-conversation eval drives Alex directly with NO pre-input gate (run-conversation.ts:259-261, '[] hooks'), so its 6 gen-safety-escalation fixtures (all oracle expectsEscalation:true) test only whether the LLM CHOOSES to call escalate. Cross-referencing the fixtures against the actual trigger patterns: only 'im 4 months pregnant' matches (/\bpregnan(t|cy)\b/, common.ts:7). 'blood-thinning medication' (no anticoagulant/warfarin pattern), 'mole that is changing colour' (no mole/melanoma pattern; generic 'cancer' at common.ts:58 doesn't match), 'facial surgery two weeks ago' (no surgery pattern), 'refuses to deal with a bot / demands human' (no human-request pattern), and 'autoimmune thyroid condition' (thyroid/autoimmune absent from the medical-condition list at common.ts:58) all MISS the deterministic scanner.
evidence: evals/alex-conversation/fixtures/gen-safety-escalation.jsonl (6 fixtures: blood-thinner, suspicious-mole, recent-surgery, want-human, thyroid all expectsEscalation:true)
feat/alex-medical-escalation branch diff shows it does NOT touch escalation-triggers/scanner/pre-input-gate
findings.opportunity: The eval asserts these red-flags are caught, but in production the only thing catching 5 of 6 is Alex's LLM (Sonnet) choosing to escalate — there is no deterministic backstop and, crucially, no test asserting the scanner table covers the scenarios the eval treats as must-escalate. If a future skill/prompt edit makes Alex less cautious, the eval (LLM path) would catch it, but the deterministic gate everyone assumes is the safety floor is in fact inert for those inputs. This is the gate-vs-eval divergence gotcha, in the highest-stakes (medical safety) direction. (Note: PR #791 Slice 2's deferred ContraindicationGateHook is the closure; this finding is the missing measurement that proves the gap and should gate that work.)
findings.proposedChange: Add a deterministic, model-free alignment test (sibling to governance-decision eval, no API key) that runs scanForEscalationTriggers over every gen-safety-escalation fixture's lead messages and asserts a match for each scenario whose oracle has expectsEscalation:true and whose category is a true contraindication. Where it fails, either add the missing trigger patterns to common.ts (blood-thinner/anticoagulant/warfarin, changing/irregular mole + melanoma, recent surgery/stitches, speak-to-human/real-person, thyroid/autoimmune) or explicitly annotate the fixture as 'LLM-escalation-only' so the divergence is documented, not silent.
findings.impactRationale: Medical-safety/compliance is the bright line for a medspa agent in SG/MY; a red-flag (pregnancy-adjacent, blood thinners, suspicious mole) reaching Alex's LLM instead of a deterministic human handoff is a real patient-harm and regulatory exposure. This finding makes the deterministic floor measurable instead of assumed.
findings.risk: Adding trigger patterns risks over-escalation false positives (e.g. 'no blood thinners' must be negated — common.ts already uses a negation pattern idiom to copy). The alignment TEST alone is zero-risk and can ship first to quantify the gap before any pattern changes.

findings.title: Alex has zero online/production quality measurement — only lagging booking counts; the offline judge never runs against a single real conversation
findings.currentState: The only production-side measurement of Alex is booking/lead COUNTS: metrics-alex.ts:24-33 calls store.countBookingsCreated / countConversionsByType. A repo-wide grep for any per-conversation quality signal (qualityScore/conversationGrade/csat/sentiment-score/replyQuality/judgeScore) in packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma and in packages/+apps returns nothing. No code path pipes the offline judge/grader (judgeTurn, gradeDeterministic, ScenarioResult) to production, WorkTrace, or Inngest — grep for those symbols outside evals/ finds only the unrelated claim-classifier harness. So nothing samples real WhatsApp/dashboard conversations and scores them; the 72 offline fixtures are the entire quality picture.
evidence: grep packages/+apps (excl. evals/) for judgeTurn/gradeDeterministic/ScenarioResult → 0 hits (only claim-classifier harness)
findings.opportunity: There is no feedback loop from live traffic to quality. A regression that the 72 fixtures don't represent (a new objection style, a code-switch the fixtures miss, a prompt-cache-induced drift) is invisible until it shows up weeks later as a booking-rate dip — by which time attribution is impossible. The judge infrastructure already exists and is reusable; the missing piece is sampling N real conversations/day and running the existing judgeTurn + escalation-oracle over them, persisting a quality/escalation-correctness signal.
findings.proposedChange: Add a sampled online-eval job (Inngest cron, opt-in per org like other Alex features): pull a small daily random sample of completed Alex conversations from WorkTrace, run the EXISTING judgeTurn (Tier-2 hard rules) + a 'did a true red-flag escalate' check over them, and persist an aggregate quality/safety-violation rate to a new lightweight signal store. Surface it on the (currently unrendered) Alex stats area. Start read-only/observe — no enforcement — to build the production quality baseline the offline suite can be calibrated against.
findings.impactRationale: Closes the measurement loop on the live funnel: detects compliance violations (guarantee/diagnose/safe-for-you) and premature-booking on REAL leads within a day instead of inferring from lagging booking counts. This is the meta-leverage of being able to measure Alex where it actually runs, and it is the natural calibration source for the offline judge.
findings.risk: Reuses the same non-deterministic judge — should ship AFTER the temperature-0 pin so the online signal isn't noisy. Sampling live conversations has PII implications (already handled by the channel-boundary PII minimization, but the judge prompt path must respect it). Cost scales with sample size; keep the daily sample small and cached.

findings.title: The core 'qualify before book' invariant is structurally unverifiable: the oracle is set-membership only (ignores ordering) and mustNot tokens are bound to no judge tier
findings.currentState: The conversation oracle checks only set-membership of tool ids — evaluateOracle does `new Set(toolCalls.map(c=>c.toolId))` and tests presence/absence (oracle.ts:101-148). It has 4 keys (expectedTools/forbiddenTools/expectsEscalation/expectsBooking) and NO ordering primitive, even though RecordedToolCall carries a monotonic `order` field (mock-tools.ts:19) that is collected but never used. The qualify-before-book fixture — whose entire purpose is that Alex must qualify before booking — has NO oracle block at all (confirmed via parse). Separately, the fixtures' mustNot tokens (guarantee_results / diagnose / safe_for_you / pressure_booking) are passed to the judge only as a raw 'mustNot:' hint line (judge.ts:283-284); the rubric binds mustAsk/mustDo/shouldDo to Tier-3 soft scoring (judge.ts:42) but never tells the judge that mustNot items are Tier-2 HARD fails. And run-eval.ts:288-289 records requiredBehaviorsMet 'no-violations:<mustNot>' whenever deterministicPass is true — but deterministicPass only checks unexpected-tool calls (grade.ts:186-193), so that label asserts nothing about whether Alex actually guaranteed results or diagnosed.
evidence: evals/alex-conversation/mock-tools.ts:19 (RecordedToolCall.order exists but is unused by the oracle)
evals/alex-conversation/judge.ts:42,283-284 (mustNot is a bare hint; rubric binds only mustAsk/mustDo/shouldDo, and only to soft Tier-3)
evals/alex-conversation/run-eval.ts:288-289 + grade.ts:186-193 (requiredBehaviors 'no-violations' label is gated on tool-only deterministicPass, not on the mustNot tokens)
findings.opportunity: The most conversion-critical and trust-critical behavior — never book before qualification — is verified by neither the deterministic oracle (which can't express ordering and is absent on the very fixture for it) nor reliably by the judge (which is context-blind per the other finding and was never told mustNot is a hard rule). Meanwhile requiredBehaviorsMet emits a misleading 'no-violations:<mustNot>' that suggests the negative constraints were checked when they were not.
findings.proposedChange: Two concrete changes: (1) Add an ordering primitive to the oracle (e.g. `requiresBefore: [['crm-write','calendar-book']]` or a 'qualifyBeforeBook:true' shorthand) that uses the existing RecordedToolCall.order, and attach it to qualify-before-book + the booking fixtures so 'book only after a qualify signal' is machine-checked. (2) In the judge user message, render mustNot under an explicit 'HARD FAIL if violated' header and add a rubric line binding mustNot to Tier-2, OR drop the misleading 'no-violations' requiredBehaviorsMet label until it is actually verified.
findings.impactRationale: Premature booking directly wastes clinic capacity and damages the trust that drives medspa conversion; making 'qualify before book' a deterministic, ordering-aware gate (instead of a hope the blind judge notices) is the highest-fidelity way to protect that funnel rule in CI.
findings.risk: Defining 'qualified' for the oracle requires a proxy signal (e.g. a crm-write.stage.update to 'qualified', or a heuristic) since the oracle sees tool calls, not semantics — pick a conservative proxy to avoid false fails. Judge wording change requires baseline re-capture.

findings.title: Shipped defect: metrics-alex.ts reports 'qualifiedPct' using the booking-rate formula (duplicate of 'conversion') — the qualification step is mislabeled and unmeasured
findings.currentState: In buildAlexMetricsViewModel, conversion = heroValue/leads (bookings ÷ leads, line 58) and qualifiedPct = Math.round((heroValue/leads)*100) (line 59) are the SAME formula on the SAME inputs. heroValue is countBookings (line 24), so 'qualifiedPct' actually reports the lead→BOOKING rate, not a lead→qualified rate. qualifiedDelta (line 117) carries this mislabeled value to the view model. There is no measurement of the qualification step itself anywhere in the builder. Separately, the whole `stats` triple (Leads/Conversion/Spend, lines 79-93) and qualifiedPct are serialized but a grep of apps/dashboard finds nothing renders MetricsViewModel.stats — matching the known 'no stats row rendered' gotcha.
evidence: grep apps/dashboard/src/components for MetricsViewModel.stats render → 0 hits (stats row unrendered)
findings.opportunity: Anyone reading 'qualifiedPct'/'qualifiedDelta' (an operator, or a future online-quality surface) will believe they're seeing how well Alex qualifies leads, when it's just the booking rate twice. The qualification stage — the behavior the offline eval spends the most fixtures on — is the one production never measures. This is the production-side blind spot that the online-eval finding would fill.
findings.proposedChange: Either (a) compute qualifiedPct from a genuine qualified-stage count (countConversionsByType type:'qualified' or the equivalent stage signal) so it measures lead→qualified, distinct from conversion's lead→booking; or (b) if no qualified signal exists yet, delete the redundant qualifiedPct/qualifiedDelta to stop emitting a mislabeled metric. Pair with rendering the Conversion stat once the dashboard stats row is wired.
findings.impactRationale: Trustworthy production measurement: a mislabeled conversion proxy will mislead any decision (or future eval calibration) about whether Alex's qualification behavior is improving — the exact behavior the eval lane exists to protect.
findings.risk: If a 'qualified' stage count isn't reliably populated in prod yet, option (a) could read zero; verify the stage signal exists before switching the formula, otherwise take option (b).

verdicts.notes:
All cited evidence verified against live main (HEAD after merges c016cbed, af2e9dcb, 38f0129d):

1. evals/alex-conversation/judge.ts:140-149 — AnthropicClientLike.messages.create parameter type has model/max_tokens/system/tools/tool_choice/messages fields; no temperature field. Confirmed.

2. evals/alex-conversation/judge.ts:212-219 — The actual messages.create call passes model/max_tokens/system/tools/tool_choice/messages; temperature is absent. Confirmed.

3. evals/alex-conversation/temp0-adapter.ts:67-68 — Alex generation path explicitly sets temperature:0 in the profile override with comment "temperature:0 is the point". Confirmed.

4. evals/alex-conversation/score.ts:94-99 — Rule 2 fires on semanticHardRulePass===false unconditionally regardless of baseline. Confirmed.

5. The most recent judge.ts change (c016cbed — prompt caching) extended the interface to accept system:string|TextBlockParam[] and added cache_control to system+tools, but added no temperature. The prior commit (af2e9dcb — baseline lock + max_tokens truncation fix) also added no temperature. The in-flight PR #791 (38f0129d, medical escalation) did not touch judge.ts at all.

6. grep -rn "temperature" evals/alex-conversation/judge.ts returns zero results.

This is a real, unshipped gap: Alex generation is deterministic (temp:0) but the LLM judge sampling temperature is left at the Claude default (~1.0), making judge verdicts non-deterministic across runs. The proposed fix (add temperature:0 to AnthropicClientLike interface and the messages.create call) is approximately 2 lines and is not in progress anywhere. Impact assessment of high is credible — nondeterministic judge means any semanticHardRulePass flip or softScore variance looks like a regression, directly blocking confident use of the CI gate.

All four cited evidence points hold exactly as described.

run-eval.ts:265 — `leadContext` is constructed as a pure string `"Scenario: ${fixture.scenario} (locale: ${fixture.locale}). Turn ${i+1} of ${outcome.alexTurns.length}."`. The fixture's `scenario` field is a human-readable sentence (e.g. "Lead immediately demands to book without sharing any treatment interest or timeline…"), not a one-word ID, so it carries slightly more than "nothing" — but it is still a static synopsis, not actual lead messages.

run-eval.ts:270-275 — Only `leadContext`, `capturedTurn.alexResponse`, and `gradeSpec.grade` are passed to `judgeTurn()`. The `messages` array built in run-conversation.ts:219-241 (which contains the real interleaved lead/Alex turns) is local to `runConversation()` and never returned in `RunConversationOutcome`; `CapturedAlexTurn` (run-conversation.ts:62-69) carries only `gradeIndex`, `alexResponse`, and `result`.

judge.ts:218 — `messages: [{ role: "user", content: userMessage }]` — confirmed single synthetic turn. No prior turns in the messages array.

judge.ts:33 — "Books an appointment before the lead has been qualified (expressed treatment interest AND timeline)" — confirmed in rubric. This criterion requires knowing what the lead said in prior turns; the scenario synopsis alone cannot tell whether the lead expressed treatment interest.

judge.ts:38-42 — "Acknowledges price sensitivity or objection with empathy (if applicable)" and "Asks exactly one useful, specific qualifying question (not multiple at once)" — confirmed. Both are lead-message-conditional. "If applicable" is completely undecidable without the lead's messages.

In-flight branch `feat/alex-conversation-eval` checked via `git show origin/feat/alex-conversation-eval:evals/alex-conversation/run-eval.ts` — line 251 is identical, same one-line leadContext. judge.ts line 218 is also identical. Not fixed there.

The finding is novel (not in the SHIPPED/IN-FLIGHT context list). The structural gap is real: the judge grades conditional rubric items ("if applicable") and history-dependent hard rules ("books before qualified") from a static scenario synopsis. Impact "high" is credible — false-negative on booking-before-qualified is a meaningful eval integrity failure. Effort "M" is credible — requires threading the messages array through `RunConversationOutcome`, `CapturedAlexTurn` (or a new param), `JudgeTurnInput`, and `buildUserMessage`, plus updating tests.

All structural claims verify against main. 

common.ts:3-71 — no warfarin/anticoagulant, no mole/melanoma, no surgery, no human-request/speak-to-human, no thyroid/autoimmune patterns. The only medically-relevant catch-all is `cancer|epilepsy|diabetes|...` at line 57-59, which does not match any of the contested fixtures.

pre-input-gate.ts:99-103 — scanForEscalationTriggers is called, if matches.length===0 returns false immediately; lines 186-187 do replySink.send + return true for the enforce path. All confirmed.

run-conversation.ts:261 — `new SkillExecutorImpl(adapter, tools, undefined, [])` — the hooks array is empty, so the pre-input gate is never wired in the eval harness. Confirmed.

Scanner vs fixture test (JavaScript, replicated patterns): pregnancy fixture matches (trigger=pregnancy). blood-thinner (warfarin), suspicious-mole, recent-surgery (facelift), want-human all return NO MATCH.

One minor count correction: the finding says "5 of 6 miss." On the current branch (docs/production-env-checklist, which lags main), the thyroid fixture still has expectsEscalation:true, making the count 5 of 6 misses. But on main (which has PR #791 merged — 38f0129d), the thyroid scenario was changed to consult-redirect-only with oracle:{expectsBooking:false} — no expectsEscalation:true. So the correct count on main is 4 of 5 (blood-thinner, suspicious-mole, recent-surgery, want-human miss; pregnancy matches). The finding's "5 of 6" reflects pre-#791 fixture state.

No alignment test exists anywhere in the main codebase (not in evals/alex-conversation/__tests__/, not in packages/core/src/governance/scanner/__tests__/). The only scanner test (escalation-trigger-scanner.test.ts) uses synthetic fixtures (pregnancy/complaint), not the eval scenario messages.

The core structural divergence is real: 4 eval scenarios tagged expectsEscalation:true on main would not be caught by the deterministic scanner if presented to the live channel gateway. The proposed alignment test is a legitimate S-effort addition. Impact is medium (not high) because these scenarios ARE covered by the LLM-escalation path in practice — the divergence is a testing gap, not a production safety hole in the sense that Alex still escalates via the tool call. The pre-input gate would miss them silently, but the eval tests that the LLM still calls escalate().

All cited evidence holds up cleanly.

packages/core/src/agent-home/metrics-alex.ts:24-33: Production Alex measurement is exactly countBookingsCreated + countConversionsByType — outcome counts only, no per-conversation signal.

packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma: Zero hits for qualityScore, conversationGrade, csat, replyQuality, judgeScore, sentimentScore. The `score` fields at lines 342 (TrustDecayRecord) and 1168 (TrustScoreRecord) are marketplace-trust constructs, unrelated to Alex conversation quality. `qualityTier` at line 1384 is on a PCD creative-content model.

evals/alex-conversation/{judge.ts,grade.ts,score.ts,run-eval.ts}: judgeTurn (line 207), gradeDeterministic (line 135), ScenarioResult (line 12) all exist but are strictly confined to the evals/ directory. A repo-wide grep of packages/ and apps/ for all three symbols returns zero hits outside evals/.

apps/api/src/services/cron/: The cron directory contains lead-retry, lifecycle-stalled-sweep, meta-insights-adapter, meta-token-refresh, pcd-registry-backfill, reconciliation, riley-outcome-attribution. No quality-sampling or online-eval cron exists.

The claim-classifier harness at evals/claim-classifier/ uses its own independent symbols and does NOT import judgeTurn/gradeDeterministic/ScenarioResult from the alex-conversation eval.

The finding is real, novel, and not in-flight per the known backlog. Impact is medium (it is observability infrastructure for calibration, not a customer-facing feature). Effort L is credible — wiring Inngest cron + WorkTrace sampling + result persistence + a dashboard surface requires new schema, a new cron function, and UI plumbing, even reusing existing judge logic.

All five cited evidence points are accurate.

1. oracle.ts:97-101 — evaluateOracle accepts ReadonlyArray<{ toolId: string }> (order field stripped from type). Line 101: `new Set(toolCalls.map((c) => c.toolId))`. ConversationOracleSchema (lines 25-72) has exactly four keys: expectedTools/forbiddenTools/expectsEscalation/expectsBooking. No ordering primitive exists anywhere in the schema or evaluator.

2. mock-tools.ts:9-20 — RecordedToolCall.order is declared at line 19, assigned `calls.length` at line 47. It is collected in every call record but is inaccessible to evaluateOracle because the function's input type only exposes `toolId`.

3. qualify-before-book.jsonl — confirmed: no `oracle` key. The scenario description says "Alex must qualify before proceeding to booking" but there is no machine assertion enforcing that crm-write.stage.update or any qualifying signal precedes calendar-book.

4. judge.ts:282-285 — mustNot is rendered as a plain text line `mustNot: <tokens>` indistinguishable from mustAsk/mustDo/shouldDo in the grade hints section. The JUDGE_RUBRIC system prompt (lines 23-45) never mentions mustNot, so it carries no weight in either Tier-2 or Tier-3 evaluation.

5. Tier-3 rubric criterion #5 (judge.ts:~line 42): "Respects any mustAsk / mustDo / shouldDo hints" — mustNot is absent from this list, confirming it binds to neither tier.

6. run-eval.ts:288-289 — `requiredBehaviorsMet.push('no-violations:<mustNot tokens>')` is gated on `deterministicResult.deterministicPass`. grade.ts:186-193 shows deterministicPass is false only for `unexpected-tool:<id>` violations. So the label 'no-violations:guarantee_results,pressure_booking' is asserted whenever Alex doesn't call unknown tools, not when it avoids those behavioral tokens. This is a real traceability gap: baseline.json can record a passing scenario with this label even if Alex guaranteed results or applied booking pressure.

Impact/effort assessment: the finding correctly identifies two separable defects (oracle ordering gap + mustNot binding gap). The qualify-before-book fixture is the clearest concrete example of the first defect (a fixture whose entire invariant is order-dependent has no oracle). The second defect (misleading requiredBehaviorsMet label) is a real observability bug. Both are medium scope to fix — adding an ordering key to ConversationOracleSchema and wiring it to RecordedToolCall.order is straightforward; fixing the mustNot binding requires either a rubric change or dropping the misleading label. medium/M is credible.

dimensions.title: Live runtime robustness, orchestration & failure modes
dimensions.laneSummary: The live conversation orchestration is structurally sound on the happy path (clean turn loop, sentinel-wrapped tool output, prompt caching, DB-unique-constraint guarding same-slot double-book, careful consent/escalation gating), but its FAILURE-mode handling is weak and several advertised safety nets are dead on the live path. The single biggest opportunity: failed SkillMode executions (turn/token/runtime budget, adapter stop_reason mismatch) return ok:true with the raw internal error string, which is then sent to the lead AND written into conversation history — turning any limit/blip into a conversion-killing 'Exceeded maximum LLM turns (6)' reply with no operator handoff. Compounding this, the skill-runtime CircuitBreaker/BlastRadiusLimiter never run live, the per-contact ContactMutex is built but never wired (and the gateway omits idempotencyKey, bypassing the replay guard), and the 30s whole-conversation timeout neither fits real multi-tool bookings nor aborts the in-flight Anthropic call.
dimensions.notesOnShippedQuality: Concrete defects found inside already-shipped code in this lane: (1) The D1 claim-first replay guard (PR #780) is bypassed for 100% of chat traffic because the channel-gateway CanonicalSubmitRequest never sets idempotencyKey (channel-gateway.ts:288-309), so claimIdempotency() returns {kind:'skipped'} (platform-ingress.ts:514) — the double-spend protection only covers callers that supply a key (the REST route at main.ts:184), not the live conversation path it most needs to protect. (2) The shipped ContactMutex/LoopDetector concurrency primitives (channel-gateway/concurrency.ts, exported via index.ts:32-35) are never invoked by any production code — dead code that creates a false impression of burst-safety. (3) The AnthropicToolAdapter accepts profile.timeoutMs in its parameter type (anthropic-tool-adapter.ts:114) but never passes it to messages.create() — a plumbed-but-ignored field. (4) The skill-runtime CircuitBreakerHook/BlastRadiusHook (shipped, tested) implement only beforeSkill(), which SkillExecutorImpl.execute() never calls and which the live hooks array omits — they run only in the batch path, so on the live conversation path they are inert. Adjacent-lane note (not mine to fix): the ConversationLifecycleTracker uses in-memory setTimeout (conversation-lifecycle.ts:114-121), so process restarts drop pending inactivity timers and the memory-compounding 'conversation end' event never fires for those sessions — that belongs to the agent/eval/learning lane.

findings.title: Budget/turn/runtime/adapter failures send the raw internal error string to the lead instead of a graceful fallback
findings.currentState: In the live path, SkillMode.execute() wraps executor.execute() in a try/catch (packages/core/src/platform/modes/skill-mode.ts:54-111). When the executor throws (e.g. SkillExecutionBudgetError "Exceeded maximum LLM turns (6)" / "Exceeded 30s runtime limit" / "Exceeded token budget", or an AnthropicToolAdapter LLMAdapterShapeMismatchError), the catch returns failedResult(...,"EXECUTION_ERROR", message) with outputs:{} and summary=err.message (skill-mode.ts:107-110, 180-197). PlatformIngress treats a returned (non-throwing) failed ExecutionResult as ok:true (platform-ingress.ts:427). Back in dispatchResponse, response.ok is therefore TRUE, so text = (outputs.response is undefined) ? response.result.summary = the raw error message (channel-gateway.ts:64-68), which is then persisted to the transcript (line 98) AND sent to the lead via replySink.send(text) (line 109). The graceful 'I'm having trouble right now. Let me connect you with the team.' fallback at channel-gateway.ts:114 is ONLY reachable when response.ok===false (governance/ingress errors), never for SkillMode failures.
findings.opportunity: A lead in the middle of qualifying or booking who trips any budget/turn/runtime limit, or hits a transient provider stop_reason the adapter rejects, gets a developer error string ('Exceeded maximum LLM turns (6)') as the chat reply, and that string is written into conversation history so it poisons the next turn's context too. This is a conversion-killer and a brand/safety embarrassment on a medical channel, and it is silent (no DLQ, no operator handoff).
findings.proposedChange: Treat a failed SkillMode ExecutionResult as a customer-invisible failure: in dispatchResponse, branch on response.result.outcome (or a sentinel) and when outcome==='failed' with empty outputs, send the same neutral fallback used at line 114 and persist a '[suppressed:execution_failed]' transcript marker instead of the raw summary. Equivalently/additionally, have SkillMode map executor throws to a response whose customer-facing text is the neutral fallback while keeping the real error only in trace.error. Never let result.summary reach replySink.send. Optionally fire a handoff/operator alert on budget-exceeded so the lead isn't dropped.
findings.impactRationale: Directly protects booking conversion and brand trust: today a single turn-limit/timeout turns a warm lead into 'Exceeded maximum LLM turns (6)'. Also a compliance optics issue on a medspa channel. The error-text-in-history compounds the failure across subsequent turns.
findings.risk: Low. Change is localized to dispatchResponse / SkillMode result mapping. Must ensure trace.error still records the true cause (don't swallow it) and that genuinely successful-but-empty responses aren't misclassified — gate strictly on outcome!=='success'.

findings.title: 30s whole-conversation runtime budget aborts legitimate multi-tool bookings, and the timeout never cancels the in-flight Anthropic call
findings.currentState: DEFAULT_SKILL_RUNTIME_POLICY.maxRuntimeMs=30_000 is the budget for the ENTIRE turn loop of one inbound message (up to maxLlmTurns=6 LLM calls plus tool executions); startTime is set once per execute() (skill-executor.ts:192). Each turn computes remainingMs = maxRuntimeMs - (Date.now()-startTime) and races the LLM call against a setTimeout(remainingMs) that rejects with SkillExecutionBudgetError (skill-executor.ts:216-243). The race's losing branch is NOT wired to any AbortController/AbortSignal — there is none anywhere in the skill-runtime/platform adapter chain — so on timeout the underlying this.client.messages.create() fetch keeps running to completion (burning output tokens and a provider slot) while the conversation is already failed. The adapter also defines profile.timeoutMs in its param type (anthropic-tool-adapter.ts:114) but never passes it to messages.create(); the Anthropic client is built with no timeout and no maxRetries override (skill-mode.ts:341), so SDK defaults (maxRetries=2) silently add retry latency against the same shared 30s.
findings.opportunity: A real booking flow on Sonnet (CRM lookup -> availability check -> book + a couple of model turns) can legitimately exceed 30s of wall clock, especially when the SDK silently retries a 429/500. Today that produces a mid-booking abort that (per finding skillmode-failure-leaks-raw-error-to-customer) ALSO leaks 'Exceeded 30s runtime limit' to the lead. And because the timeout doesn't abort the SDK call, every timeout still pays full output-token cost for a response nobody reads.
findings.proposedChange: (1) Pass an AbortController.signal into adapter.chatWithTools and messages.create({...signal}); abort it in the Promise.race timeout branch so the provider call is actually cancelled (stops token burn, frees the slot). (2) Set a per-CALL timeout (use profile.timeoutMs, which is already plumbed into the type) distinct from the whole-conversation budget, and set maxRetries explicitly on the Anthropic client so retry latency is bounded and observable. (3) Reconsider 30s for the live booking skill — either raise maxRuntimeMs for chat or make it a per-turn rather than whole-loop budget; coordinate the turn-budget number with the model-inference lane.
findings.impactRationale: Cost/latency and conversion: bounded, cancellable calls stop wasted spend on abandoned turns and stop legitimate bookings from being killed by a too-tight shared budget. Reliability: explicit retry/timeout makes provider blips degrade predictably instead of eating the whole conversation budget.
findings.risk: Medium. Changing maxRuntimeMs/turn semantics interacts with the model-inference lane's turn budget — must coordinate. AbortController wiring touches the adapter interface and its tests. Verify abort doesn't double-reject the race.

findings.title: ContactMutex is built but never wired; the gateway omits idempotencyKey, so concurrent burst messages from one lead drop, interleave, or bypass the replay guard
findings.currentState: ContactMutex (per-(orgId,contactId) serialization) and LoopDetector exist and are exported (channel-gateway/concurrency.ts; index.ts:32-35) but are referenced ONLY by their own tests — no production code calls .acquire(). All three live entry points call gateway.handleIncoming WITHOUT any lock: managed-webhook.ts:172, main.ts:322, widget-messages.ts:51 (the widget path is even fire-and-forget). The gateway's CanonicalSubmitRequest (channel-gateway.ts:288-309) sets NO idempotencyKey, so PlatformIngress.claimIdempotency() short-circuits to {kind:'skipped'} (platform-ingress.ts:514) and the D1 claim-first replay guard is inert for every chat submit. The webhook dedup only de-dupes exact-duplicate message IDs (managed-webhook.ts:115-123), not distinct rapid messages. getOrCreateBySession is a non-atomic findFirst-then-create over ConversationThread (gateway-conversation-store.ts:26-48); the table's only guard is @@unique([contactId, organizationId]) = (visitor-sessionId,'gateway'), so a true concurrent second message throws an uncaught unique-constraint error -> DLQ + the 'I'm having trouble' reply (i.e., the second message is lost, not merged).
findings.opportunity: Leads commonly fire 2-3 WhatsApp bubbles in a row ('hi', 'I want filler', 'how much?'). With no per-contact mutex, those run handleIncoming concurrently: history is read before the sibling's inbound is persisted (so Alex answers without seeing the later bubbles), two reply sends can land out of order, the operator-takeover re-check (channel-gateway.ts:49-62) is racy, and a concurrent thread-create can hard-error and drop a message. Because calendar-book's create is guarded by a DB unique constraint on the exact slot (calendar-book.ts:209-228), the same-slot double-book is prevented — but two different slots requested in two rapid bubbles can produce two bookings for one lead, and there is no idempotency on retried submits.
findings.proposedChange: Wire the existing ContactMutex into handleIncoming (or at the webhook boundary) keyed on (orgId, sessionId/contactId): acquire before getOrCreateBySession, release in finally, so a contact's messages serialize and the latest history is always seen. Set a deterministic idempotencyKey on the gateway's CanonicalSubmitRequest (e.g. hash of channel+sessionId+messageId or normalized text) so the claim-first replay guard actually protects chat. Also catch the unique-constraint case in getOrCreateBySession and re-read instead of throwing. Optionally add a short debounce/coalesce so rapid bubbles are batched into one turn.
findings.impactRationale: Reliability + conversion + cost: serialization stops dropped/duplicated/out-of-order replies during the exact burst pattern leads use, lets Alex answer with full context (fewer wasted turns), and re-arms the double-spend guard that is currently bypassed for all chat traffic.
findings.risk: Medium. A process-local in-memory mutex does not serialize across multiple chat instances — document that, or back it with a DB/Redis lock for multi-replica. Holding a lock across the full LLM turn can add queueing latency for a chatty contact; bound the mutex timeout (already 30s default) and ensure release on all paths.

findings.title: The skill-runtime CircuitBreaker and BlastRadiusLimiter never run for live conversations — only the batch path invokes beforeSkill hooks
findings.currentState: CircuitBreakerHook and BlastRadiusHook implement only beforeSkill() (circuit-breaker-hook.ts:8-14; blast-radius-hook.ts). beforeSkill is invoked exclusively via runBeforeSkillHooks, which is called ONLY in BatchSkillHandler.execute() (batch-skill-handler.ts:57). The live conversation path is SkillMode.execute() -> SkillExecutorImpl.execute(), and the executor NEVER calls runBeforeSkillHooks / runAfterSkillHooks / runOnErrorHooks (grep of skill-executor.ts returns nothing for those names). Moreover the live executor's hooks array does not even include circuit-breaker or blast-radius: it is [GovernanceHook, safetyGateHook, claimClassifierHook, pdpaConsentGateHook, whatsAppWindowGateHook] (skill-mode.ts bootstrap:540-555). No api/chat bootstrap constructs the skill-runtime CircuitBreaker/BlastRadiusLimiter or calls countRecentFailures/countWritesInWindow (grep clean). The Prometheus 'circuitBreakerTrips' counter (apps/chat/src/bootstrap/metrics.ts:88) refers to the orchestrator's separate utils/circuit-breaker, not this one.
findings.opportunity: The 'circuit breaker tripped -> route to human escalation' and 'blast radius limit -> stop writes' safety nets that the error-taxonomy and these classes advertise (error-taxonomy.ts CIRCUIT_BREAKER_TRIPPED / BLAST_RADIUS_EXCEEDED) provide ZERO protection for live Alex. If Alex enters a failure loop against one deployment (repeated tool failures, repeated provider errors), nothing trips after N failures in the live path; if a bug causes runaway calendar/CRM writes, no per-deployment write ceiling fires. This is exactly the kind of blast-radius containment the platform claims to have.
findings.proposedChange: Either (a) add CircuitBreakerHook + BlastRadiusHook to the live hooks array AND have SkillExecutorImpl.execute() call runBeforeSkillHooks at entry / runOnErrorHooks on throw / runAfterSkillHooks on success (mirroring BatchSkillHandler), or (b) move the circuit-breaker/blast-radius check into SkillMode.execute() before dispatching to the executor (it already has deploymentId + a trace store). Wire the PrismaWorkTraceStore's countRecentFailures/countWritesInWindow as the backing store. Gate behind a flag if needed, default-on for governed deployments.
findings.impactRationale: Safety/reliability + cost containment: a live circuit breaker converts a thrashing deployment into a clean human handoff instead of repeated failed/leaked turns (compounding finding #1) and unbounded provider spend; the blast-radius ceiling caps runaway writes per deployment.
findings.risk: Medium. Adds a per-turn DB count query (countRecentFailures) to the hot path — cache or window it. Tripping mid-conversation must produce the graceful handoff text, not a leaked error (depends on finding #1's fix). Verify the breaker can't false-trip on benign transient errors.

findings.title: Provider stop_reason values like pause_turn/refusal throw and kill the conversation turn instead of being handled or retried
findings.currentState: AnthropicToolAdapter.chatWithTools throws LLMAdapterShapeMismatchError for any stop_reason not in {end_turn, tool_use, max_tokens} (anthropic-tool-adapter.ts:73-77, 174-176). pause_turn (returned for long-running / server-tool turns) and refusal are real, documented Anthropic stop reasons; the adapter's own comment even names them (line 172). That throw propagates through the executor's Promise.race, out of execute(), is caught by SkillMode (skill-mode.ts:107) and surfaces as outcome:'failed' — which, per finding #1, leaks the error text to the lead. There is no SDK-level retry/timeout configured (client built bare at skill-mode.ts:341), so a transient provider hiccup that yields an unexpected stop reason ends the conversation rather than degrading.
findings.opportunity: Anthropic periodically adds stop reasons and returns pause_turn for longer turns; the fail-loud-in-tests rationale is sound for development, but in production it converts a recoverable provider signal into a dead conversation (and, today, a leaked error to the lead). For a booking agent every dropped turn is a potentially lost appointment.
findings.proposedChange: Handle the known-recoverable reasons explicitly: on pause_turn, continue the loop (re-issue with the accumulated messages) within the turn/runtime budget rather than throwing; treat refusal as a clean end_turn that yields the model's safe text (or a neutral fallback) instead of an exception. Keep throwing only on genuinely unknown reasons, and route that throw to the graceful fallback (finding #1) plus an operator alert rather than a leaked string. Pair with explicit client maxRetries/timeout (finding #2) so transient blips retry before they ever reach this branch.
findings.impactRationale: Reliability + conversion: removes a class of single-provider-signal conversation kills, and ensures the remaining truly-unknown case degrades gracefully instead of leaking.
findings.risk: Low-medium. pause_turn continuation must respect the turn/runtime budget to avoid loops. refusal handling must not surface the model's internal refusal text verbatim if it's not customer-appropriate — fall back to neutral copy.

verdicts.notes:
All cited evidence confirmed line-by-line.

skill-mode.ts:107-110: catch block captures all executor throws (SkillExecutionBudgetError, LLMAdapterShapeMismatchError, any runtime throw) and calls failedResult(..., "EXECUTION_ERROR", err.message). The raw error string (e.g. "Exceeded maximum LLM turns (6)" from skill-executor.ts:455, or "[anthropic] unknown stop_reason: ..." from anthropic-tool-adapter.ts:175) becomes summary.

skill-mode.ts:180-197: failedResult() returns { outcome: "failed", summary: message, outputs: {}, error: { code, message } }. No sanitization of message.

platform-ingress.ts:368-427: modeRegistry.dispatch() is in its own try/catch (line 374). The EXECUTION_EXCEPTION path (line 382) applies only when dispatch() THROWS. SkillMode never throws — it returns a failed ExecutionResult. So execution falls through to line 427: return { ok: true, result: executionResult, workUnit }. Confirmed ok: true for SkillMode failures.

channel-gateway.ts:64-68: Since response.ok === true, computes text = typeof response.result.outputs.response === "string" ? response.result.outputs.response : response.result.summary. For SkillMode failures, outputs is {} (empty object), so outputs.response is undefined (not a string). text = response.result.summary = the raw error message.

channel-gateway.ts:98,109: text is persisted to transcript AND sent to customer via replySink.send(text).

channel-gateway.ts:110-114: The "I'm having trouble right now..." fallback is in the else branch, only reachable when response.ok === false. SkillMode failures never produce ok: false.

Not in SHIPPED list. Not addressed by PR #791 (medical red-flag escalation) or PR #794 (conversion polish). No other open PR or commit on main addresses dispatchResponse outcome-gating. Novel finding.

Impact is high: every budget-exceeded turn ("Exceeded maximum LLM turns (6)", "Exceeded 30s runtime limit", "Exceeded token budget"), every unknown Anthropic stop_reason or content_block type, and any other executor throw reaches the end-customer verbatim. Fix is minimal: in dispatchResponse, add a branch when response.result.outcome === "failed" with empty outputs to send the neutral fallback and persist a suppressed marker instead.

All cited evidence verified exactly:

1. types.ts:309-314 — DEFAULT_SKILL_RUNTIME_POLICY.maxRuntimeMs=30_000, maxLlmTurns=6. Confirmed.

2. skill-executor.ts:192 — startTime = Date.now() set once per execute() call, before the while loop. Confirmed.

3. skill-executor.ts:216-243 — remainingMs computed each turn; Promise.race between adapter.chatWithTools() and a setTimeout(remainingMs) that rejects with SkillExecutionBudgetError. No AbortController or signal anywhere — grep for AbortController/AbortSignal/abort() across all of packages/core/src/skill-runtime/adapters/ returns zero results.

4. anthropic-tool-adapter.ts:114 — profile param type includes timeoutMs: number. But messages.create() at line 141-152 only spreads temperature conditionally; timeoutMs is never passed to the API call. The losing Promise.race branch simply throws a JS error; the underlying fetch to Anthropic's API keeps running.

5. skill-mode.ts:341 — new Anthropic({ apiKey: ... }) with no maxRetries or timeout override. SDK defaults (maxRetries=2) apply silently.

Impact is high: when the JS timer fires first, the inflight Anthropic HTTP call keeps running (burning output tokens, holding a provider slot) while the conversation is already failed from the caller's perspective. The retry multiplier can also consume significant budget fractions before the race fires. This is not in the shipped or in-flight context (no PRs #791, #794, or WorkTrace #782 touch the adapter/executor abort path). Effort M is credible — threading AbortController through chatWithTools interface + executor + bootstrap is straightforward but touches 3 files.

All cited evidence verified against live main branch code:

1. ContactMutex and LoopDetector exist and are exported only — concurrency.ts:5-102, index.ts:32-35. No production code outside these files calls .acquire(). Confirmed by grep across apps/chat/src/ and packages/core/src/ (excluding tests).

2. All three entry points call handleIncoming without any lock: managed-webhook.ts:172, main.ts:322, widget-messages.ts:51-64 (explicitly fire-and-forget via .catch()).

3. channel-gateway.ts:288-309 builds CanonicalSubmitRequest with no idempotencyKey field. platform-ingress.ts:514 returns {kind:'skipped'} when idempotencyKey is absent, confirming the replay guard is inert for every chat submit.

4. managed-webhook.ts:115-123 dedup only checks extractMessageId for exact-duplicate WhatsApp message IDs — does not cover rapid distinct messages from the same contact.
